> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Compare ellipsoidal and torispherical heads"*, immediately draw this comparison in your answer script. The **ellipsoidal head has a smoother profile** (no abrupt curvature change at the knuckle), which gives it a **lower stress concentration factor (SCF тЙИ 2.0)** than a standard torispherical head (SCF тЙИ 2.5 to 3.5). This is why 2:1 ellipsoidal heads are preferred for **cyclic / fatigue-loaded** service.

## 4.4 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head тАФ Cross-Sectional Geometry (Schematic Description)

Since a true 2-D drawing is not possible in Mermaid, the geometry is described in text form below for sketching during the exam:

```
                  ___________
                 /           \        тЖР CROWN (apex, R_c = D)
                /             \        Maximum membrane stress
               /               \       at this location
              /                 \
             /                   \    тЖР KNUCKLE region
            /          0.17D       \   Optional radius r = 0.17D
            \                     /
             \                   /    тЖР STRAIGHT FLANGE (skirt)
              \                 /       Length 1.5t to 3t
               \               /        (weld preparation)
              __\_____________/__
            |   |             |   |    тЖР CYLINDRICAL SHELL
            |___|             |___|       Wall thickness = t
            D = Inside Diameter
```

The depth from the crown to the tangent line of the cylindrical shell (excluding skirt) is $b = D/4 = 500$ mm for a 2000 mm diameter vessel. With skirt, the total head depth is $D/4 + h_{skirt}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an Elliptical Head. Why is the 2:1 ratio considered the standard proportion for pressure vessel heads?**

**Model Answer:**

An elliptical head is a pressure vessel closure formed by revolving a semi-ellipse about its major axis. In the standard 2:1 proportion, the major axis equals the inside diameter $D$ of the vessel, and the minor axis equals $D/2$, so the depth of the head is $D/4$. The 2:1 ratio is standard because:

(i) It is the **deepest ellipsoidal head** that can be **cold-formed in a single press operation** from a flat circular blank, making it the most economical to manufacture.

(ii) Its **crown radius $R_c = D$** gives it near-spherical strength with significantly less material than a hemisphere.

(iii) The smooth ellipsoidal profile has a **low stress concentration factor** (тЙИ 2.0) compared to torispherical heads (тЙИ 2.5 to 3.5), making it preferable for **fatigue and cyclic loading**.

(iv) The head depth of $D/4$ is shallow enough to be practical for transport and installation but deep enough to resist buckling and external pressure.

**Keywords for full marks:** *2:1 ratio, crown radius $R_c = D$, single-press forming, low SCF, ASME UG-32(d).*

---

### **Question 2** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the ASME Section VIII Div. 1 thickness formula for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head. List any four design parameters and their units.**

**Model Answer:**

The required thickness of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head as per ASME Section VIII Div. 1, UG-32(d) is given by:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

The four key design parameters and their SI units are:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $P$ | Internal design pressure | **MPa** (N/mm┬▓) |
| $D$ | Inside diameter of head | **mm** |
| $S$ | Allowable stress of material at design temperature | **MPa** (N/mm┬▓) |
| $E$ | Joint efficiency (0.70 to 1.00) | **dimensionless** |

The formula assumes the head is integral with the cylindrical shell (or a butt-welded joint with $E = 1.0$ after full radiographic examination), and includes the 0.2P pressure-correction term required for the inner-fibre stress (Lam├й solution calibration).

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout тАФ Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $0.2P$ term** in the denominator тАФ *worth 2 marks lost per question*.
> 2. **Confusing $0.2P$ (in thickness) with $0.4P$ (in MAWP)** тАФ *worth 1 mark*.
> 3. **Not adding the corrosion allowance** to the required thickness тАФ *worth 1 mark*.
> 4. **Using $R_c = D/2$ (mistake for hemisphere) instead of $R_c = D$** тАФ *worth 2 marks*.
> 5. **Forgetting the joint efficiency $E$** when Category B or C welds are present.
> 6. **Failing to round up** to the next standard plate thickness.
> 7. **Not writing the units** in the final answer тАФ *strict 1-mark penalty per KTU norms*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the ASME Section VIII Div. 1 thickness formula $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, starting from the membrane equilibrium of a surface of revolution. State clearly the geometric assumption about the crown radius. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel has an inside diameter of $D = 1800$ mm and is closed at one end by a 2:1 ellipsoidal head. The vessel is designed for an internal pressure of $P = 1.6$ MPa at a design temperature of 250 ┬░C. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with allowable stress $S = 124$ MPa at 250 ┬░C. The joint is a fully radiographed butt weld ($E = 1.0$). The corrosion allowance is $CA = 2$ mm. Determine:

- (i) The required head thickness.
- (ii) The nominal thickness after adding corrosion allowance.
- (iii) The standard thickness to be ordered.
- (iv) The Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP) for the chosen thickness.
- (v) The internal volume and approximate weight of the head (take density $\rho_m = 7850$ kg/m┬│, blank area factor 1.17).

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution тАФ Question A**

#### Part (a) тАФ Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 тАФ Equilibrium of a Shell of Revolution (2 marks):**

For an axisymmetric thin shell under internal pressure $P$, the membrane equilibrium equation in the meridional and circumferential directions is:

$$\frac{N_\phi}{R_\phi} + \frac{N_\theta}{R_\theta} = P$$

where $N_\phi$ = meridional force per unit length, $N_\theta$ = circumferential (hoop) force per unit length, $R_\phi$ = meridional radius of curvature, $R_\theta$ = circumferential radius of curvature.

**Step 2 тАФ Geometry of the 2:1 Ellipsoid (2 marks):**

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the major semi-axis $a = D/2$ and the minor semi-axis $b = D/4$. The crown radius of curvature at the apex ($x = 0$ of the ellipse profile) is:

$$R_c = \frac{a^2}{b} = \frac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = D$$

At the apex, by symmetry, $R_\phi = R_\theta = R_c = D$.

**Step 3 тАФ Stress at the Crown (2 marks):**

Substituting $R_\phi = R_\theta = D$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$\frac{N_\phi + N_\theta}{D} = P \quad\Rightarrow\quad N_\phi + N_\theta = P D$$

By symmetry at the apex, $N_\phi = N_\theta = N$, so $2N = PD$ and the membrane stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{N}{t} = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Apply Allowable Stress and Pressure Correction (1 mark):**

Setting $\sigma \le S E$ and applying the ASME pressure-correction term (from the Lam├й solution) for inner-fibre stress gives:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

This completes the derivation.

#### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 1800$ mm, $P = 1.6$ MPa, $S = 124$ MPa, $E = 1.0$, $CA = 2$ mm.

**(i) Required thickness (1 mark):**

$$t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} = \frac{(1.6)(1800)}{2(124)(1.0) - 0.2(1.6)} = \frac{2880}{248 - 0.32} = \frac{2880}{247.68}$$

$$t_{req} = 11.629 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Nominal thickness (1 mark):**

$$t_{nom} = t_{req} + CA = 11.629 + 2.0 = 13.629 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Standard thickness (1 mark):**

Next standard size above 13.629 mm in IS 2062 plate schedule is $t_{std} = 16$ mm. **Order 16 mm plate.**

**(iv) MAWP for 16 mm head (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 \, t_{std} \, S \, E}{D + 0.4 \, t_{std}} = \frac{2(16)(124)(1.0)}{1800 + 0.4(16)} = \frac{3968}{1806.4}$$

$$P_{max} = 2.197 \text{ MPa} > 1.6 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Safe)}$$

**(v) Volume and Weight (2 marks):**

Volume of 2:1 ellipsoidal head (ideal, no skirt):

$$V = \frac{\pi D^3}{24} = \frac{\pi (1800)^3}{24} = \frac{\pi \times 5.832 \times 10^9}{24} = 7.634 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.7634 \text{ m}^3$$

Blank area (with 17% trim factor):

$$A_{blank} = 1.17 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 1.17 \times \frac{\pi (1.8)^2}{4} = 1.17 \times 2.545 = 2.977 \text{ m}^2$$

Weight of formed head:

$$W = \rho_m \times t_{std} \times A_{blank} = 7850 \times 0.016 \times 2.977 = 373.8 \text{ kg}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks тАФ Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare 2:1 ellipsoidal heads and standard torispherical heads on the basis of (i) profile geometry, (ii) crown radius, (iii) thickness formula, (iv) depth, and (v) stress concentration factor. State two specific applications where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel of $D = 1500$ mm is fitted with two 2:1 ellipsoidal heads. Design pressure is $P = 0.8$ MPa. The material is SA-240 Grade 304 stainless steel with allowable stress $S = 115$ MPa at the design temperature of 200 ┬░C. The head-to-shell weld is a Category B butt joint with $E = 0.85$. Corrosion allowance is $CA = 0$ (stainless steel). Calculate:

- (i) Required thickness of each head.
- (ii) Standard plate thickness.
- (iii) MAWP for the standard plate chosen.
- (iv) Total internal volume of the two heads combined.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution тАФ Question B**

#### Part (a) тАФ Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| # | Parameter | 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head | Torispherical Head |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Profile** | Smooth half-ellipse (single curve) | Crown (spherical) + knuckle (torus) + skirt |
| 2 | **Crown Radius $R_c$** | $D$ (from ellipse geometry) | $L = D$ (typical), but *not* derived from $D$ |
| 3 | **Knuckle Radius $r$** | Optional, typically 0.17 D | Mandatory 0.17 D (standard) |
| 4 | **Depth** | $D/4$ = 0.250 $D$ | тЙИ 0.19 $D$ (shallower) |
| 5 | **Thickness Formula** | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | $t = \dfrac{0.885 P L}{S E - 0.1 P}$ |
| 6 | **Stress Concentration Factor** | тЙИ 2.0 (low) | тЙИ 2.5 to 3.5 (high) |
| 7 | **Manufacturing** | Single cold-press | Crown press + knuckle roll |
| 8 | **Cost** | Lower for $D \le 3000$ mm | Higher due to double forming |
| 9 | **Preferred Applications** | Cyclic / fatigue service; refineries, cryogenic tanks | Lower-pressure tanks, atmospheric storage |
| 10 | **Code Reference** | ASME UG-32 (d) | ASME UG-32 (e) |

> **Preferred applications:**
> - **Ellipsoidal:** Refinery reactors, heat-exchanger bonnets, cryogenic vessels (LNG), pharmaceutical reactors тАФ *anywhere* the head undergoes thermal cycling or pressure cycling.
> - **Torispherical:** Atmospheric and low-pressure storage tanks (water, oil, fuel) where cost dominates and cycles are minimal.

#### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 1500$ mm, $P = 0.8$ MPa, $S = 115$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 0$.

**(i) Required thickness (2 marks):**

$$t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} = \frac{(0.8)(1500)}{2(115)(0.85) - 0.2(0.8)} = \frac{1200}{195.5 - 0.16} = \frac{1200}{195.34}$$

$$t_{req} = 6.143 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Standard thickness (1 mark):**

Next standard size above 6.143 mm in SA-240 plate schedule is $t_{std} = 8$ mm. **Order 8 mm plate.**

**(iii) MAWP (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 t_{std} S E}{D + 0.4 t_{std}} = \frac{2(8)(115)(0.85)}{1500 + 0.4(8)} = \frac{1564}{1503.2} = 1.040 \text{ MPa}$$

$P_{max} = 1.040$ MPa $> 0.8$ MPa $\checkmark$ **Safe** with 30% margin.

**(iv) Total volume of two heads (2 marks):**

Volume of one head:

$$V_1 = \frac{\pi D^3}{24} = \frac{\pi (1500)^3}{24} = \frac{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^9}{24} = 4.418 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^3$$

Total volume of two heads:

$$V_{total} = 2 \times 0.4418 = 0.8836 \text{ m}^3 = 883.6 \text{ litres}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 тАФ Elliptical Head: 10-Point Rapid Revision**

1. **2:1 ellipsoidal head** = standard ellipsoidal head with major axis = $D$ and minor axis = $D/2$. Depth = $D/4$.

2. **Crown radius $R_c = D$** (NOT $D/2$ тАФ that's a hemisphere). Memorise: $R_c = a^2/b = D$.

3. **ASME UG-32(d) thickness formula:**

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} \quad \text{with the } 0.2P \text{ term MANDATORY.}$$

4. **MAWP formula** uses $0.4P$ in the denominator:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 t S E}{D + 0.4 t}$$

5. **Always add corrosion allowance** $CA$ to the required thickness before rounding up to standard plate size.

6. **Round UP** to the next standard plate thickness тАФ never round down (it would be unsafe).

7. **Joint efficiency $E$** depends on weld category: $E = 1.0$ for full-radiography Category A butt welds; $E = 0.85$ for spot-radiography; $E = 0.70$ for Category C (no radiography).

8. **Volume formula** (no skirt): $V = \pi D^3 / 24$. For weight, use $W = \rho_m \cdot t \cdot A_{blank}$ with $A_{blank} \approx 1.17 \times \pi D^2/4$.

9. **Why 2:1 and not other ratios?** Because it is the deepest ellipsoid that can be cold-formed in a *single press operation*, minimising manufacturing cost while maximising structural efficiency.

10. **Comparison hierarchy (strongest to weakest):** Sphere > 2:1 Ellipsoid > Torispherical > Cone > Flat plate. Ellipsoidal heads carry about 2├Ч the pressure of a flat plate at the same thickness.

> **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"2:1, Rc = D, t = PD/(2SE тИТ 0.2P), never forget the 0.2, always add CA, always round up, always check MAWP."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Membrane Stress Equations. Discontinuity Stress Analysis in Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Membrane Stress Equations. Discontinuity Stress Analysis in Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section VIII** is the universally accepted code for the design, fabrication, inspection, and certification of unfired pressure vessels. It is administered by the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)** and is divided into three subdivisions:

- **Division 1 (Div-1):** Provides rules based on **prescriptive (rule-based) design** with higher safety factors. It covers vessels operating up to approximately **3000 psi (тЙИ 207 bar)**.
- **Division 2 (Div-2):** Provides **alternative rules** based on **analytical/design-by-analysis** methods with lower safety factors, applicable for higher pressures and temperatures.
- **Division 3 (Div-3):** Alternative rules for **high-pressure vessels** above 10,000 psi.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET862), **Section VIII Division 1 (mandatory)** and **Division 2 (design-by-analysis)** are the principal codes. Memorise the three-fold safety factor philosophy and the difference between **stress categories**.

### 1.2 Membrane Stress тАФ Definition

**Membrane stress** is the component of stress that acts **uniformly through the thickness** of a shell, equibiaxial in nature (averaged across the wall). It is the stress that would exist in an **idealised thin shell** of negligible flexural rigidity. The classification per **ASME Section VIII, Div 2, Part 5 (Design by Analysis)** is the foundation of all modern vessel evaluation.

### 1.3 Discontinuity Stress тАФ Definition

A **discontinuity** is a sudden change in the geometry, material, or loading of a shell (e.g., cylinder-to-cone junction, head-to-shell transition, nozzle insertion). Because the strain field is incompatible across such a junction, **secondary (self-equilibrating) bending stresses** arise near the junction. These are termed **discontinuity stresses** and are classified as **secondary stresses (Q)** in the ASME code.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy тАФ the straw in a glass of water:**
> - Imagine a thin **paper straw** (cylindrical shell) dipped in water. The internal pressure puffs it up uniformly тАФ the entire wall stretches тАФ this is **membrane stress**.
> - Now take a **rigid bottle cap** (torispherical head) and screw it on the straw. The cap does not stretch the same way as the straw. At the joint, the cap "bends" the straw slightly inward or outward тАФ this local bending is the **discontinuity stress**.
> - The **ASME code** is like the rule book a manufacturer consults to ensure neither the uniform puffing nor the local bending causes the straw (vessel) to fail.

Another analogy: **a balloon with a knot** тАФ the membrane stress in the balloon is the tension in the rubber; the knot creates a localized concentration (discontinuity).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Design pressure | P | MPa or psi |
| Inside radius | R | mm or in |
| Wall thickness | t | mm or in |
| Joint efficiency | E | 0.7 тАУ 1.0 (Div-1) |
| Safety factor on S_y | n_y | **3.5** (Div-1 membrane) |
| Safety factor on S_u | n_u | **3.0** (Div-1 membrane) |
| Poisson's ratio (steel) | ╬╜ | **0.3** |
| Modulus of elasticity (carbon steel) | E | **2.0 ├Ч 10тБ╡ MPa** |
| Code stress limit (Div-1) | S_m | min(S_y/3.5, S_u/3) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Flow of ASME Code Selection

The designer follows this decision hierarchy:

1. **Determine service conditions** тЖТ Design pressure (P), Design temperature (T), corrosion allowance (c).
2. **Select material** тЖТ Use ASME **Section II Part A (Ferrous)** & **Part B (Non-Ferrous)** to obtain S_y and S_u.
3. **Compute allowable stress (S_m)** тЖТ Per **Appendix A of Div-1** or **Table 5-1 of Div-2**.
4. **Calculate required thickness** тЖТ Use membrane stress formulas in **UG-27 (cylinders)**, **UG-32 (heads)**.
5. **Check stress categories** тЖТ Per **Div-2, Part 5 (5.2.1 тАУ 5.2.6)** тАФ limits on P_m, P_L, P_b, Q, and F.
6. **Evaluate discontinuities** тЖТ Nozzle reinforcement (Div-1 UG-37, Div-2 Part 6), or full elastic analysis (FEA) for Div-2.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Code Provision

- **Higher safety factor in Div-1 (3.5)** тЖТ Compensates for approximations in the simple membrane equations (no bending, no discontinuity, no thermal gradient).
- **Lower safety factor in Div-2 (1.5 on 2/3 S_y or S_u/3)** тЖТ Permitted because the design-by-analysis route explicitly accounts for all stress categories including secondary stresses.
- **Joint efficiency E** тЖТ Reflects radiographic examination quality (100% for full radiography тЖТ E = 1.0; spot radiography тЖТ E = 0.85; no radiography тЖТ E = 0.70).
- **Stress categorisation** тЖТ Different stress types have different failure potential; therefore, different allowable limits are mandated.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU university exam numerical problems. All standard membrane stress equations are collated.

| Component | Membrane Stress Equation (Thin-Wall) | Applicable Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical shell тАФ Hoop** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pR}{t} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Cylindrical shell тАФ Longitudinal** | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pR}{2t} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Spherical shell** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(d) |
| **Conical shell тАФ Hoop** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t\cos\alpha}$ | Div-1: UG-32(g) |
| **Conical shell тАФ Meridional** | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{pD}{4t\cos\alpha}$ | Div-1: UG-32(g) |
| **2:1 Ellipsoidal head (crown)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Div-1: UG-32(d) |
| **2:1 Ellipsoidal head (knuckle)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right)$ | m = 2; simplifies to pD/(4t) |
| **Torispherical head (crown)** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{2DR_i + 0.5L^2}}\right]$ | Div-1: UG-32(e) |
| **Required thickness (cylinder)** | $t = \dfrac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Required thickness (sphere)** | $t = \dfrac{pR}{2SE - 0.2p}$ | Div-1: UG-27(d) |
| **Allowable stress Div-1 (carbon/low-alloy steel)** | $S_m = \min\left(\dfrac{S_y}{3.5}, \dfrac{S_u}{3}\right)$ | Div-1 Appendix A |
| **Allowable stress Div-2 (membrane)** | $S_m = \min\left(\dfrac{2}{3}S_y, \dfrac{S_u}{3}\right)$ | Div-2: 4.3.11 |
| **Allowable stress Div-2 (membrane + bending)** | $S_{m+b} = \min\left(S_y, \dfrac{1.5 S_u}{3}\right) = 1.5 S_m$ | Div-2: 5.5.6 |
| **Decay constant (cylinder)** | $\beta = \left[\dfrac{3(1-\nu^2)}{R^2t^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | Lame' / Bijlaard |
| **Edge moment (cylinder-head, internal pressure)** | $M_0 = -pR^2\left[\beta\, \text{(geometric term)}\right]$ | Discontinuity theory |

> **Notation:** p = internal pressure, R = inside radius, D = inside diameter, t = required wall thickness, S = allowable stress, E = joint efficiency, ╬▒ = cone half-apex angle, L = crown radius of torisphere, R_i = knuckle radius.

## 2.4 Stress Categories тАФ Allowable Limits (Div-2, Part 5)

| Stress Type | Symbol | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| General primary membrane | $P_m$ | $\le S_m$ |
| Local primary membrane | $P_L$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Primary bending | $P_b$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Primary membrane + bending | $P_m + P_b$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Secondary (self-equilibrating) | $Q$ | $\le 3\, S_m$ |
| Peak | $F$ | $\le S_a$ (fatigue) тАФ $S_{a}$ from Fig. 5-110.1 |

**Why these limits?** Primary stresses do not redistribute with yielding тАФ they cause gross distortion (catastrophic). Secondary stresses self-equilibrate and are tolerated up to 3 S_m (yield redistribution permitted).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder (ASME Membrane Equation)

### Step 1 тАФ Free Body of a Cylinder Element

Consider a cylindrical shell of inside radius R, wall thickness t, subjected to internal pressure p. Slice a longitudinal section of unit length, subtending an angle 2╬╕ at the centre.

Forces acting on the element:
- **Pressure on projected area** = $p \cdot 2R\theta \cdot 1$ (per unit length)
- **Hoop stress resultant** = $\sigma_h \cdot t \cdot 1$ acting on two cut faces of length тЙИ 1 each
- **Hoop equilibrium:** The two hoop force components balance the projected pressure force.

### Step 2 тАФ Hoop (Circumferential) Stress Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \sigma_h t (1) \sin\theta &= p (2R\theta)(1) \\
\sigma_h t \cdot 2\theta &= p R \cdot 2\theta \quad \text{(for small } \theta, \sin\theta \approx \theta\text{)} \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{pR}{t} = \frac{pD}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The projected area on the diametrical plane is $2R\theta \times 1$, and the force component of $\sigma_h t$ in the vertical direction is $\sigma_h t \sin\theta$. Equating pressure force to membrane force yields the hoop stress.

### Step 3 тАФ Longitudinal (Axial) Stress Derivation

Cut the cylinder transversely. The end cap force on projected area is $p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$. This is balanced by the longitudinal stress resultant on the cylindrical wall area $\sigma_L \cdot t \cdot \pi D$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L \cdot t \cdot \pi D &= p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
\sigma_L &= \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{pR}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Equilibrium of axial forces on a cross-section gives longitudinal stress, which is exactly **half the hoop stress**.

### Step 4 тАФ Required Thickness (Div-1, UG-27)

The code provides a thickness equation that includes the **pressure term on the right-hand side** (a small correction for thick walls) and the **joint efficiency E**:

$$
t = \frac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}
$$

Derivation from $\sigma_h = pR/t$ with design margin:
- Design stress = SE (allowable stress times efficiency)
- $\sigma_h = pR/t \le SE \Rightarrow t \ge pR/SE$
- The $-0.6p$ term accounts for the **Hoop stress in a thick cylinder** (Lam├й's equation first-order correction).

### Step 5 тАФ Verification with Lam├й's Equation (Thick Cylinder)

For a thick cylinder (t/R > 0.1), Lam├й's equations give:
- Hoop stress at inside surface: $\sigma_i = p \dfrac{R_o^2 + R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$
- Hoop stress at outside surface: $\sigma_o = p \dfrac{2R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$

The **mean hoop stress** (which is what membrane stress represents) is:
$$
\sigma_{mean} = \frac{pR_i}{t}\left(1 - \frac{t}{2R_i}\right)
$$

For t/R_i тЙд 0.05, this reduces to the thin-wall formula.

## 3.2 Derivation: Discontinuity Stress at a CylinderтАУHead Junction

### Step 1 тАФ Kinematic Incompatibility

Consider a cylindrical shell of radius R and a 2:1 ellipsoidal head welded to it. Under internal pressure, the **free (unrestrained) radial growths** at the junction are:
- Cylinder: $\delta_{cyl} = \dfrac{pR^2}{Et}(1 - \nu/2)$
- Ellipsoidal head (at the junction where radius of curvature = R): $\delta_{head} = \dfrac{pR^2}{2Et}(1 - \nu)$

### Step 2 тАФ Incompatibility

These two displacements are not equal. Their difference $\Delta$ must be absorbed by **edge forces (P_o)** and **edge moments (M_o)** at the junction.

$$
\Delta = \delta_{cyl} - \delta_{head} = \frac{pR^2}{2Et}(1 - \nu/2)
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Solution Using Beam-on-Elastic-Foundation Analogy

The cylinder behaves like a semi-infinite beam (Winkler foundation with stiffness $k = \dfrac{Et}{R^2}$). The edge loads $M_0$ and $P_0$ decay with distance x from the junction.

The **decay constant** $\beta$ is:

$$
\beta = \left[\frac{3(1-\nu^2)}{R^2 t^2}\right]^{1/4}
$$

The displacement, rotation, moment, and shear along the cylinder are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(x) &= e^{-\beta x}\left(A \cos\beta x + B \sin\beta x\right) \\
\phi(x) &= \beta e^{-\beta x}\left[(B-A)\cos\beta x - (A+B)\sin\beta x\right] \\
M(x) &= -D \dfrac{d^2\delta}{dx^2} \\
V(x) &= -D \dfrac{d^3\delta}{dx^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where D is the flexural rigidity of the shell.

### Step 4 тАФ Edge Moment for CylinderтАУ2:1 Ellipsoidal Head

For an internal pressure p on a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the edge moment at the cylinder-head junction is given by (per Zick & Watkins, also in Megyesy):

$$
M_0 = -pR^2 \left[\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}\right] \cdot (\text{geometric factor})
$$

For a **flat head** welded to a cylinder (stiff head), the moment is approximately:

$$
M_0 \approx -0.5\, pR^2
$$

The **maximum bending stress** at the junction on the cylinder wall is:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 M_0}{t^2}
$$

### Step 5 тАФ Decay of Bending Stress

The bending stress decays exponentially:
$$
\sigma_b(x) = \sigma_{b,0} \, e^{-\beta x} \left(\cos\beta x + \sin\beta x\right)
$$

Within a distance of approximately $\pi/\beta$, the discontinuity stress is **essentially zero** тАФ this is why junction effects are localized.

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Junction Type | Pressure-induced M_0 (sign convention) | Decay Length тЙИ $\pi/\beta$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder тАУ 2:1 Ellipsoidal head | M_0 < 0 (compressive at cylinder OD) | $\pi/\beta$ |
| Cylinder тАУ flat head | M_0 тЙИ -0.5 pR┬▓ (large bending) | Short (stiff) |
| Cylinder тАУ torispherical head | Function of L/R ratio | Moderate |
| Cylinder тАУ cone (small ╬▒) | M_0 тЙИ -0.05 pR┬▓ | Long |
| Sphere тАУ cylindrical nozzle | M_0 high; reinforcement needed | Very short |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ASME Code Selection

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision tree** that a designer follows when selecting between Div-1 and Div-2, and the analytical route thereafter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Pressure Vessel] --> B{Service Conditions Defined?}
    B -- No --> B1[Define P, T, Corrosion Allowance]
    B1 --> B
    B -- Yes --> C[Select Material from ASME Section II]
    C --> D{Determine Allowable Stress Sm}
    D -- Div 1 --> D1[Sm = min Sy/3.5, Su/3]
    D -- Div 2 --> D2[Sm = min 2Sy/3, Su/3]
    D1 --> E{Calculate Required Thickness t}
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Membrane Stress Equations]
    F --> G{Discontinuities Present?}
    G -- No --> H[Check Code Compliance]
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Edge Loads M0 and P0]
    I --> J[Apply Beam-on-Elastic-Foundation Solution]
    J --> K[Decay Function e to power negative beta x]
    K --> H
    H --> L{Design by Analysis Required?}
    L -- No --> M[Use Div 1 Rule-Based]
    L -- Yes --> N[Use Div 2 Part 5 Stress Categorisation]
    N --> O[Check Pm, PL, Pb, Q, F Limits]
    O --> P[Final Design Approved]
```

## 4.2 Stress Categorisation Hierarchy (ASME Div-2, Part 5.2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Stress in Vessel Wall] --> B[Primary Stress P]
    A --> C[Secondary Stress Q]
    A --> D[Peak Stress F]
    B --> B1[General Membrane Pm - uniform - limit Sm]
    B --> B2[Local Membrane PL - within sqrt R t - limit 1.5 Sm]
    B --> B3[Bending Pb - due to pressure on non-thin region - limit 1.5 Sm]
    C --> C1[Self Equilibrating - includes discontinuity and thermal - limit 3 Sm]
    D --> D1[Localised - fatigue governed - limit Sa from Fig 5-110.1]
```

## 4.3 CylinderтАУHead Junction тАФ Force Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Pressure p] --> B[Cylindrical Shell Expansion delta_cyl]
    A --> C[Ellipsoidal Head Expansion delta_head]
    B --> D{Incompatibility Delta}
    C --> D
    D --> E[Edge Moment M0 and Shear P0 at Junction]
    E --> F[Bending Stress sigma_b at Junction]
    F --> G[Decay along length e to power negative beta x times cos and sin]
    G --> H[Negligible Beyond pi over beta]
```

## 4.4 Membrane Stress Distribution in a Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thin Cylindrical Shell Pressure p] --> B[Hoop Stress sigma_h = pR/t]
    A --> C[Longitudinal Stress sigma_L = pR/2t]
    B --> D[Maximum - governs design for cylinders under internal pressure]
    C --> E[Secondary design stress for closed-end vessels]
    D --> F[Code Reference ASME Div 1 UG-27 c 1]
    E --> F
```

> **Diagram note:** The Mermaid flowcharts above map the functional and analytical architecture of the ASME code application. The actual physical stress distribution in a cylinder is **uniform through the wall (membrane)** with **peak discontinuity stresses** localised at the end junctions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between primary membrane stress (P_m) and secondary stress (Q) as classified by ASME Section VIII, Division 2. Why is the allowable limit for Q higher than for P_m?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Primary Membrane Stress (P_m) | Secondary Stress (Q) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Internal/external pressure, mechanical loads | Self-equilibrating thermal or geometric discontinuity |
| Equilibrium with applied load | Yes (must be balanced by external forces) | No (self-equilibrating within the vessel) |
| Redistribution on yielding | No | Yes (local yielding relieves it) |
| Failure potential | Gross plastic deformation / collapse | Fatigue (ratcheting limit applies) |
| Allowable limit | $\le S_m$ | $\le 3 S_m$ |

**Reason for higher Q limit (1 mark):** Because secondary stresses are **self-equilibrating**, local plastic flow is permitted without leading to overall failure тАФ only a limited amount of distortion occurs. Hence the code permits a 3-fold higher stress limit compared to P_m.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Retest Paper]

**Q: State the membrane stress equations (per ASME Section VIII, Div-1) for (i) a thin cylindrical shell and (ii) a thin spherical shell subjected to internal pressure. Compare their magnitudes and explain why cylindrical shells are designed by hoop stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) Cylinder:
$$
\sigma_h = \frac{pR}{t} \quad ; \quad \sigma_L = \frac{pR}{2t}
$$

(ii) Sphere:
$$
\sigma = \frac{pR}{2t}
$$

**Comparison:** The hoop stress in a cylinder is **twice** the longitudinal stress and **twice** the membrane stress in a sphere of the same R, t, and p. The hoop stress is the **maximum** and therefore governs the design. (1 mark each for equations + comparison + reasoning)

---

## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]

**A 3 m internal diameter cylindrical pressure vessel of length 8 m is to operate at a design pressure of 2.5 MPa. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with S_y = 260 MPa, S_u = 485 MPa, E = 200,000 MPa, ╬╜ = 0.3. Full radiographic examination is used (E = 1.0). Corrosion allowance c = 1.5 mm.**

**(a)** Calculate the **required wall thickness** of the cylinder per ASME Section VIII, Div-1. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)** and the **longitudinal stress** for the actual thickness selected. Discuss the **discontinuity stress** expected at the cylinder-head junction. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a): Required Thickness

**Step 1: Compute allowable stress S_m (Div-1, Appendix A).**

$$
S_m = \min\left(\frac{S_y}{3.5}, \frac{S_u}{3}\right) = \min\left(\frac{260}{3.5}, \frac{485}{3}\right) = \min(74.29, 161.67) = 74.29 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Div-1, UG-27(c)(1) formula for cylinder hoop stress.**

$$
t = \frac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}
$$

where R = D/2 = 3.0 / 2 = 1.5 m = 1500 mm, S = 74.29 MPa, E = 1.0, p = 2.5 MPa.

$$
t = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{74.29 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 2.5} = \frac{3750}{74.29 - 1.5} = \frac{3750}{72.79} = 51.52 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Add corrosion allowance.**

$$
t_{design} = t + c = 51.52 + 1.5 = 53.02 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Select next standard plate thickness.** Common plate increments are 2 mm or 4 mm. Select **t_actual = 56 mm** (or 54 mm if finer increments are allowed).

**Final required thickness (rounded):** $t_{required} = 53 \text{ mm}$; selected $t = 56 \text{ mm}$. тЬУ

---

#### Model Solution to A(b): MAWP, Longitudinal Stress, and Discontinuity Discussion

**Step 1: MAWP for the selected thickness (56 mm).**

Rearranging UG-27(c)(1):
$$
p_{max} = \frac{SEt}{R + 0.6t} = \frac{74.29 \times 1.0 \times 56}{1500 + 0.6 \times 56} = \frac{4160.24}{1533.6} = 2.713 \text{ MPa}
$$

(MAWP > design pressure 2.5 MPa тЖТ Safe. тЬУ)

**Step 2: Longitudinal stress for selected t.**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 56} = \frac{3750}{112} = 33.48 \text{ MPa}
$$

Compared to $S_m = 74.29$ MPa тЖТ $\sigma_L / S_m = 0.45$ (within limit).

**Step 3: Discontinuity stress discussion.**

At the cylinder-head junction (assumed 2:1 ellipsoidal head), the **incompatible radial growth** of the cylinder and head produces an **edge moment M_0** and **edge shear V_0** at the junction. The decay constant is:

$$
\beta = \left[\frac{3(1 - 0.3^2)}{1500^2 \times 56^2}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\frac{2.73}{1.0 \times 10^{13}}\right]^{1/4} = (2.73 \times 10^{-13})^{0.25} = 7.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/mm}
$$

Decay length: $\pi/\beta \approx 4350 \text{ mm} = 4.35 \text{ m}$

**Implication:** The cylinder length is 8 m. Within тЙИ 4.35 m from each head, discontinuity bending stresses are present; over the central region, only pure membrane stresses act. The **bending stress** at the junction is:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 M_0}{t^2} \approx \frac{6 \times 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 1500^2}{56^2} = \frac{6 \times 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 2.25 \times 10^6}{3136} \approx 2687 \text{ MPa (very high locally)}
$$

This is why the **secondary stress Q (discontinuity) limit is 3 S_m = 222.87 MPa**, not 74.29 MPa. (Note: real M_0 is much smaller; an approximate M_0 тЙИ 0.5 pR┬▓ ├Ч K, where K is a geometric factor < 0.2 for ellipsoidal heads.) The discontinuity stress is **localised** and **self-equilibrating**, so it does not lead to global failure.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **stress categorisation** as per **ASME Section VIII, Div-2, Part 5**. Discuss the limits on P_m, P_L, P_b, Q, and F. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a **2:1 semi-ellipsoidal head** welded to a cylindrical shell of 1.2 m diameter, operating at 1.8 MPa, with S_m = 90 MPa, calculate:
   (i) The **crown membrane stress**.
   (ii) The **knuckle membrane stress**.
   (iii) The **required head thickness** per Div-1, UG-32(d). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B(a): Stress Categorisation

(See Section 2.4 above for the table. The student should present the **5.2.1 to 5.2.6** classification and the **limits in 5.5.2 to 5.5.6** with examples for each. Key points to write:)

- **P_m** arises from **pressure or mechanical loads**, uniform across a section. Self-equilibrating in the section. **Limit: тЙд S_m**.
- **P_L** is membrane stress averaged over a **local region not exceeding $\sqrt{Rt}$** in any direction. **Limit: тЙд 1.5 S_m** (only when region is < $\sqrt{Rt}$).
- **P_b** is primary bending due to pressure on a **non-circular cross-section** (e.g., flat head). **Limit: тЙд 1.5 S_m**.
- **Q** is secondary stress: **discontinuity** (geometric mismatch) and **thermal gradient**. **Limit: тЙд 3 S_m**.
- **F** is peak stress: local **stress concentration** (notch, weld toe). **Limit: тЙд S_a** (fatigue curve).
- **P_m + P_b тЙд 1.5 S_m** (combined primary limit).
- **P_m + P_b + Q тЙд 3 S_m** (primary + secondary limit тАФ the basis of "level D" service limits in Div-2).

---

#### Model Solution to B(b): Ellipsoidal Head Design

**Given:** D = 1.2 m = 1200 mm, p = 1.8 MPa, S_m = 90 MPa, E = 1.0, m = 2 (2:1 ellipsoidal).

**(i) Crown membrane stress:**

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the maximum stress occurs at the **crown** (top of the head) and is:

$$
\sigma_{crown} = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2t} = \frac{1080}{t} \text{ MPa (with t in mm)}
$$

(Wait тАФ actually, for 2:1 ellipsoid, crown stress = pD/(2t); knuckle stress = pD/(4t) is wrong. Let me correct:)

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head with major axis = D, minor axis = D/4 (semi-axes D/2 and D/4):
- **Crown stress** (at top): $\sigma_{crown} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$
- **Knuckle stress** (at junction with cylinder): $\sigma_{knuckle} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right) = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$

(With m = major/minor axis ratio for 2:1 head.)

**(ii) Knuckle stress:**

$$
\sigma_{knuckle} = \frac{pD}{4t}
$$

(Substituting numerical values once t is known.)

**(iii) Required thickness (Div-1, UG-32(d)):**

The code formula for an ellipsoidal head is:

$$
t = \frac{pD}{2SE - 0.2p}
$$

$$
t = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2 \times 90 \times 1.0 - 0.2 \times 1.8} = \frac{2160}{180 - 0.36} = \frac{2160}{179.64} = 12.025 \text{ mm}
$$

Add corrosion allowance c = 1.5 mm:
$$
t_{design} = 12.025 + 1.5 = 13.525 \text{ mm}
$$

**Select t = 14 mm.**

**Crown stress (with t = 14 mm):**
$$
\sigma_{crown} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2 \times 14} = \frac{2160}{28} = 77.14 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is less than S_m = 90 MPa тЬУ (safe).

**Knuckle stress (with t = 14 mm):**
$$
\sigma_{knuckle} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{4 \times 14} = \frac{2160}{56} = 38.57 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The crown is the more critical region (stress ratio 77.14/90 = 0.857 vs. knuckle 38.57/90 = 0.428), and the head is safe per Div-1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing 2:1 ellipsoidal crown formula** with sphere formula. The crown of 2:1 ellipsoid has stress = pD/(2t), **NOT** pD/(4t). The latter is the knuckle (or sphere) formula. Many students lose 2тАУ3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** when reporting "required" vs. "actual/design" thickness.
> 3. **Mixing Div-1 and Div-2 allowable stress limits** (3.5 vs. 1.5 / 2/3). Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Sign convention for edge moment M_0** тАФ the sign depends on the type of head (flat, ellipsoidal, torispherical). Always state the assumption.
> 5. **In Part B(a) stress categorisation**, students often omit the **fatigue limit on F (peak stress)**, which is a 1-mark deduction. Always include the S_a reference.
> 6. **Neglecting the -0.6p term** in the thickness formula for cylinders; it appears small but examiners expect it for high-pressure calculations.
> 7. **Decay constant ╬▓** in discontinuity analysis is **independent of pressure**; it is a pure geometric/material property.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision bullets for last-minute KTU exam prep:**

### тЬЕ ASME Section VIII Architecture
- [ ] **Div-1** тЖТ Rule-based, **S_m = min(S_y/3.5, S_u/3)**, joint efficiency E explicitly used.
- [ ] **Div-2** тЖТ Design-by-analysis, **S_m = min(2 S_y/3, S_u/3)**, no joint efficiency (E = 1.0 implied).
- [ ] **Div-3** тЖТ High-pressure vessels (>10,000 psi).
- [ ] Allowable stress lookup тЖТ **ASME Section II, Part A (ferrous) or Part B (non-ferrous)**.

### тЬЕ Membrane Stress Equations (Thin-Wall, t/R тЙд 0.1)
- [ ] **Cylinder hoop:** $\sigma_h = pR/t = pD/(2t)$ тЖР **governs design**.
- [ ] **Cylinder longitudinal:** $\sigma_L = pR/(2t) = pD/(4t)$.
- [ ] **Sphere:** $\sigma = pR/(2t) = pD/(4t)$.
- [ ] **Cone hoop:** $\sigma_h = pD/(2t\cos\alpha)$.
- [ ] **Cone meridional:** $\sigma_m = pD/(4t\cos\alpha)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal crown (2:1):** $\sigma = pD/(2t)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal knuckle (2:1):** $\sigma = pD/(4t)$ тЖР half of crown.
- [ ] **Torispherical crown:** complex formula involving L, R_i (refer to UG-32(e)).

### тЬЕ Div-1 Thickness Formulas
- [ ] **Cylinder (UG-27):** $t = pR / (SE - 0.6p)$.
- [ ] **Sphere (UG-27(d)):** $t = pR / (2SE - 0.2p)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal head (UG-32(d)):** $t = pD / (2SE - 0.2p)$.
- [ ] **Conical shell (UG-32(g)):** $t = pD / (2\cos\alpha)(SE - 0.6p)$.
- [ ] **Flat head (UG-34):** $t = D \sqrt{Kp/(SE)}$ тАФ K is a tabulated factor (0.25 to 0.33).

### тЬЕ Stress Categories and Limits (Div-2, Part 5)
- [ ] **P_m тЙд S_m** (primary membrane).
- [ ] **P_L тЙд 1.5 S_m** (local membrane, area < $\sqrt{Rt}$).
- [ ] **P_b тЙд 1.5 S_m** (primary bending).
- [ ] **P_m + P_b тЙд 1.5 S_m**.
- [ ] **Q тЙд 3 S_m** (secondary тАФ discontinuity + thermal).
- [ ] **P_m + P_b + Q тЙд 3 S_m**.
- [ ] **F тЙд S_a** (peak тАФ fatigue governed, Fig. 5-110.1).

### тЬЕ Discontinuity Stress Essentials
- [ ] Discontinuity arises at **geometric mismatch** (cylinder-head, nozzle-shell, cone-cylinder).
- [ ] Solution uses **beam-on-elastic-foundation** (Winkler model).
- [ ] **Decay constant:** $\beta = [3(1-\nu^2)/(R^2 t^2)]^{1/4}$ for cylinders.
- [ ] **Decay length** тЙИ $\pi/\beta$; beyond this, stress is negligible.
- [ ] **Edge moment M_0** and **edge shear V_0** are the two unknowns.
- [ ] For 2:1 ellipsoidal head on cylinder, M_0 < 0 (tends to bend cylinder outward at OD).
- [ ] For flat head, M_0 is **largest** тЖТ highest discontinuity stress.
- [ ] For torispherical head, M_0 depends on **L/R ratio**.

### тЬЕ Exam-Writing Tips for KTU
- [ ] **Always** state the code reference (e.g., "per ASME Sec VIII, Div-1, UG-27(c)(1)").
- [ ] **Always** include units in every step of the numerical solution.
- [ ] **Always** compute S_m and show the **min(...) selection** explicitly.
- [ ] **Always** add corrosion allowance to the calculated thickness.
- [ ] **Always** verify the design by computing the **actual stress** with the selected thickness.
- [ ] For Part B (14-mark) questions, structure the answer into: (i) Given data, (ii) Code reference, (iii) Formula, (iv) Substitution with units, (v) Result with comment, (vi) Conclusion/Safety verification.

### тЬЕ Common Numerical Values to Memorise
- [ ] **Safety factors:** 3.5 (S_y Div-1), 3.0 (S_u Div-1), 1.5 (S_y Div-2), 3.0 (S_u Div-2).
- [ ] **Poisson's ratio** for steel: 0.3.
- [ ] **Modulus of elasticity** for carbon steel: 2 ├Ч 10тБ╡ MPa.
- [ ] **Joint efficiencies:** 1.0 (100% radiography), 0.85 (spot), 0.70 (no radiography).
- [ ] **Standard corrosion allowance:** 1.5 mm (typical); 3 mm for severe service.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Thick Walled Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Thick Walled Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **thick-walled cylinder** is a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the wall thickness is comparable to (or greater than) one-twentieth of the internal diameter, i.e., the geometric ratio satisfies

$$\frac{t}{D_i} \;>\; \frac{1}{20}$$

Under this condition, the stress is **no longer uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness. The radial and hoop (circumferential/tangential) stresses become functions of the radial coordinate $r$, and their variation must be derived from the classical theory of elasticity тАФ most commonly using **Lam├й's Equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When $t/D_i \le 1/20$, the cylinder is classified as *thin-walled* and treated with the simple membrane-stress formula. When $t/D_i > 1/20$, the **thick-cylinder formulation (Lam├й)** must be applied without exception.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy тАФ "The Onion Shell"**
> Imagine slicing an onion radially. Each ring experiences a different squish тАФ the innermost ring is crushed hard by the rings around it, while the outermost shell only carries its own weight. Similarly, inside a pressurized thick cylinder, the **inner fiber** is squeezed most by pressure and the hoop stress there is the largest. As you move outward, each successive fiber carries less load. Lam├й's equations quantify exactly how this "load-sharing" decays with radius.

In contrast, a thin-walled cylinder is like a single sheet of paper stretched over a balloon тАФ the load is shared roughly equally, so we treat it as constant.

## 1.3 Why Thin-Wall Theory Fails for Thick Walls

| Aspect | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Stress through thickness | Assumed **uniform** | **Varies** with radius $r$ |
| Governing theory | Membrane equilibrium | Lam├й / Elasticity |
| Applicable ratio | $t/D_i \le 1/20$ | $t/D_i > 1/20$ |
| Radial stress magnitude | Neglected | Considered fully |
| Examples | LPG gas cylinders, small tanks | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, high-pressure boilers, reactor shells |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Internal pressure:** $p_i$ тАФ typically **MPa** or **N/mm┬▓**.
- **External pressure:** $p_o$ тАФ usually **0 MPa** for atmospheric exposure.
- **Inner radius:** $r_i$ тАФ measured in **mm**.
- **Outer radius:** $r_o = r_i + t$ тАФ measured in **mm**.
- **Allowable stress:** $\sigma_t$ or $S$ тАФ derived from **ultimate tensile strength** divided by a **factor of safety**.
- **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu$ (typically **0.3** for steels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lam├й's Governing Equations тАФ Step-by-Step Logic

The radial stress $\sigma_r$ and hoop stress $\sigma_h$ at any radius $r$ in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$ are given by:

### 2.1.1 Lam├й's Constants (A and B)

Consider an elemental ring at radius $r$. Force equilibrium + plane-strain elasticity yields two constants:

$$A = \frac{p_i \, r_i^{2} \;-\; p_o \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} \;-\; r_i^{2}}$$

$$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) \, r_i^{2} \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} \;-\; r_i^{2}}$$

### 2.1.2 The Two Stress Components

$$\sigma_r(r) = A \;-\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = A \;+\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These two equations drive every ASME Section VIII, IS 2825, and high-pressure vessel design calculation. The maximum hoop stress dictates the wall thickness required to prevent yielding at the bore.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation тАФ Master These Before Solving Any Numerical.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant $A$ | $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | MPa | тАФ |
| 2 | Constant $B$ | $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | MPa┬╖mm┬▓ | тАФ |
| 3 | Radial stress | $\sigma_r(r) = A - \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r_i$: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ |
| 4 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h(r) = A + \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r_o$: $\sigma_r = -p_o$ |
| 5 | Maximum hoop stress (closed ends case) | $\sigma_{h,\max} = \dfrac{p_i (r_o^{2}+r_i^{2}) - 2 p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2}-r_i^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 6 | Radial stress at bore | $\sigma_{r,\min} = -p_i$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 7 | Maximum shear stress (Lame) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2}\Big\vert_{\max}$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 8 | Effective stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_{\text{eff}} = \sigma_h - \sigma_r$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 9 | Cylinder "thick" condition | $t/D_i > 1/20$ | тАФ | тАФ |
| 10 | Ratio simplification (only $p_o = 0$) | $K = r_o/r_i$ | тАФ | design parameter |

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Physical Interpretation

1. **At inner surface $r = r_i$:** $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive, equals internal pressure).
2. **At outer surface $r = r_o$:** $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (if exposed to atmosphere, $p_o = 0$).
3. **Hoop stress is maximum at $r = r_i$**, regardless of whether $p_o = 0$ or $p_o > 0$.
4. **Hoop stress is minimum at $r = r_o$**.
5. **Radial stress goes from $-p_i$ (compressive) at bore to $-p_o$ (or $0$) at outer surface.**

## 2.4 Stress Distribution Across Wall (Visual Trend)

- The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ curve is **hyperbolic**, decaying outward.
- The radial stress $\sigma_r$ curve is **compressive throughout**, rising (becoming less negative) outward.
- The maximum stress differential (and therefore maximum shear) is always **at the inner bore**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Hydraulic presses and accumulators** тАФ must withstand very high internal pressures (often > 100 MPa).
> - **Gun barrels and cannon tubes** тАФ thick cylinder analysis is critical for autofrettage design.
> - **High-pressure reactor shells** in petrochemical plants тАФ designed using ASME code which references Lam├й's equations directly.
> - **Nuclear pressure vessels** тАФ radial stress variation governs the thermal-stress superposition.

## 2.5 Common Derivations Required by KTU Examiners

1. Derivation of **Lam├й's equations** from an elemental ring.
2. Derivation of expressions for **maximum hoop stress, minimum hoop stress, and maximum shear stress**.
3. Design for internal pressure given allowable stress: rearrange the $\sigma_h$ equation to solve for $r_o / r_i$.
4. Compound cylinder (shrink-fit / autofrettage) тАФ add thermal/initial fit terms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lam├й's Equations (Closed-End Cylinder)

### 3.1.1 Free Body тАФ Elemental Ring

Consider an elemental ring of unit length (or unit axial length), located at radial coordinate $r$, of thickness $dr$, subtending angle $d\theta$.

Forces acting on this element:
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ on the inner face of area $r \, d\theta \cdot 1$ acting outward.
- Radial stress $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on the outer face of area $(r+dr)\, d\theta \cdot 1$ acting inward.
- Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ on the two side faces of area $dr \cdot 1$ acting tangentially inward, with pressure $p$ acting radially.

### 3.1.2 Force Equilibrium in the Radial Direction

Summing forces in the radial direction (outward positive):

$$\sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta \;-\; (\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r+dr)\, d\theta \;+\; 2 \sigma_h \, dr \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = 0$$

For small angles, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$, and neglecting higher-order differentials $(dr \cdot d\sigma_r)$:

$$-d\sigma_r \cdot r \;-\; \sigma_r \, dr \;-\; d\sigma_r \, dr \;+\; \sigma_h \, dr = 0$$

$$-r \, d\sigma_r - \sigma_r \, dr + \sigma_h \, dr = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $dr$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} \;+\; \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_h}{r} = 0\;}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration тАФ Total Radial Force on a Half-Cylinder

Consider a half-cylinder (cut through a diametrical plane). Total radial force on the curved surface at any radius $r$ must equal the force on the projected area:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \sigma_r \cdot r \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta \, d\theta \;=\; -p_i \, r_i \, L$$

Carrying out the integration (and similarly for $p_o$ at $r_o$):

$$2 \sigma_r \, r \, L \;=\; -2 p_i r_i L \quad \text{at } r_i$$
$$2 \sigma_r \, r \, L \;=\; -2 p_o r_o L \quad \text{at } r_o$$

Combining these via superposition yields:

$$\sigma_r \;=\; \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;-\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2}\, r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

Let $A$ and $B$ denote the constants. We obtain the **first Lam├й equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_r(r) \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;}$$

where

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2}\, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Substituting into the equilibrium equation and integrating:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_h(r) \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;}$$

This is the **second Lam├й equation**.

## 3.2 Special Case: External Pressure = 0 ($p_o = 0$)

With $p_o = 0$:

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 - \frac{r_o^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + \frac{r_o^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]$$

### 3.2.1 Maximum Hoop Stress (at $r = r_i$)

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + 1\right] = p_i \, \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

### 3.2.2 Minimum Hoop Stress (at $r = r_o$)

$$\sigma_{h,\min} = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + 1\right] = \frac{2 p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

### 3.2.3 Maximum Shear Stress (at $r = r_i$)

At the bore, $\sigma_r = -p_i$, hence:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{h,\max} - \sigma_{r,\min}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[p_i \, \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + p_i\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{\max} = \frac{p_i \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}\;}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Pattern Reference)

**Problem:** A steel cylinder has $r_i = 50$ mm, $r_o = 100$ mm, and carries an internal pressure $p_i = 80$ MPa with $p_o = 0$. Find (i) hoop stress at $r = 50$ mm and $r = 75$ mm, (ii) radial stress at those radii, (iii) maximum shear stress.

**Step 1 тАФ Compute $A$ and $B$:**

$$A = \frac{80 \times 50^{2}}{100^{2} - 50^{2}} = \frac{80 \times 2500}{7500} = 26.667 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{80 \times 50^{2} \times 100^{2}}{100^{2} - 50^{2}} = \frac{80 \times 2500 \times 10000}{7500} = 266{,}667 \text{ MPa┬╖mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 тАФ At $r = 50$ mm:**

$$\sigma_r = 26.667 - \frac{266{,}667}{50^{2}} = 26.667 - 106.667 = -80.0 \text{ MPa} \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\sigma_h = 26.667 + \frac{266{,}667}{50^{2}} = 26.667 + 106.667 = 133.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 тАФ At $r = 75$ mm:**

$$\sigma_r = 26.667 - \frac{266{,}667}{75^{2}} = 26.667 - 47.41 = -20.74 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_h = 26.667 + \frac{266{,}667}{75^{2}} = 26.667 + 47.41 = 74.07 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Maximum shear stress at $r = r_i$:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2} = \frac{133.33 - (-80)}{2} = 106.67 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Alert:** Notice the hoop stress at the bore (133.33 MPa) is significantly higher than the thin-wall estimate would give ($\sigma_h = pr/t = 80 \times 50 / 50 = 80$ MPa). This is exactly why Lam├й's analysis is mandatory for thick cylinders.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Lam├й's Stress Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressure Vessel Geometry Input] --> B{Is t/Di greater than 1 by 20?}
    B -- No --> C[Apply Thin Wall Theory Membrane Stresses]
    B -- Yes --> D[Activate Lame Analysis Module]
    D --> E[Identify pi and po]
    D --> F[Identify ri and ro]
    E --> G[Compute Constant A]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Compute Constant B]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Compute Sigma r at any r]
    H --> I
    G --> J[Compute Sigma h at any r]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Evaluate at inner radius ri]
    J --> K
    I --> L[Evaluate at outer radius ro]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Maximum Hoop Stress Check]
    L --> N[Minimum Hoop Stress Check]
    K --> O[Maximum Shear Stress at Bore]
    M --> P[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P -- Safe --> Q[Design Accepted]
    P -- Unsafe --> R[Increase Wall Thickness or Reduce pi]
```

## 4.2 Schematic тАФ Elemental Ring and Stress Directions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Inner Radius ri with Internal Pressure pi acting outward] --> nodeB[Elemental Ring of Thickness dr at radius r]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Radial Stress Sigma r acting inward on outer face]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Hoop Stress Sigma h acting tangentially on side faces]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Outer Radius ro with External Pressure po acting inward]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Radial Equilibrium yields Lame Constants A and B]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Final Equations Sigma r = A minus B by r squared]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Final Equations Sigma h = A plus B by r squared]
```

## 4.3 Radial Stress vs Hoop Stress Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisStart[Centerline at r equals 0] --> axisInner[At r equals ri: Sigma r equals negative pi and Sigma h equals maximum]
    axisInner --> axisMid[At r equals mid radius: Sigma r and Sigma h move closer together]
    axisMid --> axisOuter[At r equals ro: Sigma r equals negative po and Sigma h equals minimum]
    axisInner --> note1[Maximum Shear Stress Located at Inner Bore]
    axisMid --> note2[Hoop Stress Decays Hyperbolically Outward]
    axisOuter --> note3[Radial Stress Becomes Zero when po equals 0]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix тАФ Thick vs Thin Wall

| Decision Factor | Thin Wall Path | Thick Wall Path |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric Ratio $t/D_i$ | $\le 1/20$ | $> 1/20$ |
| Governing Equation | $\sigma_h = p r / t$ | Lam├й's equations |
| Hoop Stress Variation | Constant through thickness | Hyperbolic, max at bore |
| Radial Stress | Neglected (assumed zero) | Considered, varies from $-p_i$ to $-p_o$ |
| Typical Vessel Examples | LPG cylinders, small air receivers | Hydraulic presses, gun barrels, high-pressure boilers |
| KTU Exam Weightage | 2тАУ3 marks | 7тАУ14 marks (frequently Part B) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`
**Define a thick cylinder. State the conditions under which a cylinder is classified as thick.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cylinder is termed **thick** when its wall thickness is comparable to or exceeds one-twentieth of its internal diameter, i.e., when

$$\frac{t}{D_i} > \frac{1}{20}$$

Under such a condition, the stress distribution across the wall is **non-uniform**, with hoop stress being maximum at the inner surface and decaying outward hyperbolically. In contrast, for a thin cylinder ($t/D_i \le 1/20$), the stress is assumed uniformly distributed and is computed using membrane theory. Hence, thick cylinders must be analyzed using **Lam├й's equations**, which account for radial stress variation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`
**Write down Lam├й's equations for radial and hoop stress in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;-\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;+\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

These can be compactly written as $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ and $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$, where $A$ and $B$ are Lam├й's constants.

---

## PART B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023]`

A thick steel cylinder has an internal diameter of **150 mm** and external diameter of **300 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **60 MPa**. The external pressure is atmospheric and can be neglected. Calculate:
**(a)** Lam├й's constants $A$ and $B$.
**(b)** The hoop stress and radial stress at the inner and outer surfaces. Comment on the maximum shear stress.

#### Part (a) тАФ Lam├й's Constants (7 marks)

Given:
- $r_i = 75$ mm
- $r_o = 150$ mm
- $p_i = 60$ MPa
- $p_o = 0$

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 75^{2}}{150^{2} - 75^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 5625}{22500 - 5625} = \frac{337500}{16875} = 20.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 5625 \times 22500}{16875} = \frac{7{,}593{,}750{,}000}{16875} = 450{,}000 \text{ MPa┬╖mm}^{2}$$

**Therefore $A = 20$ MPa and $B = 4.5 \times 10^{5}$ MPa┬╖mm┬▓.**

#### Part (b) тАФ Stresses at Inner and Outer Surfaces (7 marks)

**At inner surface $r = 75$ mm:**

$$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 20 + \frac{450000}{5625} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 20 - 80 = -60 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(matches } -p_i\text{)}$$

**At outer surface $r = 150$ mm:**

$$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r_o^{2}} = 20 + \frac{450000}{22500} = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r_o^{2}} = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(matches } -p_o = 0\text{)}$$

**Maximum Shear Stress (at inner bore):**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2}\bigg\vert_{r = r_i} = \frac{100 - (-60)}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comment:** The hoop stress varies from 100 MPa at the bore to 40 MPa at the outer surface тАФ confirming the hyperbolic decay. Maximum shear stress is at the inner bore, which is the most likely location for yielding initiation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]`

A thick cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal radius of **100 mm** and is required to withstand an internal pressure of **100 MPa**. The allowable tensile stress for the material is **240 MPa**. The external pressure is negligible.
**(a)** Determine the required outer radius using Lam├й's equation.
**(b)** Calculate the maximum hoop stress and verify the design is safe. Also find the radial stress at mid-thickness.

#### Part (a) тАФ Required Outer Radius (7 marks)

For maximum hoop stress condition at the inner surface (Lam├й, $p_o = 0$):

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$$

$$240 \ge 100 \cdot \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

$$\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = 2.4$$

Let $r_o^{2} = x$. Then:

$$x + 10000 = 2.4(x - 10000)$$
$$x + 10000 = 2.4x - 24000$$
$$34000 = 1.4 x$$
$$x = \frac{34000}{1.4} = 24285.71$$

$$r_o = \sqrt{24285.71} = 155.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Therefore the required outer radius is approximately 156 mm** (round up for safety, often take $r_o = 160$ mm).

#### Part (b) тАФ Stress Verification and Mid-Thickness Radial Stress (7 marks)

Using $r_o = 156$ mm:

$$A = \frac{100 \times 100^{2}}{156^{2} - 100^{2}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{24336 - 10000} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{14336} = 69.75 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{100 \times 100^{2} \times 156^{2}}{14336} = \frac{2.4336 \times 10^{10}}{14336} = 1.6976 \times 10^{6} \text{ MPa┬╖mm}^{2}$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress at bore:**

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = A + \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 69.75 + \frac{1.6976 \times 10^{6}}{10000} = 69.75 + 169.76 = 239.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $239.5 \text{ MPa} < 240 \text{ MPa}$ тАФ **Design is SAFE** тЬУ

**Radial Stress at Mid-Thickness** ($r = (100+156)/2 = 128$ mm):

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}} = 69.75 - \frac{1.6976 \times 10^{6}}{128^{2}} = 69.75 - 103.62 = -33.87 \text{ MPa}$$

The radial stress at mid-thickness is approximately **тИТ33.87 MPa (compressive)**, confirming the radial stress decays from тИТ100 MPa at the bore to 0 MPa at the outer surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** $r$ values must remain in **mm** consistently when $B$ has units MPa┬╖mm┬▓. Mixing cm with mm is a guaranteed 1тАУ2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting external pressure:** If $p_o \ne 0$, both $A$ and $B$ formulas must include the $p_o r_o^{2}$ term. Many students wrongly use only the $p_o = 0$ form.
> 3. **Sign convention error:** Radial stress at the bore should come out **negative** (compressive). If your answer is positive, you have a sign error тАФ typically caught by the examiner.
> 4. **Maximum shear stress location:** Examiners expect $\tau_{\max}$ to be evaluated **at the inner bore**, not at $r = r_o$ or $r = 0$.
> 5. **Thin-wall shortcut applied incorrectly:** KTU examiners specifically test whether students recognize when thin-wall formulas are **invalid** тАФ a 1-mark deduction for using $\sigma_h = pr/t$ on a thick-wall problem.
> 6. **Hoop stress at bore:** A very common slip is computing hoop stress at $r = r_o$ instead of $r = r_i$. Always remember: **maximum hoop stress is at the inner bore**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Print This Before Your Exam!**

- **Thick cylinder definition:** $t/D_i > 1/20$ тАФ use Lam├й, not membrane theory.
- **Lam├й's two equations:** $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ and $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$.
- **Lam├й's constants:**
  - $A = (p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2})/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$
  - $B = (p_i - p_o) r_i^{2} r_o^{2}/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$
- **Boundary conditions:** $\sigma_r(r_i) = -p_i$ and $\sigma_r(r_o) = -p_o$.
- **Hoop stress is maximum at $r = r_i$** (inner bore) тАФ always.
- **Hoop stress is minimum at $r = r_o$** (outer surface).
- **Radial stress is always compressive** between $r_i$ and $r_o$.
- **Maximum shear stress occurs at the inner bore:** $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_{h,\max} - \sigma_{r,\min})/2$.
- **For $p_o = 0$ simplified form:** $\sigma_{h,\max} = p_i (r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$.
- **Design equation (rearrange for $r_o$):** $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \ge p_i (K^2 + 1)/(K^2 - 1)$, where $K = r_o/r_i$.
- **Plane strain assumption:** Valid for long cylinders; for short cylinders, end effects (3D stress state) must be considered.
- **Material is assumed homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** тАФ Lam├й's derivation relies on Hooke's Law in the radial direction.
- **Compound cylinders** (next Module 3 topic) build on these same equations by adding a third boundary at the interface radius.
- **Tresca vs von Mises:** For thick cylinders, **Tresca** is more conservative and is the default check in ASME codes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lame's Equation for Internal and External Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lame's Equation for Internal and External Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Lam├й's Equations** constitute the closed-form analytical solution for the **two-dimensional axisymmetric stress distribution** in a **thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessel** subjected to uniform internal and/or external pressure. The equations describe the **radial stress (╧Гс╡г)** and the **circumferential / hoop stress (╧Г╬╕)** at any radial location `r` within the wall of the cylinder, under the fundamental assumptions of:

- **Plane strain** (long cylinder, plane sections remain plane) тАФ sometimes plane stress for very short vessels.
- **Axisymmetry** тАФ no variation in the angular (╬╕) or axial (z) direction of stresses.
- **Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material**.
- **No body forces** (gravity neglected relative to pressure).
- **Uniform radial pressure** on the inner (r = a) and outer (r = b) surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Lam├й's equations form the foundation of Module 2. They are the precursor to **compound cylinders, shrink-fit assemblies, autofrettage, and external pressure stability** problems. Mastering the derivation and boundary-condition application is **non-negotiable** for ESE and module tests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a **roll of toilet paper** (thick wall = many layers) with your palm. The outer paper feels one pressure, the inner cardboard core feels another, and every layer in between carries a *different* share of the load.

Now imagine a **single sheet of paper** squeezed тАФ every fiber feels nearly the same stress (thin-wall approximation). Lam├й's equations are the *mathematical microscope* that tells you exactly how much stress each "layer" of the thick wall carries.

> **Plain English Summary:**  
> Lam├й's equations tell us that **stress is NOT uniform** across the thickness of a thick cylinder. The hoop stress is **maximum at the inner surface** and **minimum at the outer surface** for internal pressure. For external pressure, the compressive hoop stress is **maximum at the outer surface**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Inner radius:** `a` (m or mm)
- **Outer radius:** `b` (m or mm)
- **Internal pressure:** `pс╡в` (MPa or N/mm┬▓)
- **External pressure:** `pтВС` (MPa or N/mm┬▓)
- **Radial coordinate:** `r` (m or mm), where `a тЙд r тЙд b`
- **Stress convention:** **Tensile = positive**, Compressive = negative
- **Young's Modulus:** `E` (typically **200 GPa** for carbon steel, **70 GPa** for aluminum alloys)
- **Poisson's ratio:** `╬╜` (typically **0.3** for steels)
- **Yield strength:** `╧Гy` (typically **250 MPa** for SA-516 Gr.70 carbon steel)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Pressure acting inward on a free surface produces a **compressive radial stress**. So at r = a, ╧Гс╡г = тИТpс╡в. At r = b, ╧Гс╡г = тИТpтВС (if external pressure exists). Students frequently lose marks by using the wrong sign.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (Why the Theory Works)

1. **Long cylinder approximation:** Plane sections remain plane тЖТ axial stress is constant across the cross-section. We treat it as a 2D plane problem.
2. **No angular variation:** The vessel is symmetric about its central axis тЖТ no shear stress ╧Дс╡г╬╕ exists.
3. **Equilibrium is radial:** The element only experiences radial forces.
4. **Compatibility:** Strains are related to displacements such that the cylinder remains continuous (no gaps).

### 2.2 Derivation Logic Flow (Bulleted Reasoning)

- **Step 1 тАФ Free Body Cut:** Imagine slicing the cylinder with two radial planes (separated by angle d╬╕) and two concentric cylinders (at radii r and r + dr).
- **Step 2 тАФ Force Balance:** Sum forces in the radial direction on this curved wedge element.
- **Step 3 тАФ Pressure ├Ч Area тЖТ Stress ├Ч Area:** Convert pressure on inner/outer curved faces to equivalent forces, and ring stresses on the radial cut faces to forces.
- **Step 4 тАФ The Governing ODE:** Yields the first fundamental equilibrium equation:
$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$
- **Step 5 тАФ Compatibility via Displacement:** Express radial displacement `u` in terms of strains ╬╡с╡г and ╬╡╬╕. Hooke's Law in cylindrical coordinates:
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$
- **Step 6 тАФ StressтАУStrain Relations:** Apply generalized Hooke's law to get ╧Гс╡г and ╧Г╬╕ in terms of u.
- **Step 7 тАФ Substitute into Equilibrium:** Obtain a second-order ODE in u(r).
- **Step 8 тАФ Solve the ODE:** General solution `u = CтВБr + CтВВ/r`. Differentiate to get stresses.
- **Step 9 тАФ Apply Boundary Conditions:** Use known pressures at r = a and r = b to find CтВБ and CтВВ.
- **Step 10 тАФ Final Lam├й Equations** emerge.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Equilibrium (radial) | $d\sigma_r/dr + (\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r = 0$ | тАФ | First fundamental relation |
| 2 | Radial stress (combined) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \dfrac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$ | MPa | General Lam├й result |
| 3 | Hoop stress (combined) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$ | MPa | General Lam├й result |
| 4 | Max hoop (internal p only) | $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i\,(b^2 + a^2)/(b^2 - a^2)$ | MPa | Occurs at r = a |
| 5 | Min hoop (internal p only) | $\sigma_{\theta,\min} = 2 p_i a^2/(b^2 - a^2)$ | MPa | Occurs at r = b |
| 6 | Max radial (internal p only) | $\sigma_{r,\max} = -p_i$ (at r = a) | MPa | Compressive at bore |
| 7 | Min radial (internal p only) | $\sigma_{r,\min} = 0$ (at r = b) | MPa | Free surface |
| 8 | External p only тАФ ╧Гс╡г | $\sigma_r = -\dfrac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$ | MPa | Always compressive |
| 9 | External p only тАФ ╧Г╬╕ | $\sigma_\theta = -\dfrac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$ | MPa | Max compressive at r = b |
| 10 | Radius ratio | $k = a/b$ (or $b/a$, check textbook) | тАФ | Used for simplified tables |
| 11 | Lam├й constants (general) | $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$, $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^2$ | MPa | A, B found from BCs |
| 12 | Hoop/radial ratio (internal p) | $\sigma_\theta/\sigma_r = -(b^2 + r^2)/(b^2 - r^2)$ | тАФ | Negative тЖТ opposite signs |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL MISTAKE AVOIDED:** Never write `╧Гr = A + B/r┬▓` and `╧Г╬╕ = A тИТ B/r┬▓`. The + and тИТ are **flipped** compared to intuitive guess. The hoop stress carries the **plus** because of the bending-like curvature effect.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of high-pressure reactors**: hydrocrackers, ammonia converters, urea reactors operating at **200тАУ400 bar** where thin-wall theory fails (D/t > ~20 is no longer valid).
- **Gun barrels and cannon tubes**: autofrettage (a process built directly on Lam├й's equations) introduces beneficial compressive residual stresses.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant pipes**: subject to both internal coolant pressure and external insulation/overburden pressure.
- **Submarine hulls and deep-sea pipelines**: dominant external hydrostatic pressure on cylindrical hulls.
- **Compound cylinders**: shrink-fit assemblies (e.g., artillery, autoclaves) where the Lam├й principle is applied in successive layers.
- **Hydraulic cylinders**: thick-walled barrels for high-pressure actuation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Lam├й's Equations

#### Step 1 тАФ Geometry and Element

Consider a thick-walled cylinder with:
- Inner radius = `a`
- Outer radius = `b`
- Subjected to internal pressure `pс╡в` at r = a and external pressure `pтВС` at r = b.

Cut out a small wedge-shaped element bounded by:
- Two concentric cylinders at radii `r` and `r + dr`
- Two radial planes at angles `0` and `d╬╕`

The arc length of the inner face is `(r d╬╕)`, outer face is `((r + dr) d╬╕)`, and the radial thickness is `dr`. The axial length is taken as unity (plane analysis).

#### Step 2 тАФ Radial Equilibrium of the Element

Sum forces in the **radial direction** (outward positive):

Force from ╧Гс╡г on inner face (acting inward on the element): `тИТ╧Гс╡г ├Ч (r d╬╕) ├Ч 1`
Force from ╧Гс╡г on outer face (acting outward on element): `(╧Гс╡г + d╧Гс╡г)(r + dr)d╬╕ ├Ч 1`
Force from ╧Г╬╕ on the two radial faces (inward components): `тИТ2 ╧Г╬╕ sin(d╬╕/2) ├Ч dr ├Ч 1`

For small d╬╕, `sin(d╬╕/2) тЙИ d╬╕/2`, so the two ╧Г╬╕ terms combine to `тИТ╧Г╬╕ ├Ч d╬╕ ├Ч dr`.

Setting ╬гFс╡г = 0:

$$(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)\,d\theta - \sigma_r\,r\,d\theta - \sigma_\theta\,d\theta\,dr = 0$$

#### Step 3 тАФ Simplify the Equilibrium Equation

Expand `(╧Гс╡г + d╧Гс╡г)(r + dr)`:

$$= \sigma_r\,r + \sigma_r\,dr + r\,d\sigma_r + dr\,d\sigma_r$$

Drop the second-order term `dr┬╖d╧Гс╡г`. Substitute back:

$$\sigma_r\,r + \sigma_r\,dr + r\,d\sigma_r - \sigma_r\,r - \sigma_\theta\,dr = 0$$

The `╧Гс╡г┬╖r` terms cancel. Divide by `r┬╖dr`:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

This is **Eq. (1)** тАФ the radial equilibrium of a thick cylinder.

#### Step 4 тАФ StrainтАУDisplacement Relations (Kinematics)

For axisymmetric deformation, the only displacement is radial `u(r)`:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$

#### Step 5 тАФ Generalized Hooke's Law (Plane Stress Form)

Assuming **plane stress** (long cylinder with free ends, or analysis neglecting ╧Г_z):

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu\,\sigma_\theta)$$
$$\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_\theta - \nu\,\sigma_r)$$

#### Step 6 тАФ Compatibility Condition

Eliminate `u` by differentiating:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_\theta}{dr} = \frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_r - \varepsilon_\theta}{r}$$

Substitute Hooke's law expressions, simplify using equilibrium Eq. (1), and after algebraic manipulation we obtain the **second-order ODE**:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2} = 0$$

#### Step 7 тАФ General Solution

This is a CauchyтАУEuler equation. Try `u = rтБ┐`:

$$n(n-1) + n - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \pm 1$$

Therefore:

$$u(r) = C_1\,r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Where `CтВБ` and `CтВВ` are constants of integration.

#### Step 8 тАФ Stresses from Displacement

Substitute u(r) back into the Hooke's law expressions. After simplification:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{(1-\nu^2)(1+\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)C_1 - (1+\nu)\frac{C_2}{r^2}\right] \cdot \text{(plane stress factor)}$$

For the **plane strain / thick cylinder** standard form (combining constants), we write:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

Where:
- `A` and `B` are **Lam├й constants**
- `A = E\,C_1 / [(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)] ┬╖ (1тИТ╬╜)` (for plane strain), or analogous plane-stress form
- `B = тИТE\,C_2 / [(1+\nu)(1тИТ2╬╜)]` (for plane strain), or analogous plane-stress form

> **Key Insight:** The form `╧Гс╡г = A тИТ B/r┬▓` and `╧Г╬╕ = A + B/r┬▓` is the **only** mathematical shape that satisfies both equilibrium and compatibility for an axisymmetric thick cylinder.

#### Step 9 тАФ Apply Boundary Conditions (Internal Pressure Only: pтВС = 0)

Boundary conditions:
- At r = a: ╧Гс╡г = тИТpс╡в (compressive radial stress, since pressure pushes inward on the bore)
- At r = b: ╧Гс╡г = 0 (free outer surface)
