From `╧Гс╡г(b) = 0`:

$$A - \frac{B}{b^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{B}{b^2}$$

From `╧Гс╡г(a) = тИТpс╡в`:

$$A - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p_i$$

Substitute A:

$$\frac{B}{b^2} - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p_i$$

$$B\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{a^2}\right) = -p_i$$

$$B\cdot\frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2 b^2} = -p_i$$

$$B = \frac{p_i\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

Then:

$$A = \frac{B}{b^2} = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

#### Step 10 тАФ Final Lam├й Equations (Internal Pressure Only)

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

**Verification:**
- At r = a: ╧Гс╡г = pс╡в┬╖a┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 тИТ b┬▓/a┬▓) = pс╡в┬╖a┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(a┬▓тИТb┬▓)/a┬▓ = тИТpс╡в тЬУ
- At r = b: ╧Гс╡г = pс╡в┬╖a┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 тИТ 1) = 0 тЬУ
- At r = a: ╧Г╬╕ = pс╡в┬╖a┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 + b┬▓/a┬▓) = pс╡в(b┬▓ + a┬▓)/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓) тАФ maximum тЬУ

#### Step 11 тАФ Lam├й Equations for External Pressure Only (pс╡в = 0, pтВС = p)

Boundary conditions:
- At r = a: ╧Гс╡г = 0 (bore is open / no internal pressure)
- At r = b: ╧Гс╡г = тИТpтВС (compressive)

From `╧Гс╡г(a) = 0`:

$$A - \frac{B}{a^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{B}{a^2}$$

From `╧Гс╡г(b) = тИТpтВС`:

$$\frac{B}{a^2} - \frac{B}{b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B\cdot\frac{b^2 - a^2}{a^2 b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B = -\frac{p_e\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

Then:

$$A = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

So:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

**Verification:**
- At r = b: ╧Г╬╕ = тИТpтВС┬╖b┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 + a┬▓/b┬▓) = тИТpтВС(b┬▓ + a┬▓)/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓) тАФ maximum compression тЬУ
- At r = a: ╧Г╬╕ = тИТpтВС┬╖b┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 + 1) = тИТ2pтВС┬╖b┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓) тАФ wait, this is bigger? No: substitute a тЖТ a, and since a < b, the magnitude at r = b is larger. тЬУ

#### Step 12 тАФ Combined Internal and External Pressure (By Superposition)

Since the problem is **linear elastic**, superpose the two cases:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A thick-walled cylinder has inner radius **a = 50 mm** and outer radius **b = 100 mm**. It is subjected to internal pressure **pс╡в = 80 MPa** and external pressure **pтВС = 10 MPa**. Calculate ╧Гс╡г and ╧Г╬╕ at r = 50 mm, 75 mm, and 100 mm. Find the location and value of maximum hoop stress.

**Solution:**

Given:
- a = 50 mm, b = 100 mm
- pс╡в = 80 MPa, pтВС = 10 MPa
- pс╡в тИТ pтВС = 70 MPa
- b┬▓ тИТ a┬▓ = 10000 тИТ 2500 = 7500 mm┬▓
- a┬▓b┬▓ = 2500 ├Ч 10000 = 2.5 ├Ч 10тБ╖ mmтБ┤

Compute the common coefficient:

$$C_1 = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{(80)(2500) - (10)(10000)}{7500} = \frac{200000 - 100000}{7500} = \frac{100000}{7500} = 13.333 \text{ MPa}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{(70)(2.5 \times 10^7)}{7500} = \frac{1.75 \times 10^9}{7500} = 2.333 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa┬╖mm}^2$$

Now compute at each radius:

**At r = a = 50 mm (r┬▓ = 2500):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{2500} = 13.333 - 93.333 = -80 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 93.333 = 106.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 75 mm (r┬▓ = 5625):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{5625} = 13.333 - 41.481 = -28.148 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 41.481 = 54.815 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = b = 100 mm (r┬▓ = 10000):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{10000} = 13.333 - 23.333 = -10 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 23.333 = 36.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress:** Occurs at **r = a = 50 mm**, with **╧Г╬╕_max = 106.667 MPa**.

**Equivalent verification using simplified formula:**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\max} = \frac{p_i\,(b^2 + a^2) - 2 p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{80(10000+2500) - 2(10)(10000)}{7500} = \frac{200000 - 200000}{7500} + 80\cdot\frac{12500}{7500}$$

Wait, let me recompute:

$$\sigma_\theta(r=a) = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$= \frac{(80)(2500) - (10)(10000)}{7500} + \frac{(70)(10000)}{7500}$$

$$= \frac{100000}{7500} + \frac{700000}{7500} = \frac{800000}{7500} = 106.667 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Free Body Element for Equilibrium Derivation

```mermaid

    nodeI["Outer pressure force equals sigma r plus d sigma r times r plus dr d theta"]
    nodeJ["Hoop force equals 2 sigma theta sin d theta over 2 times dr"]
    nodeK["Equilibrium First Equation"]
    nodeL["d sigma r over dr plus sigma r minus sigma theta over r equals zero"]
    nodeM["Compatibility and Hookes Law"]
    nodeN["Second Order ODE in u of r"]
    nodeO["General solution u equals C1 r plus C2 over r"]
    nodeP["Lame Stress Equations"]

    nodeR["sigma theta equals A plus B over r squared"]
    nodeS["Apply Boundary Conditions"]
    nodeT["Final Lam├й Equations"]
    nodeU["sigma r equals p i a squared over b squared minus a squared times 1 minus b squared over r squared"]
    nodeV["sigma theta equals p i a squared over b squared minus a squared times 1 plus b squared over r squared"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]** State the assumptions made in deriving Lam├й's equations for a thick-walled cylinder.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The cylinder material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (1 mark).
2. The cylinder is **long** so that plane sections remain plane тАФ plane strain or plane stress assumption is valid (1 mark).
3. The loading is **axisymmetric** (uniform pressure around the circumference), and **body forces (gravity) are neglected** relative to pressure (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]** Write down the general Lam├й equations for radial and circumferential stresses in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure `pс╡в` and external pressure `pтВС`.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_e)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

Where `a` = inner radius, `b` = outer radius, `r` = radial coordinate.

---

### 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). Students choose **ONE** from the OR alternatives.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** тАФ **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Lam├й equation for the radial and hoop stress distribution in a thick-walled cylinder subjected to **internal pressure only**. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 тАФ Assumptions** (1 mark)
- Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material
- Long cylinder тЖТ plane sections remain plane
- Axisymmetric loading
- No body forces
- Uniform pressure on the inner surface

**Step 2 тАФ Equilibrium** (2 marks)

Consider an element at radius r with thickness dr and angle d╬╕. Force balance gives:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0 \quad \cdots \text{(i)}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Compatibility & Hooke's Law** (2 marks)

Using ╬╡с╡г = du/dr and ╬╡╬╕ = u/r, with Hooke's law, the displacement is:

$$u(r) = C_1 r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Stresses take the form:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Boundary Conditions** (1 mark)

- At r = a: ╧Гс╡г = тИТpс╡в
- At r = b: ╧Гс╡г = 0

Solving: `A = pс╡вa┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)` and `B = pс╡вa┬▓b┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)` (1 mark)

**Final Lam├й Equations:**

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right) \quad \text{(1 mark, final boxed result)}$$

---

**(b)** A thick-walled cylinder has **inner radius 75 mm** and **outer radius 150 mm**. It is subjected to **internal pressure of 60 MPa**. Determine the **radial and hoop stresses** at radii **r = 75 mm, 100 mm, and 150 mm**. Also find the **maximum hoop stress** and its location. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** a = 75 mm, b = 150 mm, pс╡в = 60 MPa, pтВС = 0

**Compute the common factor:**

$$\frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{60 \times 75^2}{150^2 - 75^2} = \frac{60 \times 5625}{22500 - 5625} = \frac{337500}{16875} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 75 mm (r┬▓ = 5625, b┬▓/r┬▓ = 22500/5625 = 4):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 4) = -60 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks for substitution})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 4) = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 100 mm (r┬▓ = 10000, b┬▓/r┬▓ = 22500/10000 = 2.25):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 2.25) = -25 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 2.25) = 65 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**At r = 150 mm (r┬▓ = 22500, b┬▓/r┬▓ = 1):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 1) = 0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 1) = 40 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress:** ╧Г╬╕_max = **100 MPa** at **r = a = 75 mm** (inner surface) (1 mark).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks тАФ Alternative)** тАФ **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the general Lam├й equations, derive the expressions for **radial and hoop stresses** in a thick-walled cylinder subjected to **external pressure only**. Also state the location of maximum stress. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**General Lam├й form:**

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Boundary Conditions for External Pressure Only (pс╡в = 0, pтВС = p):**
- At r = a: ╧Гс╡г = 0 (bore open / no internal pressure) тЖТ A = B/a┬▓ (1 mark)
- At r = b: ╧Гс╡г = тИТpтВС (compressive) (1 mark)

Substituting A = B/a┬▓ into the second BC:

$$\frac{B}{a^2} - \frac{B}{b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B = -\frac{p_e a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}, \quad A = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Final Lam├й Equations for External Pressure:**

$$\sigma_r = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) \quad (1 \text{ mark for boxed equations})$$

**Location of Maximum Stress:** Since ╧Г╬╕ is negative and the magnitude grows with r┬▓, the **maximum compressive hoop stress** occurs at the **outer surface r = b** (1 mark):

$$|\sigma_\theta|_{\max} = \frac{p_e(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

---

**(b)** A thick-walled cylinder has **inner radius 100 mm** and **outer radius 200 mm**. It is subjected to **external pressure of 25 MPa**. Determine the **radial and hoop stresses** at **r = 100 mm, 150 mm, and 200 mm**. Find the **maximum compressive hoop stress** and verify the boundary condition at the bore. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** a = 100 mm, b = 200 mm, pтВС = 25 MPa, pс╡в = 0

**Common coefficient:**

$$\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{25 \times 40000}{40000 - 10000} = \frac{1000000}{30000} = 33.333 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 100 mm (a┬▓/r┬▓ = 10000/10000 = 1):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 1) = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(verified тАФ free bore)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 1) = -66.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 150 mm (a┬▓/r┬▓ = 10000/22500 = 0.444):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 0.444) = -18.519 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 0.444) = -48.148 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**At r = 200 mm (a┬▓/r┬▓ = 10000/40000 = 0.25):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 0.25) = -25 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(verified тАФ equals тИТpтВС)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 0.25) = -41.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum Compressive Hoop Stress** (most negative): **|╧Г╬╕|max = 66.667 MPa** (compression), occurring at **r = a = 100 mm** (inner surface) (1 mark). 

> Note: Although the magnitude increases from outer surface to inner surface for the external pressure case, the **magnitude** at r = a is greater because the expression ╧Г╬╕ = тИТpтВСb┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖(1 + a┬▓/r┬▓) reaches its absolute maximum when r is smallest (r = a). Always check by substituting; do not assume "outer surface" based on intuition.

**Boundary Verification:** ╧Гс╡г(r = a) = 0 тЬУ and ╧Гс╡г(r = b) = тИТpтВС = тИТ25 MPa тЬУ (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign error** (most common, ~40% of marks lost): Forgetting that ╧Гс╡г is **compressive (тИТpс╡в or тИТpтВС)** at the loaded surface. If you write ╧Гс╡г(a) = +pс╡в, you will lose **2тАУ3 marks** instantly.
> - **Confusing A and B**: Some students swap the constants and produce a wrong stress distribution. Always verify that ╧Г╬╕ is **maximum at r = a** for internal pressure.
> - **Units mismatch**: Mixing N/mm┬▓ and MPa inconsistently. Both are equal numerically, but mention the unit explicitly.
> - **No boundary verification**: KTU examiners **always reward** students who verify that ╧Гс╡г(b) = 0 for internal pressure case.
> - **Incomplete derivation**: Stating the final equations without showing equilibrium + compatibility gets **partial credit only**. Always derive both.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Lam├й's equations** give ╧Гс╡г(r) and ╧Г╬╕(r) for thick-walled cylinders under uniform internal/external pressure.
- **Equilibrium ODE:** `d╧Гс╡г/dr + (╧Гс╡г тИТ ╧Г╬╕)/r = 0` тАФ must memorize.
- **General solution form:** `╧Гс╡г = A тИТ B/r┬▓` and `╧Г╬╕ = A + B/r┬▓`. Note the **sign** тАФ hoop gets **+**.
- **Sign convention:** Tensile = positive. Pressure produces **compressive** ╧Гс╡г at the loaded surface.
- **Internal pressure only:**
  - ╧Г╬╕ is **max at r = a**: `╧Г╬╕_max = pс╡в(b┬▓ + a┬▓)/(b┬▓ тИТ a┬▓)`
  - ╧Г╬╕ is **min at r = b**: `╧Г╬╕_min = 2pс╡вa┬▓/(b┬▓ тИТ a┬▓)`
  - ╧Гс╡г ranges from **тИТpс╡в at r = a** to **0 at r = b**.
- **External pressure only:**
  - Both ╧Гс╡г and ╧Г╬╕ are **compressive**.
  - **|╧Г╬╕|max** at **r = a** (inner surface, smallest radius): `|╧Г╬╕|_max = pтВСb┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)┬╖2 = 2pтВСb┬▓/(b┬▓тИТa┬▓)`.
  - **|╧Гс╡г|max** at **r = b**: equals `pтВС`.
- **Combined pressure:** Linear superposition applies. The general expression has two terms: a constant and a `1/r┬▓` term.
- **Stress variation:** Hoop stress is **non-linear** in r тАФ it varies as `1/r┬▓`.
- **Thin-wall limit check:** When `b тЖТ a`, both ╧Г╬╕_max formulas should reduce to `pс╡в┬╖D/(2t)` (Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder).
- **Design implication:** Since ╧Г╬╕_max occurs at the bore for internal pressure, **bores are crack initiation sites** тЖТ autofrettage deliberately induces beneficial compressive residual stresses at the bore.
- **Verification habit:** Always substitute r = a and r = b into your final equations and confirm the boundary conditions.
- **Limitation of Lam├й theory:** Does not handle **plastic deformation**, **thermal stresses**, or **creep** тАФ those need Tresca, von Mises, or viscoelastic models.
- **KTU exam tip:** For a 14-mark question, allocate ~7 marks to derivation and ~7 marks to numerical application. Always **draw the FBD** of the wedge element for full marks.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrink Fit Assembly (Built-up Cylinder):** A composite pressure vessel constructed by mechanically joining two or more concentric cylinders through thermal interference, where the outer cylinder is heated (to expand), the inner cylinder is cooled (to contract), and upon temperature equalization, the resulting differential contraction generates a **residual contact pressure (P_c)** at the cylindrical interface. This pre-stressing mechanism shifts the maximum tangential stress from the inner surface to the interface, significantly increasing the vessel's pressure-carrying capacity.

### Geometric Nomenclature
| Symbol | Geometric Parameter | Typical Units |
|:------:|:-------------------|:-------------:|
| $a$ | Inner radius of inner cylinder | **mm** |
| $b$ | Common interface radius (outer of inner, inner of outer) | **mm** |
| $c$ | Outer radius of outer cylinder | **mm** |
| $\delta$ | Radial interference (interference at common radius) | **mm** |
| $P_c$ | Contact (interface) pressure | **MPa** |
| $P_i$ | Internal operating pressure | **MPa** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy тАФ The "Tightening Grip" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine gripping a metal rod inside a slightly smaller metal ring. You cannot push the rod in by hand. However, if you **heat the ring** (it expands) and **chill the rod in ice** (it shrinks), the rod slips in easily. As both return to room temperature, the ring squeezes the rod with enormous radial forceтАФthis squeezing force is the **contact pressure $P_c$**. The rod is now in **compression** and the ring is in **tension**, even before any internal pressure is applied. This is exactly the principle used in cannon barrels, hydraulic cylinder liners, autoclaves, and gun barrels.

### Physical Constants Referenced
- Young's Modulus: $E \approx 200$ **GPa** for carbon steel
- Poisson's Ratio: $\nu \approx 0.3$ for most engineering metals

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Shrink Fit

The mechanism operates in a **two-stage stress superposition**:

1. **Stage 1 (Residual / Assembly Stresses):** Pure interference fit produces tangential compression ($\sigma_\theta < 0$) in the inner cylinder and tangential tension ($\sigma_\theta > 0$) in the outer cylinder at the interface.

2. **Stage 2 (Operating Stresses):** When internal pressure $P_i$ is applied, these stresses **superimpose algebraically** on the Stage 1 stresses. The result is a flatter, more uniform tangential stress distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use shrink fit?** A monolithic (single-piece) thick cylinder experiences a **stress concentration** at the inner surface where $\sigma_\theta$ is maximum. A built-up cylinder with the correct interference redistributes this stress, pushing the maximum hoop stress **inward toward the interface**, thus exploiting material capacity more uniformly.

## 2.2 Assumptions for Analysis

- Cylinders are **long** тЖТ **Plane Strain** conditions apply (closed-end assumption: $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta)$).
- Material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys Hooke's Law.
- No body forces (gravity neglected).
- Contact is **uniform** along the cylindrical interface (no eccentricity).
- The interface contact pressure $P_c$ acts normal to the surface, equal and opposite on both cylinders.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet тАФ Shrink Fit Stresses

| # | Quantity | Formula (Plane Strain, Long Cylinder) | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:------------------------------------|:------|
| 1 | Lame's radial stress (general) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{P_i a^2 - P_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2}$ | MPa |
| 2 | Lame's hoop stress (general) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{P_i a^2 - P_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2}$ | MPa |
| 3 | Inner cyl. radial displacement at $r = b$ (external $P_c$) | $u_b^{(i)} = \dfrac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\dfrac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]$ | mm |
| 4 | Outer cyl. radial displacement at $r = b$ (internal $P_c$) | $u_b^{(o)} = \dfrac{b P_c}{E}\left[\dfrac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]$ | mm |
| 5 | **Contact pressure $P_c$ (pure shrink fit)** | $P_c = \dfrac{\delta E}{b} \left[\dfrac{b^2 - a^2}{a^2(1+\nu)+b^2(1-\nu)} + \dfrac{c^2 - b^2}{b^2(1-\nu)+c^2(1+\nu)}\right]^{-1}$ | MPa |
| 6 | Diametral interference | $\Delta = 2\delta$ | mm |
| 7 | Effective modulus (plane strain) | $E' = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}$ ; $\nu' = \dfrac{\nu}{1-\nu}$ | GPa |
| 8 | Combined (Stage 1 + Stage 2) hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta^{total} = \sigma_\theta^{shrink} + \sigma_\theta^{pressure}$ | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** Compressive stresses are **negative**; tensile stresses are **positive**. Radial stress at a free surface is always zero. When checking yielding, use $|\sigma_\theta|$ for magnitude and apply von Mises or Tresca criteria.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gun barrels and cannons** тАФ Pre-compresses the inner liner to resist bursting from propellant gases.
- **High-pressure autoclaves** тАФ Multi-layer shrink-fit construction allows vessel diameters up to 2+ meters.
- **Hydraulic cylinder liners** тАФ Hard-chrome inner liner shrink-fitted into a ductile outer sleeve.
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** тАФ Forging limitations are overcome by multilayer shrink-fit construction.
- **Concrete reinforcement analogy** тАФ Like pre-stressed concrete, the inner member is placed in beneficial compression.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case A тАФ Pure Shrink Fit (No Internal Pressure, $P_i = 0$)

### 3.1.1 Stage 1a: Stresses in the Inner Cylinder (loaded by external pressure $P_c$)

**Setup:** Inner cylinder is treated as a thick cylinder with:
- Inner radius: $a$
- Outer radius: $b$
- Internal pressure: $P_i = 0$
- External pressure: $P_o = P_c$ (the contact pressure from the outer cylinder squeezing inward)

**Step 1 тАФ Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_r$:**

Substitute $P_i = 0$, $P_o = P_c$ into the general Lame's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{(0)\cdot a^2 - P_c \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(0 - P_c)\cdot a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(-P_c)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{P_c\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_r^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[\frac{a^2}{r^2} - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The first term is a uniform (axisymmetric) compression; the second term decays with $r^2$ and provides the radial variation.

**Step 2 тАФ Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{(0)\cdot a^2 - P_c \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(0 - P_c)\cdot a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{P_c\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(r) &= -\frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Since $P_c$ is compressive on the outside, the entire inner cylinder is in tangential compression. At the inner surface ($r = a$): $\sigma_\theta = -P_c(a^2 + b^2)/(b^2 - a^2)$.

**Step 3 тАФ Radial displacement of inner cylinder at $r = b$:**

Using the plane-strain displacement relation $u(r) = \dfrac{r}{E}[\sigma_\theta - \nu\,\sigma_r]$:

Evaluate stresses at $r = b$:
- $\sigma_r^{(i)}(b) = -P_c$ (boundary condition check тЬУ)
- $\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(b) = -\dfrac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_b^{(i)} &= \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(i)}(b)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[-\frac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} - \nu(-P_c)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[-\frac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} + \nu P_c\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2 + b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \nu\right] \\[6pt]
u_b^{(i)} &= \frac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The negative sign indicates the inner cylinder's outer surface moves **inward** (radially) under the squeezing action of the outer cylinder.

---

### 3.1.2 Stage 1b: Stresses in the Outer Cylinder (loaded by internal pressure $P_c$)

**Setup:** Outer cylinder is treated as a thick cylinder with:
- Inner radius: $b$
- Outer radius: $c$
- Internal pressure: $P_i = P_c$ (the contact pressure pushing outward on the inner surface of the outer cylinder)
- External pressure: $P_o = 0$ (free outer surface)

**Step 4 тАФ Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_r$:**

Substitute $P_i = P_c$, $P_o = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c \cdot b^2 - 0}{c^2 - b^2} - \frac{(P_c - 0)\cdot b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2} - \frac{P_c\, b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_r^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 - \frac{c^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{P_c\, b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 + \frac{c^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The outer cylinder is in **tangential tension** at the interface, with maximum tensile hoop stress at $r = b$ equal to $P_c(b^2 + c^2)/(c^2 - b^2)$.

**Step 6 тАФ Radial displacement of outer cylinder at $r = b$:**

Evaluate stresses at $r = b$:
- $\sigma_r^{(o)}(b) = -P_c$ (matches the inner cylinder тАФ Newton's 3rd law тЬУ)
- $\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) = \dfrac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_b^{(o)} &= \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(o)}(b)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[\frac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2} - \nu(-P_c)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2 + c^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \nu\right] \\[6pt]
u_b^{(o)} &= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Positive value indicates the outer cylinder's inner surface moves **outward** (radially) due to internal pressurization from the contact force.

---

### 3.1.3 Compatibility Equation тАФ Solving for $P_c$

**Step 7 тАФ Geometric compatibility at the interface:**

The total radial interference $\delta$ must equal the difference in radial displacements of the two mating surfaces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= u_b^{(o)} - \left(-u_b^{(i)}\right) = u_b^{(o)} + \left\vert u_b^{(i)} \right\vert \\[6pt]
\delta &= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right] + \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 тАФ Factor out $\dfrac{b P_c}{E}$:**

$$
\delta = \frac{b P_c}{E}\left\{\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right\}
$$

**Step 9 тАФ Solve for $P_c$:**

$$
\boxed{P_c = \frac{\delta\, E}{b}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]^{-1}}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the **residual contact pressure** produced purely by the mechanical shrink fit. It is independent of any internal operating pressure.

---

## 3.2 Case B тАФ Combined Shrink Fit and Internal Pressure $P_i$

### Superposition Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Superposition:** Total stress = (Stresses due to shrink fit alone) + (Stresses due to internal $P_i$ on a single equivalent cylinder).

**Step 10 тАФ Stage 2 stress in the inner cylinder due to $P_i$ alone (treated as a thick cylinder with $a$ to $b$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{P_i}(r) &= \frac{P_i\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right] \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{P_i}(r) &= \frac{P_i\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 тАФ Stage 2 stress in the outer cylinder due to $P_c$ (at interface, $P_i = 0$ external):**

This is identical to the shrink-fit-only result derived in Step 4тАУ5. However, here $P_c$ is **not** solely due to interference but is determined by the simultaneous action of $P_i$.

**Step 12 тАФ Revised compatibility (radial displacements must match at $r = b$):**

Inner cylinder now sees internal $P_i$ and external $P_c$ simultaneously:

$$
u_b^{(i,\,total)} = \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(i,total)}(b)\right]
$$

where:
- $\sigma_r^{(i,total)}(b) = -P_c$
- $\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) = \dfrac{P_i\, a^2 - P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_c)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)b^2} = \dfrac{P_i\, a^2 - P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_c)\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$

Simplifying:
$$
\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) = \frac{P_i\, a^2(1 + b^2/b^2) - P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2 P_i\, a^2 - P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 13 тАФ Final form of compatibility equation for combined loading:**

$$
\boxed{\delta = \frac{b}{E}\left[P_c\left(\frac{a^2(1+\nu)+b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2-a^2}+\frac{b^2(1-\nu)+c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2-b^2}\right) - P_i\left(\frac{2 a^2(1-\nu)+b^2(1+\nu) - 2a^2}{b^2-a^2}\right)\right]}
$$

Solving this for $P_c$ yields the **operating contact pressure**, which is **lower** than the assembly contact pressure (since the internal pressure pushes outward, partially relieving the interface compression).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Loading Stage | Inner Cylinder Loading | Outer Cylinder Loading | Interface Pressure |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Pre-pressurization (Assembly)** | External $P_c$ only | Internal $P_c$ only | $P_c = P_c^{assembly}$ (maximum) |
| **Operating (Internal $P_i$ applied)** | Internal $P_i$ + External $P_c$ | Internal $P_c$ only | $P_c = P_c^{operating} < P_c^{assembly}$ |
| **Net Effect on $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$** | Increases (tensile) | тАФ | Reduced from monolithic peak |
| **Net Effect on $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = b$** | Decreases (compressive residual reduced) | Increases (tensile residual reduced) | Redistributes stress |

```mermaid
    F1["Residual Contact Pressure P_c acts radially inward on inner cylinder at r=b"] -.->|squeezes| R2

    L1["Stage 1: Shrink fit creates compressive sigma_theta in inner cylinder"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Shrink Fit Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C --> D["STEP 4: Cool inner cylinder below ambient (optional)"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: Slide inner cylinder into outer cylinder with care for alignment"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: Allow temperature to equalize at room temperature"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: Outer cylinder contracts and inner cylinder expands, creating interference fit"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]**
**Define "shrink fit" as applied to built-up pressure vessels. State two engineering advantages of using a shrink-fit construction over a monolithic cylinder.**

**Model Answer:**

A shrink fit is a method of assembling two or more concentric cylinders by creating a deliberate **radial interference** ($\delta$) at the common interface. This is achieved by heating the outer cylinder (causing thermal expansion), sliding the inner cylinder into position, and allowing temperature equalization. Upon cooling, the outer cylinder contracts onto the inner cylinder, generating a **residual contact pressure $P_c$** at the interface.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Stress redistribution:** The maximum tangential (hoop) stress in a monolithic cylinder occurs at the inner surface. A shrink fit introduces pre-compression in the inner cylinder, which **partially cancels** the tensile stress produced by internal pressure, thereby increasing the burst pressure capacity.
2. **Manufacturing feasibility:** Very large-diameter thick-walled vessels (e.g., for hydrocrackers, nuclear reactors) cannot be forged as a single piece. Shrink-fit construction allows layered assembly, overcoming forging size limitations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**
**Write down Lame's equations for radial and tangential (hoop) stresses in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$. Define all terms.**

**Model Answer:**

For a thick-walled cylinder with inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{P_i\, a^2 - P_o\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(P_i - P_o)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{P_i\, a^2 - P_o\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(P_i - P_o)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

**Where:**
- $\sigma_r$ = radial stress at radius $r$
- $\sigma_\theta$ = tangential (hoop) stress at radius $r$
- $P_i$ = internal pressure, $P_o$ = external pressure
- $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius
- $r$ = radial coordinate where stress is evaluated

**Boundary conditions:** At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = -P_i$ (if $P_i$ is the applied internal pressure, by sign convention); at $r = b$: $\sigma_r = -P_o$.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A two-layer shrink-fit cylinder has the following dimensions: inner radius $a = 100$ **mm**, interface radius $b = 200$ **mm**, outer radius $c = 300$ **mm**. The radial interference is $\delta = 0.10$ **mm**. Calculate the **contact pressure $P_c$** generated by the shrink fit. Assume $E = 200$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.3$. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** For the cylinder in part (a), determine the **maximum tangential stress in the inner cylinder** and the **maximum tangential stress in the outer cylinder** due to the shrink fit alone. Comment on which cylinder is more critical. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $a = 100$ **mm**, $b = 200$ **mm**, $c = 300$ **mm**
- $\delta = 0.10$ **mm**
- $E = 200 \times 10^3$ **MPa** = $200\,000$ **N/mm┬▓** (since 1 GPa = 1000 N/mm┬▓ = 1000 MPa)
- $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1 тАФ Compute the two bracket terms:**

Inner cylinder term:
$$
T_1 = \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{(100)^2(1.3) + (200)^2(0.7)}{(200)^2 - (100)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10\,000 \times 1.3 + 40\,000 \times 0.7}{40\,000 - 10\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{13\,000 + 28\,000}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{41\,000}{30\,000} = 1.3667
\end{aligned}
$$

Outer cylinder term:
$$
T_2 = \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= \frac{(200)^2(0.7) + (300)^2(1.3)}{(300)^2 - (200)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{40\,000 \times 0.7 + 90\,000 \times 1.3}{90\,000 - 40\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{28\,000 + 117\,000}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{145\,000}{50\,000} = 2.9000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Apply the contact pressure formula:**

$$
P_c = \frac{\delta\, E}{b\, (T_1 + T_2)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{0.10 \times 200\,000}{200 \times (1.3667 + 2.9000)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20\,000}{200 \times 4.2667} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20\,000}{853.33} \\[4pt]
P_c &= 23.4375 \text{ MPa} \approx 23.44 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{P_c \approx 23.44 \text{ MPa}}$

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (b):**

**Maximum hoop stress in the inner cylinder (occurs at $r = a$):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(a) = -\frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{a^2}{a^2}\right] = -\frac{2 P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(a) &= -\frac{2 \times 23.44 \times (200)^2}{(200)^2 - (100)^2} \\[4pt]
&= -\frac{2 \times 23.44 \times 40\,000}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= -\frac{1\,875\,200}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= -62.51 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum hoop stress in the outer cylinder (occurs at $r = b$):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) = \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 + \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right] = \frac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) &= \frac{23.44 \times ((200)^2 + (300)^2)}{(300)^2 - (200)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{23.44 \times (40\,000 + 90\,000)}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{23.44 \times 130\,000}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3\,047\,200}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= 60.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b):**
- Inner cylinder: $|\sigma_\theta^{(i)}|_{max} = 62.51$ **MPa** (compressive) at $r = a$
- Outer cylinder: $|\sigma_\theta^{(o)}|_{max} = 60.94$ **MPa** (tensile) at $r = b$

**Comment:** Although the magnitudes are comparable, the **outer cylinder is more critical** because:
1. Its stress is **tensile** (the inner cylinder's is compressive, which is safer).
2. The **interface stress** of 60.94 MPa governs design, since failure (yielding) typically initiates at the highest tensile location.
3. Under operating pressure, the tensile stress in the outer cylinder will further **increase**, while the compressive stress in the inner cylinder will be partially relieved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** A built-up cylinder consists of an inner liner (inner radius $a = 50$ **mm**, outer radius $b = 150$ **mm**) shrink-fitted into an outer shell (inner radius $b = 150$ **mm**, outer radius $c = 250$ **mm**). The radial interference is $\delta = 0.05$ **mm**. The assembly is subjected to an internal pressure of $P_i = 50$ **MPa**. Compute the **operating contact pressure** $P_c$ at the interface. Use $E = 200$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.3$. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** If the same cylinder were made as a **monolithic** (single-piece) cylinder with the same dimensions ($a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 250$ **mm**), calculate the **maximum hoop stress** at the inner surface for the same internal pressure. Compare and comment on the **efficiency of the built-up construction**. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 150$ **mm**, $c = 250$ **mm**
- $\delta = 0.05$ **mm**, $P_i = 50$ **MPa**
- $E = 200\,000$ **MPa**, $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1 тАФ Compute coefficients $T_1$ and $T_2$ (same as before):**

$$
T_1 = \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2500(1.3) + 22500(0.7)}{22500 - 2500} = \frac{3250 + 15750}{20000} = \frac{19000}{20000} = 0.95
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2} = \frac{22500(0.7) + 62500(1.3)}{62500 - 22500} = \frac{15750 + 81250}{40000} = \frac{97000}{40000} = 2.425
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Write the combined compatibility equation:**

$$
\delta = \frac{b P_c}{E}(T_1 + T_2) - \frac{b P_i}{E}\left[\frac{2a^2}{b^2-a^2}\right]
$$

(The second term accounts for the radial expansion of the inner cylinder's outer surface due to $P_i$, which partially relieves the interference.)

**Step 3 тАФ Compute the second term coefficient:**

$$
K = \frac{2a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2 \times 2500}{22500 - 2500} = \frac{5000}{20000} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Solve for $P_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{b}{E}\left[P_c(T_1 + T_2) - P_i\, K\right] \\[4pt]
0.05 &= \frac{150}{200\,000}\left[P_c(0.95 + 2.425) - 50 \times 0.25\right] \\[4pt]
0.05 &= 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\left[3.375 P_c - 12.5\right] \\[4pt]
\frac{0.05}{7.5 \times 10^{-4}} &= 3.375 P_c - 12.5 \\[4pt]
66.667 &= 3.375 P_c - 12.5 \\[4pt]
3.375 P_c &= 79.167 \\[4pt]
P_c &= 23.46 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{P_c^{operating} \approx 23.46 \text{ MPa}}$

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (b):**

**Monolithic cylinder:** $a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 250$ **mm**, $P_i = 50$ **MPa**, $P_o = 0$.

**Maximum hoop stress occurs at $r = a$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{max} = \frac{P_i(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{max} &= \frac{50 \times (2500 + 62500)}{62500 - 2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50 \times 65000}{60000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3\,250\,000}{60\,000} \\[4pt]
&= 54.17 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison and Comment:**

| Configuration | $\sigma_\theta$ at Inner Surface | Burst Pressure Capacity |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **Monolithic** | +54.17 MPa (pure tensile) | Limited by inner surface |
| **Built-up with $P_c \approx 23.46$ MPa** | Net = $54.17 - $ (compressive component from $P_c$) | Significantly higher |

For the built-up cylinder, the **net inner surface hoop stress** is the superposition:
- Tensile from $P_i$: $+54.17$ **MPa**
- Compressive from $P_c$: $-P_c \times 2b^2/(b^2-a^2) = -23.46 \times 4.5 = -105.57$ **MPa**

**Net $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$ for built-up cylinder:**
$$
\sigma_\theta^{net}(a) = 54.17 - 105.57 = -51.40 \text{ MPa (still compressive)}
$$

**Comment:** The built-up cylinder keeps the **inner surface in compression** even under full operating pressure. This dramatically delays yielding and raises the **burst pressure** by approximately **60тАУ80%** over the monolithic design. This is the key engineering benefit of shrink-fit construction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in shrink-fit problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in displacement equations:** Forgetting that the inner cylinder's outer surface moves **inward** (negative radial displacement), so the total interference is the **algebraic sum** of the two displacements. Marks lost: 2тАУ3 per part.
>
> 2. **Mixing up inner and outer cylinder terms:** Students sometimes apply the wrong sign for $(1+\nu)$ and $(1-\nu)$ in $T_1$ vs. $T_2$. **Memory aid:** The term with the **interface radius $b$** appears in **both** $T_1$ and $T_2$. In $T_1$, $b$ carries $(1-\nu)$; in $T_2$, $b$ carries $(1-\nu)$. Verify by checking dimensions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** Mixing **mm** with **GPa** is a guaranteed zero. Always use **N/mm┬▓ = MPa** consistently, or convert everything to SI (Pa, m).
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you do not state "plane strain," "long cylinder," "no body forces," etc.
>
> 5. **Confusing diametral vs. radial interference:** If a problem gives "diametral interference $\Delta$", the radial interference is $\delta = \Delta/2$. Marks lost: 2.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Poisson's effect:** Setting $\nu = 0$ gives a 5тАУ10% error in $P_c$, which is unacceptable for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist тАФ Shrink Fit in Built-up Cylinders**

- **Definition:** Shrink fit = two cylinders joined by thermal interference generating residual contact pressure $P_c$ at the common interface.
- **Geometry:** $a$ (inner) $< b$ (interface) $< c$ (outer). Three radii define the system.
- **Lame's Equations:** Foundation of thick-cylinder analysis. Use with correct sign convention.
- **Sign Convention:** $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_\theta$ tensile = positive; compressive = negative.
- **Contact pressure $P_c$ (pure shrink fit):** Function of $\delta$, $E$, $\nu$, and all three radii $a, b, c$.
- **Compatibility equation:** $\delta = u_b^{(o)} + \vert u_b^{(i)} \vert$ (the sum of outward expansion of outer cylinder's inner surface and inward contraction of inner cylinder's outer surface).
- **Inner cylinder stress state (shrink fit only):** Tangential **compression** throughout; maximum compressive $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$.
- **Outer cylinder stress state (shrink fit only):** Tangential **tension** throughout; maximum tensile $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = b$.
- **Operating contact pressure < Assembly contact pressure** because $P_i$ partially relieves interface compression.
- **Superposition principle:** Total stress = Shrink fit stress + Pressure-induced stress.
- **Design benefit:** Built-up cylinders exhibit **higher burst pressure** (typically 50тАУ80% increase) and **more uniform stress distribution** than monolithic cylinders of equivalent size.
- **Critical surfaces for design check:**
  - Inner cylinder: $r = a$ (during operation, maximum tensile stress).
  - Outer cylinder: $r = b$ (interface, maximum tensile residual stress).
- **Plane strain assumption** is standard for long cylinders; replace $E \to E/(1-\nu^2)$ and $\nu \to \nu/(1-\nu)$ if using plane-stress formulas.
- **Standard units:** $E$ in **MPa** (= N/mm┬▓) when radii are in mm; $\delta$ in mm; $P_c$ in MPa.
- **Memory aid for $T_1$ and $T_2$:** The term with the **squared radius that is NOT the interface** carries the $(1+\nu)$ factor; the term with the **interface radius** carries $(1-\nu)$. (Inner cylinder: $a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)$; Outer cylinder: $b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)$.)
- **Key advantage to remember for viva:** Pre-stressing shifts the maximum hoop stress **away from the bore**, enabling higher operating pressures than monolithic design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Reinforced Opening (Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Reinforcement Analysis)
Design of Tall Vessel under Wind and Seismic Load. Design of Leg, Lug and Saddle Supports. Buckling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Tall Vessel under Wind and Seismic Load. Design of Leg, Lug and Saddle Supports. Buckling

# MODULE 3: Design of Tall Vessels, Supports, and Buckling Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Tall Vessel"?
A pressure vessel is classified as a **Tall Vessel** when its height (or tangent length) is large enough that environmental loads тАФ particularly **wind** and **earthquake (seismic)** тАФ produce bending moments and shears comparable to (or greater than) the stresses caused by internal pressure. The classical industry rule is:

$$\frac{H}{D_i} \geq 10 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{L}{D_i} \geq 5$$

where $H$ is the total height, $L$ is the tangent length, and $D_i$ is the inside diameter. By this rule, a distillation column, a catalytic reactor stack, or a regenerator is virtually always a "tall vessel."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET862 / Module 3):**
> A tall vessel is a vertical pressure vessel whose structural design is governed by the combined action of internal pressure, **weight**, **wind**, and **seismic** loads, requiring explicit calculation of **overturning moment**, **base shear**, and **maximum compressive longitudinal stress** at the windward (and leeward) meridian. Supports тАФ legs, lugs, or saddles тАФ are designed to transmit these loads safely to the foundation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Palm Tree and the Skyscraper"
Imagine a **palm tree** standing in a storm. Three forces act on it: the wind pushing it sideways (lateral), gravity pulling it down, and the soil pushing it back at the roots (reaction). A tall pressure vessel behaves in **exactly** the same way:
- **Wind / Earthquake** = the storm (lateral load).
- **Weight of vessel + contents** = gravity (axial compression).
- **Anchor bolts / base ring** = the soil (reaction at supports).

The "bending stress" in the trunk of the tree corresponds to the **bending stress in the shell** of the vessel. If the tree is too slender, it **buckles** тАФ that is the connection between tall-vessel design and **buckling analysis**.

### 1.3 Types of Supports Studied in This Module
| Support Type | Typical Use | Load Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| **Leg Support** (cylindrical/conical) | Small-to-medium tall vessels, $D \leq 1.5$ m | Axial compression тЖТ concrete pedestal |
| **Lug Support** (plate lug + gusset) | Horizontal vessels on columns; vertical vessels braced to steel | Shear & bending тЖТ structural steel |
| **Saddle Support** | Horizontal vessels only (not for vertical) | Two-line contact тЖТ vessel shell |

### 1.4 Buckling тАФ A First Look
Buckling is **sudden, catastrophic lateral failure** of a structural member under compression, *not* by crushing of material but by **loss of geometric stability**. For pressure vessels, three types are critical:

1. **Column buckling** of vessel shell (treated as a cantilever).
2. **Shell buckling under external pressure** (vacuum).
3. **Local buckling** of stiffeners, conical transitions, and tori-spherical heads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEMET862 explicitly links wind/seismic loads to **combined longitudinal stress** in the shell, the design of **leg / lug / saddle** supports, and the **buckling** checks mandated by ASME Section VIII Div. 1 (UG-28, UG-54) and IS 2825.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wind Load on a Tall Vessel (IS 875 Part 3, 2015)

A tall vessel is treated as a **cylindrical structure** with one or more attachments (nozzles, platforms, ladders). The wind pressure is *not* uniform тАФ it increases with height (the "wind gradient"). IS 875 Part 3 (2015) prescribes:

**Step 1 тАФ Basic Wind Speed $V_b$**
A zone-specific value (33тАУ50 m/s for Kerala) read from the IS 875 map.

**Step 2 тАФ Design Wind Speed $V_z$ at height $z$**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

where
- $k_1$ = risk coefficient (1.0 for normal buildings; 1.08 for important structures).
- $k_2$ = terrain, height & structure size factor (1.0тАУ1.36).
- $k_3$ = topography factor (1.0 for flat).
- $k_4$ = importance factor for cyclonic region.

**Step 3 тАФ Design Wind Pressure**

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^{\,2} \quad \left[\text{N/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 4 тАФ Wind Force on a Section**

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$

where $C_f$ is the **force coefficient** (тЙИ 0.7 for a smooth cylinder with $V_z D \leq 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$; up to 1.2 for $V_z D \geq 45 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ тАФ IS 875 Table 10), and $A_e$ is the effective frontal area.

**Step 5 тАФ Overturning Moment**
The vessel is divided into $n$ horizontal strips; the moment about the base is:

$$M_{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot h_i$$

where $F_i$ is the wind force on strip $i$ and $h_i$ is the centroid height of that strip above the base.

### 2.2 Seismic Load (IS 1893 Part 4, 2015 / Equivalent Static Method)

For industrial structures (which includes tall vessels), the **equivalent static method** is acceptable for $H \leq 40$ m. The **design horizontal seismic coefficient** is:

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | Zone factor (Kerala = III) | **0.16** |
| $I$ | Importance factor | 1.0 / 1.5 |
| $R$ | Response reduction factor (ductile vessel) | **3.0 тАУ 4.0** |
| $S_a/g$ | Spectral acceleration coefficient (from IS 1893 spectra) | 1.0 тАУ 2.5 |

The total **base shear**:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W_e$$

where $W_e$ is the **seismic weight** = dead weight + 25 % of full live load (for vessels: weight of empty vessel + 100 % of operating weight of contents + 25 % of insulation).

The **base moment**:

$$M_B = V_B \cdot \bar{h}$$

where $\bar{h}$ is the height of the center of mass above the base (тЙИ $0.6 H$ to $0.7 H$ for a uniformly distributed mass).

### 2.3 Combined Longitudinal Stress (Critical for KTU)

For a vertical vessel the **maximum longitudinal compressive stress** at the windward side (or leeward, depending on the wind direction) is:

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{PL} + \sigma_{BL} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}}$$

with

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r (D_i + 0.6 t)}{4 t E_w} \quad \text{(from UG-23 internal pressure; corrected for joint efficiency)}$$

Wait тАФ the *longitudinal* stress from internal pressure in a *vertical* vessel (where pressure is axial) is:

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r \cdot D_i}{4 t \cdot E_w}$$

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{4 M_{\max}}{\pi D_i^{\,2} t} \quad \text{(bending тАФ for thin shells)}$$

For *thick* shells or for rigorous analysis the ASME Code uses the section-modulus of the ring (UG-54). For Module 3 the thin-shell form is acceptable when $t/D_i \leq 0.05$.

The **allowable** (ASME VIII-1, UG-23 combined) is:

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 1.0 \cdot S E \quad \text{when wind/seismic is not the governing load}$$
$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 1.2 \cdot S E \quad \text{when wind/seismic governs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 1.2 factor?** The ASME Code permits a 20 % overstress for "occasional" loads such as wind and earthquake because their probability of simultaneous occurrence with maximum pressure is small тАФ this is a classic *load combination* concept.

### 2.4 Leg Support тАФ Design Procedure
Legs are **compression members**, often I-sections or pipe sections welded to a base ring on the vessel bottom.

**Axial load per leg:**

$$P = \frac{W}{N}$$

**Bending moment per leg** (eccentricity of vertical load from column centroid):

$$M_{leg} = \frac{M_{\text{wind}}}{N} + P \cdot e$$

**Compressive stress in leg:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A_g} + \frac{M_{leg}}{Z_g}$$

**Slenderness check (IS 800 / AISC):**

$$\lambda = \frac{K L}{r_{\min}} \leq 200 \quad \text{(for bracing members)}$$

**Column buckling stress (IS 800 тАФ secant / Perry-Robertson):**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{f_y}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_e^{\,2}}}$$

where $\phi = 0.5 \left[1 + \alpha (\lambda_e - 0.2) + \lambda_e^{\,2}\right]$ and $\lambda_e = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cc}}$.

### 2.5 Lug Support тАФ Design Procedure
A **lug plate** carries **shear** $V$ and **tension/compression** $P$ from a horizontal vessel or a vertical vessel braced to a column. The critical checks are:

| Check | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Bearing on lug plate** | $\sigma_b = V / (d \cdot t_{lug}) \leq 0.9 S_y$ |
| **Shear in pin** | $\tau = 4V / (\pi d^2) \leq 0.4 S_y$ |
| **Bending of lug plate** | $\sigma = 1.5 V L / (t \cdot b^2) \leq 0.66 S_y$ |
| **Fillet weld to vessel** | $t_w = V / (0.707 \cdot S_{weld} \cdot 0.707 \cdot L_w)$ (E70XX, $S_{weld}$=320 MPa design) |
| **Tear-out (block shear)** | per AISC J4 |

### 2.6 Saddle Support тАФ Zick's Analysis (Horizontal Vessels)
The classic Zick (1956) method for a horizontal vessel on **two saddles** gives:

**Longitudinal bending stress (at saddle horn, top of shell):**

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \cdot \pi R^2 t}$$

**Longitudinal stress at bottom (between saddles):**

$$\sigma_2 = +\frac{M_C}{K_2 \cdot \pi R^2 t}$$

**Tangential shear at saddle horn:**

$$\tau = \frac{K_3 \cdot V}{R t}$$

**Circumferential bending at saddle:**

$$\sigma_3 = -\frac{K_4 \cdot V}{A} \leq 0.5 S$

**Stress intensification factor** at the saddle (K-factor from Zick chart) and the **stiffener ring** design (if $L/R > 8$).

### 2.7 Buckling тАФ The Three Critical Checks

**(a) Column Buckling of Tall Vessel (treated as cantilever)**
Using Euler's formula (for long, slender shells):

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K H)^2}$$

The vessel must satisfy $P_{\text{actual}} \leq P_{cr} / F.S.$

**(b) Shell Buckling under External Pressure (ASME VIII-1 UG-28)**

For a cylinder, the critical pressure is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D_o)^{2.5}}{(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}} \quad \text{(long cylinders)}$$

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D_o)^{1.5}}{\frac{L/D_o}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{1.0}{(L/D_o)^2}}}} \quad \text{(intermediate)}$$

The **allowable** is $p_a = p_{cr} / F.S.$ with $F.S. = 3.0$ for vessels.

**(c) Wind-Induced Buckling (HTRI / Brownell)**
For tall columns under wind:

$$\sigma_c + \sigma_{BL} \leq \sigma_{cr,\text{shell}}$$

where $\sigma_{cr,\text{shell}}$ is the elastic shell-buckling stress (Donnell-Batdorf):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{0.6 E \, t}{R}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design wind speed at $z$ | $V_z = V_b k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4$ | m/s |
| 2 | Wind pressure | $p_z = 0.6 V_z^{\,2}$ | Pa (N/m┬▓) |
| 3 | Wind force on strip | $F_i = C_f A_e p_z$ | N |
| 4 | Overturning moment | $M_w = \sum F_i h_i$ | N┬╖m |
| 5 | Seismic base shear | $V_B = A_h W_e$ | N |
| 6 | Seismic moment | $M_B = V_B \bar{h}$ | N┬╖m |
| 7 | Long. stress from pressure | $\sigma_{PL} = p_r D_i / (4 t E_w)$ | MPa |
| 8 | Bending stress in shell | $\sigma_{BL} = 4 M / (\pi D_i^{\,2} t)$ | MPa |
| 9 | Allowable combined | $1.2 S E$ (wind/eq governs) | MPa |
| 10 | Leg slenderness | $\lambda = KL/r_{\min} \leq 200$ | тАФ |
| 11 | Euler buckling load | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (KH)^2$ | N |
| 12 | External pressure (long cyl.) | $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5} / [L/D_o - 0.45(t/D_o)^{0.5}]$ | Pa |
| 13 | Shell buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr} = 0.6 E (t/R)$ | MPa |
| 14 | Saddle moment $M_A$ | $M_A = P L R (1 - 2a/L) / 4$ | N┬╖m |
| 15 | Lug bending | $\sigma = 1.5 V L / (t b^2)$ | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** This design workflow is used in refinery columns (Indian Oil, BPCL), LNG storage tanks, ethylene cracking columns, and pharma reactors. The wind/seismic logic is also the backbone of *ASME STS-1* (Stainless Steel Chimney Liners) and *API 650* Annex E (Seismic Design of Storage Tanks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 тАФ Overturning Moment from Wind

Consider a tall vessel divided into $n$ horizontal strips. The total wind force on each strip is:

$$F_i = C_{f,i} \cdot (D_o \cdot \Delta h_i) \cdot p_{z,i}$$

**Step 1.** Identify the *centroid height* $h_i$ of each strip. For a strip from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$:

$$h_i = \frac{z_{i-1} + z_i}{2}$$

**Step 2.** Each strip's force creates a moment about the base. The total overturning moment is the *sum* (since moments are vector quantities about a common point):

$$M_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot h_i$$

**Step 3.** Substituting the wind force:

$$M_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{f,i} \cdot D_o \cdot \Delta h_i \cdot 0.6 \cdot V_{z,i}^{\,2} \cdot h_i$$

**Step 4.** For *practical* KTU calculations the vessel is often modeled as 4тАУ6 strips (e.g. 0тАУ5 m, 5тАУ10 m, 10тАУ20 m, 20тАУ30 m, 30тАУ40 m, 40 mтАУtop). The sum of each term gives the design moment.

**Step 5.** The *base shear* (horizontal force transmitted to foundation) is:

$$V_B^{(w)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 тАФ Maximum Longitudinal Compressive Stress

**Step 1.** The vessel shell is a thin cylinder; internal pressure produces a *membrane* stress along the axis:

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r \cdot r_i}{t} = \frac{p_r \cdot D_i}{2 t \cdot 2 \cdot E_w} \cdot 2 = \frac{p_r D_i}{4 t E_w}$$

Here $E_w$ is the *weld joint efficiency* (= 1.0 for full radiography, 0.85 for spot, 0.70 for none тАФ UG-23).

**Step 2.** Bending due to wind/earthquake produces a linearly varying stress across the diameter. For a circular cross-section treated as a thin ring, the *section modulus* is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{\pi R^3 t}{R} = \pi R^2 t = \frac{\pi D_i^{\,2} t}{4}$$

**Step 3.** The bending stress is:

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M}{\pi R^2 t} = \frac{4 M}{\pi D_i^{\,2} t}$$

**Step 4.** Combining them, the *most compressed fibre* (windward) carries:

$$\sigma_{L} = \sigma_{PL} + \sigma_{BL}$$

**Step 5.** Code allowable (UG-23, including the 1.2 overstress for occasional loads):

$$\sigma_L \leq 1.2 \cdot S \cdot E \quad \text{when wind or seismic dominates}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 тАФ Euler Buckling of a Cantilever Tall Vessel

A vertical vessel anchored at the base acts as a *cantilever* under axial compression. The effective length factor $K = 2.0$ for a fixed-free column, and the slenderness is:

$$\lambda = \frac{K H}{r}$$

where $r$ is the **radius of gyration** of the shell. For a thin cylindrical shell:

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi R^3 t}{\pi \cdot 2 R t}} = \frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$$

The Euler critical load is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K H)^2}$$

**Step 1.** Substitute $I = \pi R^3 t$ and $K = 2.0$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E \pi R^3 t}{(2H)^2} = \frac{\pi^3 E R^3 t}{4 H^2}$$

**Step 2.** Critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^3 E R^3 t}{4 H^2 \cdot 2 \pi R t} = \frac{\pi^2 E R^2}{8 H^2}$$

**Step 3.** Define a *buckling safety factor*:

$$\text{FS} = \frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_L} \geq 1.5 \quad \text{(typical KTU problem requirement)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** The $H^2$ in the denominator means that **doubling the height reduces the critical load by 4├Ч**. This is why tall-vessel design is dominated by the moment and buckling check, not by the pressure check.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 тАФ Shell Buckling under External Pressure (Long Cylinder)

For a *long* cylinder ($L > 10 D_o$), experimental and theoretical results (Von Mises) give:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \frac{(t/D_o)^{2.5}}{L/D_o - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}}$$

**Step 1.** For steels, $\nu = 0.3$, so $(1 - \nu^2) \approx 0.91$. The code form is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5}}{(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}}$$

**Step 2.** Code allowable (UG-28, factor of safety 3):

$$p_a = \frac{p_{cr}}{3.0}$$

**Step 3.** The design check requires:

$$p_{\text{ext}} \leq p_a$$

where $p_{\text{ext}}$ = external pressure (= 1 bar for vacuum, or = design external pressure).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 тАФ Zick's Saddle Longitudinal Stress (Top of Shell at Saddle)

For a horizontal vessel of radius $R$, total length $L$, on two saddles separated by distance $2a$ (i.e. one saddle at $a$ from each head), with weight $W$ uniformly distributed:

**Step 1.** Reaction at each saddle (vertical):

$$R = \frac{W}{2}$$

**Step 2.** Bending moment at the saddle (over the support) тАФ Zick's formula:

$$M_A = \frac{-W a}{2} \left(1 - \frac{a}{L}\right) = -\frac{W a (L - a)}{2 L}$$

**Step 3.** Bending moment at midspan (between saddles):

$$M_C = +\frac{W a^2}{2 L}$$

**Step 4.** Longitudinal stress at top of shell at the saddle (the *highest* compressive point):

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{12 M_A \cdot R}{t^2 (3 L_1 + 1.56 \sqrt{R t}) \cdot K_1}$$

For a thin shell (K-factor approach):

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \pi R^2 t}$$

with $K_1$ from the Zick chart (function of $L/R$ and $a/R$).

**Step 5.** Stress check:

$$\vert \sigma_1 \vert \leq 1.5 S \quad \text{(per UG-77 for local stress)}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem for KTU)

**Problem:** A vertical distillation column is $H = 30$ m tall with $D_i = 2.0$ m, $t = 25$ mm, $W_{\text{empty}} = 250$ kN, $W_{\text{operating}} = 1200$ kN. $V_b = 39$ m/s, Zone III, $k_2 = 1.10$, $k_1 = k_3 = k_4 = 1.0$, $C_f = 0.8$, $S = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $E_w = 1.0$. Design pressure = 1.0 MPa.

**Step 1 тАФ Wind load (one strip approximation, using average $V_z$).**
Take $V_z = 39 \times 1.10 = 42.9$ m/s; conservative uniform strip.

$$p_z = 0.6 \times 42.9^2 = 0.6 \times 1840 = 1104 \text{ Pa}$$

Wind force on shell (per unit length):

$$q = C_f \cdot D_o \cdot p_z = 0.8 \times 2.05 \times 1104 = 1811 \text{ N/m}$$

Overturning moment (uniform load, $q$):

$$M_w = \frac{q H^2}{2} = \frac{1811 \times 30^2}{2} = 815{,}000 \text{ N┬╖m} = 815 \text{ kN┬╖m}$$

Base shear:

$$V_B^{(w)} = q H = 1811 \times 30 = 54{,}330 \text{ N} = 54.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Seismic load (IS 1893).**
Take $A_h = 0.10$ (typical for Zone III, $R=3$, $I=1$, $S_a/g = 2.0$).

$$V_B^{(s)} = A_h W_e = 0.10 \times 1200 = 120 \text{ kN}$$

Seismic moment ($\bar{h} = 0.6 \times 30 = 18$ m):

$$M_s = 120 \times 18 = 2160 \text{ kN┬╖m}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Governing load (larger moment governs in this case, i.e. seismic).**
$M_{\max} = M_s = 2160$ kN┬╖m.

**Step 4 тАФ Bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{4 \times 2160 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (2000)^2 \times 25} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^9}{3.14 \times 10^9} = 2.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Pressure longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{1.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 25 \times 1.0} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Combined:**

$$\sigma_L = 20 + 2.75 = 22.75 \text{ MPa} \leq 1.2 \times 250 = 300 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ$$

**Step 7 тАФ Check column buckling.** $K=2$, $r = R/\sqrt{2} = 1025/\sqrt{2} = 725$ mm.

$$\lambda = \frac{2 \times 30{,}000}{725} = 82.7$$

$\sigma_{cr}$ (Euler):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{9.87 \times 2 \times 10^5}{82.7^2} = 288 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{FS} = \frac{288}{22.75} = 12.7 \;\; \text{(very safe)}$$

**Step 8 тАФ External pressure check (assume vacuum, $p_{\text{ext}} = 0.1$ MPa):**
$D_o = 2050$ mm, $L = 30{,}000$ mm, $L/D_o = 14.6$ тАФ long cylinder.

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (25/2050)^{2.5}}{14.6 - 0.45 (25/2050)^{0.5}} \approx \frac{5.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.86 \times 10^{-6}}{14.5} = 0.0666 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **less than 0.1 MPa vacuum** тЖТ a *stiffener ring* is mandatory! (Discussed in module 3 syllabus under buckling.)

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tall Vessel тАФ Free-Body Diagram of Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV["Environmental Loads"]
        W["Wind Force F_w (horizontal)"]
        E["Seismic V_B (horizontal)"]
    end

    subgraph VSL["Tall Vessel (H/D > 10)"]
        P["Internal Pressure p_r (axial & hoop)"]
        G["Weight W_op (axial)"]
        M["Bending Moment M = M_w or M_s"]
    end

    subgraph SUP["Supports"]
        L["Leg / Lug / Saddle"]
    end

    subgraph FOUN["Foundation"]
        AB["Anchor Bolts"]
        R["Reaction: N, V, M"]
    end

    W -->|produces| M
    E -->|produces| M
    P -->|longitudinal stress| VSL
    G -->|axial compression| VSL
    M -->|transmitted to| SUP
    VSL -->|load path| L
    L --> AB
    AB --> R
```

### 4.2 Design Workflow for Tall Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: Vessel dimensions D, H, t"] --> B["Estimate W_empty, W_op"]
    B --> C{"Compute V_z & p_z<br/>per IS 875"}
    C --> D["F_i = C_f A_e p_z"]
    D --> E["M_w = sum F_i h_i"]
    B --> F["A_h = ZI/R x Sa/g<br/>per IS 1893"]
    F --> G["V_B = A_h W_e"]
    G --> H["M_s = V_B x h_bar"]
    E --> I["M_max = max of M_w, M_s"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["sigma_PL = p_r D / 4tE"]
    I --> K["sigma_BL = 4M / pi D^2 t"]
    J --> L{"sigma_L = sigma_PL + sigma_BL<br/>is it <= 1.2 S E?"}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M["OK for combined stress"]
    L -->|No| N["Increase t or stiffen"]
    M --> O{"Column buckling FS >= 1.5?"}
    N --> O
    O -->|Yes| P["OK"]
    O -->|No| Q["Add stiffener ring or<br/>increase t"]
    Q --> R["Re-check p_cr (UG-28)"]
    P --> S["Design supports<br/>(leg, lug, or saddle)"]
    R --> S
    S --> T["End"]
```

### 4.6 Buckling-Check Flowchart (UG-28 / Module 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Given: D_o, t, L, E, nu, p_ext"] --> B["Compute t/D_o and L/D_o"]
    B --> C{"Is L > 10 D_o?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Use long-cylinder<br/>p_cr = 2.6E(t/D)^2.5<br/>/(L/D - 0.45(t/D)^0.5)"]
    C -->|No| E["Use intermediate formula"]
    D --> F["p_a = p_cr / 3.0"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"p_a >= p_ext?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Shell OK"]
    G -->|No| I["Add stiffener ring"]
    I --> J["Compute ring spacing<br/>L_eff = max(L_s, D_o)"]
    J --> K["Re-evaluate p_cr with L_eff"]
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** The KTU answer-sheet evaluator often awards marks for a clean *flow diagram* of the design process. The four diagrams above are the most commonly drawn (free-body, design flow, support schematic, buckling flow). A student reproducing these in the exam using boxes and arrows (or an equivalent neat sketch) secures 1тАУ2 extra marks per answer.
