## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A тАФ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2023, Model QP, Part A]**
Define a *tall vessel*. Why are wind and seismic loads critical for its design, while they can be ignored for a small horizontal pressure vessel?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A tall vessel is one whose height $H$ satisfies $H/D_i \geq 10$ or $L/D_i \geq 5$ (1 mark).
- For such a vessel, the lateral (wind/earthquake) load creates a bending moment $M$ at the base that produces a longitudinal bending stress $\sigma_{BL} = 4M/(\pi D_i^{\,2} t)$ comparable to the pressure-induced longitudinal stress $\sigma_{PL} = p_r D_i/(4 t E_w)$ (1 mark).
- For small horizontal vessels, the lateral load is supported by saddles and the vessel's axis of bending is horizontal, producing only localized stresses; hence the combined stress check is not critical (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2022, Model QP, Part A]**
State the formula for **shell buckling under external pressure** for a long cylinder. Why is the factor of safety taken as 3.0 in ASME Section VIII Div. 1?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Formula: $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5}}{(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}}$ (1 mark).
- The factor of safety 3.0 accounts for: (a) initial geometric imperfections (out-of-roundness, dents) which drastically reduce $p_{cr}$, (b) uncertainty in residual stresses from welding, and (c) statistical scatter in elastic modulus $E$ (1 mark).
- Therefore the allowable external pressure is $p_a = p_{cr}/3.0$ and the design requires $p_{\text{ext}} \leq p_a$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B тАФ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024, Model Pattern, Module 3]

**A.** *(a)* A vertical pressure vessel of total height $H = 28$ m and inside diameter $D_i = 1.8$ m has a wall thickness $t = 22$ mm. The vessel operates at internal pressure $p = 1.5$ MPa and is located in Kerala (Zone III, basic wind speed $V_b = 39$ m/s). Empty weight = 200 kN, operating weight = 950 kN, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $S = 250$ MPa, $E_w = 1.0$, $C_f = 0.85$, $k_1 = k_3 = k_4 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 1.08$. Compute the **wind overturning moment** and the **maximum combined longitudinal stress**. Check against the allowable. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Design wind speed at average height (use 1.08 factor):
$$V_z = 39 \times 1.08 = 42.12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2.** Wind pressure:
$$p_z = 0.6 \times 42.12^2 = 0.6 \times 1774 = 1064.4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3.** Wind force per unit length (treating as uniform strip тАФ conservative):
$$q = C_f \cdot D_o \cdot p_z = 0.85 \times 1.844 \times 1064.4 = 1668 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 4.** Overturning moment (uniform distribution):
$$M_w = \frac{q H^2}{2} = \frac{1668 \times 28^2}{2} = 653{,}856 \text{ N┬╖m} \approx 654 \text{ kN┬╖m}$$

**Step 5.** Base shear:
$$V_B = q H = 1668 \times 28 = 46{,}704 \text{ N} \approx 46.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6.** Longitudinal stress from pressure:
$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{1.5 \times 1800}{4 \times 22 \times 1.0} = 30.68 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7.** Bending stress:
$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{4 \times 653{,}856 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 1800^2 \times 22} = \frac{2.615 \times 10^9}{2.237 \times 10^8} = 11.69 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8.** Combined:
$$\sigma_L = 30.68 + 11.69 = 42.37 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9.** Allowable (wind governs, so 1.2 ├Ч S ├Ч E):
$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 1.2 \times 250 \times 1.0 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_L = 42.37 \text{ MPa} \;\; \ll 300 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ \; \text{SAFE}$$

**Conclusion:** Thickness is adequate. (If it were *not* adequate, increase $t$ or add a stiffener ring.)

---

*(b)* With the same vessel, check **column buckling** as a cantilever. Use $K = 2.0$ and a factor of safety of 1.5. The vessel shell is the only compression member. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Compute radius of gyration of shell:
$$R = \frac{D_o}{2} = \frac{1844}{2} = 922 \text{ mm}$$
$$r = \frac{R}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{922}{\sqrt{2}} = 652 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2.** Slenderness ratio (fixed-free, $K = 2$):
$$\lambda = \frac{KH}{r} = \frac{2 \times 28{,}000}{652} = 85.9$$

**Step 3.** Euler critical stress (assuming elastic buckling, since $\lambda > \lambda_c$):
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{9.87 \times 2 \times 10^5}{85.9^2} = \frac{1.974 \times 10^6}{7380} = 267.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4.** Allowable stress (with FS = 1.5):
$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{267.5}{1.5} = 178.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5.** Check actual compressive stress (axial + bending):
$$\sigma_{L} = 42.37 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{L} \leq 178.3 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ \; \text{SAFE (FS achieved} = 267.5/42.37 = 6.3)$$

**Conclusion:** Column buckling is safe; the actual FS = 6.3 is far higher than the required 1.5.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023, Model Pattern, Module 3]

**B.** *(a)* Design a **lug support** for a horizontal pressure vessel of outer diameter $D_o = 1200$ mm, weight $W = 80$ kN, supported on two columns spaced 4 m apart. The lug plate is 200 mm wide and 16 mm thick, made of E250 steel ($S_y = 250$ MPa), welded to the vessel shell with a fillet weld of leg 8 mm. Use SS400 electrode. Check **bearing, shear in pin, bending of plate, and weld strength**. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Load per lug (two lugs, one at each end):
$$V = \frac{W}{2} = 40 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2.** Pin diameter тАФ assume $d = 30$ mm, with pin of $S_y = 250$ MPa.

Shear in pin (double shear):
$$\tau = \frac{V}{2 \times (\pi d^2 / 4)} = \frac{40{,}000}{2 \times 706.9} = 28.3 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.4 \times 250 = 100 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ$$

**Step 3.** Bearing on lug plate (projected area = $d \times t$):
$$\sigma_b = \frac{V}{d \cdot t} = \frac{40{,}000}{30 \times 16} = 83.3 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{b,\text{allow}} = 0.9 \times 250 = 225 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ$$

**Step 4.** Bending of lug plate. Treat the plate as a cantilever from the weld root with load $V$ at distance $L$ from weld.

Take effective lever $L = 1.5 d = 45$ mm.

Bending moment:
$$M = V \cdot L = 40{,}000 \times 45 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬╖mm}$$

Section modulus of plate (rectangular):
$$Z = \frac{b t^2}{6} = \frac{200 \times 16^2}{6} = 8533 \text{ mm}^3$$

Bending stress:
$$\sigma_{bp} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{8533} = 211 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{bp,\text{allow}} = 0.66 \times 250 = 165 \text{ MPa} \;\; \text{EXCEEDED тАФ not safe!}$$

**Step 5.** Increase plate thickness to 20 mm.

$$Z = \frac{200 \times 20^2}{6} = 13{,}333 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{bp} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{13{,}333} = 135 \text{ MPa} \;\; тЬУ \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 6.** Fillet weld (leg = 8 mm, throat $t_t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66$ mm). 
Weld length required (double fillet, two sides):
$$L_w = \frac{V}{2 \times 0.707 \times t_t \times S_{weld}} = \frac{40{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 5.66 \times 144} = 17.4 \text{ mm}$$

(use 100 mm minimum, hence weld is *not* the critical element тАФ bending of plate was).

**Conclusion:** Use lug plate **200 ├Ч 20 mm** with **8 mm fillet weld, 100 mm long** on each side.

---

*(b)* A horizontal vessel of $D_o = 1500$ mm, length $L = 8$ m, weight $W = 120$ kN is mounted on **two saddles** placed at $a = 1.5$ m from each head. Compute the **longitudinal bending stresses** at the saddle (top) and at midspan (bottom) using Zick's method. Take $t = 16$ mm, $R = 750$ mm, $K_1 = 0.103$ (read from Zick chart for $L/R = 10.67$, $a/R = 2$). Use $K_2 = 0.050$. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Compute $L/R$ and $a/R$:
$$\frac{L}{R} = \frac{8}{0.75} = 10.67 \qquad \frac{a}{R} = \frac{1.5}{0.75} = 2.0$$

**Step 2.** Reaction at each saddle:
$$R = \frac{W}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3.** Moment at the saddle (Zick, top fibre, negative = compression):
$$M_A = -\frac{W a (L - a)}{2 L} = -\frac{120{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 6.5}{2 \times 8} = -73{,}125 \text{ N┬╖m} = -73.1 \text{ kN┬╖m}$$

**Step 4.** Moment at midspan (between saddles, positive = tension on bottom fibre):
$$M_C = +\frac{W a^2}{2 L} = +\frac{120{,}000 \times 1.5^2}{2 \times 8} = +16{,}875 \text{ N┬╖m} = +16.9 \text{ kN┬╖m}$$

**Step 5.** Longitudinal stress at top of shell at the saddle:
$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \pi R^2 t} = -\frac{-73{,}125 \times 10^3}{0.103 \times \pi \times 750^2 \times 16} = +\frac{7.3125 \times 10^7}{2.91 \times 10^7} = +2.51 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Wait тАФ sign convention check. Zick's $\sigma_1$ at the top of the shell *at the saddle* is **compressive** on the windward side; the formula with $M_A$ taken as the algebraic moment gives:

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{12 M_A R}{t^2 (\text{denom})}$$

Using the simplified K-factor form:
$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \pi R^2 t} = -\frac{73{,}125 \times 10^3}{0.103 \times \pi \times 750^2 \times 16} = -2.51 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 6.** Longitudinal stress at bottom midspan:
$$\sigma_2 = +\frac{M_C}{K_2 \pi R^2 t} = +\frac{16{,}875 \times 10^3}{0.050 \times \pi \times 750^2 \times 16} = +\frac{1.6875 \times 10^7}{1.414 \times 10^7} = +1.19 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 7.** Code check (UG-77 / IS 2825): both stresses are very small compared to allowable ($\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 1.5 S$ at this local region), so the saddle design is **SAFE**. The vessel thickness is governed by pressure, not by saddle stresses.

**Conclusion:** Saddle stresses are within limits; the design is safe.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common mistakes students make in Module 3 (forfeiting 1тАУ4 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.2 overstress factor** when wind or seismic dominates. The ASME allows $1.2 S E$ for occasional loads тАФ if you compare against $S E$ alone, the design will look *worse* than it is, and you may *unnecessarily* increase thickness.
> 2. **Confusing longitudinal stress direction.** In a *vertical* vessel, internal pressure produces a *longitudinal* (axial) stress. In a *horizontal* vessel, internal pressure produces a *hoop* stress, and the longitudinal stress comes from pressure only on the dished ends (a "pressure equivalent axial load"). Mixing these up is a classic error.
> 3. **Using the wrong slenderness check** for leg design. The KLU/IS 800 limit is $\lambda \leq 200$ for leg *bracing members*, but the *leg itself* as a column has a different effective length (use $K L$). Students often write $L/r$ instead of $K L / r$.
> 4. **Sign convention in Zick's formula.** $\sigma_1$ is *compressive* at the top of the shell at the saddle, while $\sigma_2$ is *tensile* at the bottom midspan. Drawing a free-body diagram and labeling the sense of $M_A$ prevents errors.
> 5. **Ignoring effective length factor $K$.** For a vessel *fixed at base, free at top* (cantilever) $K = 2.0$. For *fixed-fixed* (which rarely happens in practice) $K = 0.5$. The most common error is using $K = 1.0$ (pin-ended), which *underestimates* the critical load by 4├Ч.
> 6. **Stiffener ring spacing (UG-29).** If external pressure governs, the ring spacing is taken as $\max(L_s, D_o)$ where $L_s$ is the actual spacing. Missing this gives a non-conservative design.
> 7. **Not including joint efficiency $E_w$.** A common KTU trap: full-radiography $E_w = 1.0$, spot $E_w = 0.85$, none $E_w = 0.70$. Wrong $E_w$ means wrong allowable.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tall vessel criterion:** $H/D_i \geq 10$ (or $L/D_i \geq 5$). For such vessels, wind and seismic are the **governing design loads** for the shell and supports.
- **Wind:** $V_z = V_b k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4$; $p_z = 0.6 V_z^2$; $F = C_f A_e p_z$; $M_w = \sum F_i h_i$. **Use $C_f$ from IS 875 Table 10** (depends on $V_z D$).
- **Seismic:** $A_h = (Z/2)(I/R)(S_a/g)$; $V_B = A_h W_e$; $M_s = V_B \bar h$. **Kerala is Zone III ($Z = 0.16$).**
- **Combined longitudinal stress check:** $\sigma_L = p_r D_i/(4 t E_w) + 4M/(\pi D_i^{\,2} t) \leq 1.2 S E$ (when wind/seismic governs). The 1.2 multiplier is **critical** тАФ don't forget it.
- **Leg design:** $P = W/N$ axial, $M_{leg}$ = wind moment per leg. Check **bearing, compression, slenderness** ($\lambda = K L/r \leq 200$), and **column buckling** using IS 800 or Euler for $\lambda \geq 100$.
- **Lug design:** 4 critical checks тАФ **pin shear, bearing, plate bending, weld throat**. Often plate bending is the *governing* check, not the pin.
- **Saddle design (Zick, 1956):** Two key stresses тАФ $\sigma_1$ (top of shell at saddle) and $\sigma_2$ (bottom midspan). The Zick $K$-chart is read with $L/R$ and $a/R$. **Stiffener ring mandatory when $L/R > 8$.**
- **Buckling тАФ three flavors:**
  - **Column buckling** (vessel as cantilever): $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (K H)^2$; FS тЙе 1.5.
  - **External pressure buckling** (UG-28): $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5} / [(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}]$ for long cyl.; $p_a = p_{cr}/3$.
  - **Shell buckling stress** (Donnell-Batdorf): $\sigma_{cr} = 0.6 E t / R$.
- **Stiffener ring** is the practical solution for external pressure / vacuum service on tall vessels тАФ it reduces the effective length $L$ of the shell in the buckling formula.
- **Allowable-stress increase** for occasional loads (wind, seismic) is 1.2 тАФ the most-frequently-missed point in KTU valuations.
- **Wind direction matters:** design for the **maximum** of wind and seismic, *not* their sum. Codes use SRSS for orthogonal components.
- **Anchor bolt design** follows from base shear and overturning moment: $T_{\text{bolt}} = (M_B / D_{bolt\, circle}) - (W/N)$; ensure $T \leq 0.5 A_b S_y$ (tension) and $V \leq 0.3 A_b S_u$ (shear), with combined check per AISC J3.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Tip (Module 3):** Memorize the *three* formulas тАФ $p_{cr}$ (external pressure), $\sigma_{BL}$ (bending), and the Zick $\sigma_1$ тАФ and the *one* 1.2 overstress rule. With these four items, you can solve 80 % of the questions that appear in PEMET862 Module 3. Always draw a clean free-body diagram; KTU examiners award 1тАУ2 marks for clarity of sketch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Elastic Buckling of Cylinders or Pipes under External Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Elastic Buckling of Cylinders or Pipes under External Pressure # MODULE 3 тАФ ELASTIC BUCKLING OF CYLINDERS & PIPES UNDER EXTERNAL PRESSURE ## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Elastic Buckling of a Cylindrical Shell under External Pressure** is a structural instability phenomenon in which a thin-walled cylinder, when subjected to a uniform external hydrostatic or pneumatic pressure, suddenly loses its circular cross-section and deforms into a lobed (multi-petaled) pattern at a well-defined **Critical Buckling Pressure ($p_{cr}$)** тАФ *without* any yielding of the parent material (i.e., stresses remain entirely within the elastic regime governed by Hooke's Law, $E = 200$ GPa for carbon steel). In the context of **PEMET862**, this analysis belongs to the **external pressure design** branch of pressure vessel and piping codes (ASME Section VIII Div. 1 UG-28, BS 5500, EN 13445). The shell **buckles before it yields** when the diameter-to-thickness ratio ($D/t$) is large тАФ a fundamental design limit that often dictates the minimum required wall thickness of vacuum vessels, submarine pipelines, and offshore risers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of an **empty aluminium soda can**. Before opening, it is rigid and strong. But once you crush it from the sides with your palms, the cylindrical wall collapses into a flat, polygonal shape almost instantaneously тАФ and the metal never had a chance to yield. This *instantaneous geometric collapse* is **elastic buckling**. The same thing happens to: - ЁЯЪв Submarine hulls at great depth - ЁЯЫвя╕П Pipeline sections under deep-sea hydrostatic head - ЁЯзк Vacuum-jacketed dewars in cryogenic service - ЁЯМбя╕П Steam condenser shells operating under vacuum **Why does the shell buckle and not just compress uniformly?** Because a perfect cylinder is a *structurally unstable shape* тАФ a tiny out-of-plane disturbance (initial ovality, fabrication imperfection) grows rapidly at a critical pressure, and the shell "snaps" into a stable lobed configuration. This is mathematically analogous to a ball balanced on top of a hill тАФ any perturbation causes it to roll down. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Young's Modulus (Carbon Steel) | $E$ | **$200 \text{ GPa}$** | At ambient temperature | | Young's Modulus (Austenitic SS) | $E$ | **$193 \text{ GPa}$** | 304/316 stainless | | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **$0.3$** | For most engineering metals | | Diameter-to-thickness ratio | $D/t$ | **$10$ to $1000+$** | Thinness metric | | Length-to-diameter ratio | $L/D$ | **$0.5$ to $20+$** | Determines long/short classification | # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 Governing Physics тАФ Why Does a Cylinder Buckle? A perfect cylindrical shell under external pressure is in **membrane (hoop) compression only**: $$\sigma_{hoop} = -\frac{pD}{2t}$$ The **negative sign** indicates compression. As $p$ increases, $\sigma_{hoop}$ becomes more negative (compressive). The shell does not fail by crushing тАФ it fails by **loss of shape stability**, which occurs at a stress level far lower than the material's yield stress. The buckling pressure depends on **three** key geometric ratios: 1. **Thinness ratio** $D/t$ тАФ thinner shells buckle at lower pressure (very sensitive: $\propto (t/D)^3$). 2. **Slenderness ratio** $L/D$ тАФ longer shells buckle at lower pressure. 3. **Number of circumferential lobes** $n$ тАФ the buckled shape has $n$ "petals" around the circumference; the optimum $n$ minimises $p_{cr}$. ## 2.2 Classification тАФ Long, Short, and Intermediate Cylinders > [!IMPORTANT] > The buckling formula depends **critically** on whether end constraints and shell length allow the buckled waves to form fully along the length. | Cylinder Type | Defining Condition | Governing Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **Short Cylinder** | $L/D \leq$ small, $D/t$ moderate | Ends restrain shell тАФ buckling waves localise near mid-length | | **Long Cylinder** | $L/D$ large | Many half-waves fit along length тАФ ends irrelevant | | **Intermediate** | Transition zone | Use von Mises chart or numerical eigenvalue analysis | A practical boundary: - **Long** if $L \geq 1.73 \sqrt{R\,t}$ (sometimes cited as $\geq 4.4\sqrt{Rt}$). - **Short** if $L \leq 0.86 \sqrt{R\,t}$. ## 2.3 Classical Elastic Buckling Pressure Formulas > [!IMPORTANT] > The three classic formulas by **Bresse (1866)**, **Bryan (1891)**, and **von Mises (1914)** form the backbone of KTU Module 3 and ASME external pressure charts. ### (a) Short Cylinder (Bresse Formula тАФ 1866) For a *short* cylinder where the ends are effectively clamped and the buckled lobes have negligible axial half-wavelength: $$p_{cr} = \frac{2\,E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$ For $\nu = 0.3$: $$p_{cr} \approx 2.197\,E\,\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3} \;\;(\text{MPa, when } E \text{ in MPa})$$ ### (b) Long Cylinder (Bryan-Timoshenko Formula тАФ 1891) For a *long* cylinder where end effects vanish, the critical pressure is governed by: $$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6\,E}{\left(1-\nu^{2}\right)^{3/4}}\;\frac{\left(t/D\right)^{5/2}}{\left(L/D\right)}$$ For $\nu = 0.3$, the numerical coefficient reduces to approximately **$2.60$**: $$p_{cr} = \frac{2.60\,E}{\left(L/D\right)}\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{5/2}$$ The corresponding **optimum number of circumferential lobes** is: $$n = \sqrt[4]{12\left(1-\nu^{2}\right)}\,\sqrt{\frac{L^{2}}{R\,t}} \approx 1.72\sqrt{\frac{L}{D}}\left(\frac{D}{t}\right)^{1/4} \quad (\nu = 0.3)$$ ### (c) Intermediate Length тАФ von Mises Method > [!NOTE] > For $L$ between the long and short limits, **no closed-form analytical formula exists**. The classical approach uses the **von Mises chart** тАФ a graphical plot of $p_{cr}(1-\nu^{2})/E$ versus $L/R$ for fixed $R/t$. KTU students should be familiar with the chart-based interpolation. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Equation | Applies To | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Short Cylinder** (Bresse) | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ | Short, $L/R$ small | Vacuum vessel heads, short shells | | 2 | **Long Cylinder** (Bryan) | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E}{\left(1-\nu^{2}\right)^{3/4}}\dfrac{(t/D)^{5/2}}{(L/D)}$ | Long, $L/R$ large | Pipeline sections, condenser shells | | 3 | **Long, $\nu=0.3$** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.60\,E}{(L/D)}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{5/2}$ | Long cylinder, steel | Quick KTU calculation | | 4 | **Number of Lobes** | $n \approx 1.72\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{D}}\left(\dfrac{D}{t}\right)^{1/4}$ | Long cylinder | Buckled mode shape | | 5 | **Long-Short boundary** | $L_{c} \approx 1.73\sqrt{R\,t}$ | Boundary criterion | Classify before applying formula | | 6 | **Hoop Stress (membrane)** | $\sigma_{h} = -\dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Any cylinder | Compare with $p_{cr}$ | | 7 | **ASME UG-28 design margin** | $p_{design} = \dfrac{p_{cr}}{FS}$ | Code design | $FS = 3$ typical | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility** тАФ These formulas feed directly into the ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1 external pressure chart (Figure G). The chart is **dimensionless**: it plots $p_{cr}(D/t)^3/E$ versus $L/D$ for various $D/t$ values, eliminating the need for repeated calculation. ## 2.5 Effect of Geometric Imperfections > [!IMPORTANT] > Real shells are never perfectly circular. **Initial ovality** ($\Delta_0$) reduces $p_{cr}$ dramatically тАФ a 1% deviation can reduce buckling capacity by **20тАУ40%**. ASME addresses this with a knockdown factor embedded in the chart. ## 2.6 Why Buckling Theory Matters in Piping Design In offshore and subsea piping, **external hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$ is: $$p_{ext} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,h \approx 0.0101\,h \;\;\text{(bar, for seawater, depth in m)}$$ At 3000 m depth, $p_{ext} \approx 30$ bar тАФ easily sufficient to buckle an un-stiffened pipe. This is why deep-water pipelines use **buckling arrestors**, **thick-walled pipe**, or **internal pressure** to suppress collapse. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Short Cylinder Formula (Bresse, 1866) ### Step 1 тАФ Set Up the Shell Geometry Consider a short cylindrical shell of mean radius $R$, thickness $t$, length $L$, and end conditions: **simply supported at both ends**. Under uniform external pressure $p$, the pre-buckling membrane state is pure hoop compression: $$\sigma_{x} = 0,\quad N_{x} = 0$$ $$N_{\theta} = -pR$$ ### Step 2 тАФ Linearise the Pre-Buckling Equilibrium Since the cylinder is short, the axial wavelength of buckling is much larger than the circumferential wavelength. Axial bending stiffness dominates; axial membrane stretching is negligible. ### Step 3 тАФ Write the Donnell-Type Shell Equation For axisymmetric short-shell buckling (only the radial displacement $w$ matters), the linear stability equation is: $$D\,\nabla^{8}w + \dfrac{E\,t}{R^{2}}\,\dfrac{\partial^{4}w}{\partial x^{4}} = 0$$ where $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ is the flexural rigidity per unit length. ### Step 4 тАФ Assume a Buckling Mode Shape For a short cylinder clamped at ends with buckled lobes forming in the circumferential direction, assume: $$w(x,\theta) = A\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{L}\right)\cos(n\theta)$$ where $n$ = number of circumferential lobes (integer), $m$ = number of axial half-waves. ### Step 5 тАФ Substitute into the Stability Equation After substitution and using the Laplacian in cylindrical coordinates: $$\nabla^{2} = \dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{R^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial \theta^{2}}$$ We obtain: $$D\left[\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]^{4}w + \dfrac{E\,t}{R^{2}}\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{L}\right)^{4}w = 0$$ ### Step 6 тАФ Solve for the Critical Pressure For non-trivial $w \neq 0$: $$D\left[\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]^{4} = -\dfrac{E\,t}{R^{2}}\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{L}\right)^{4}$$ For a short cylinder ($m=1$ dominant, $L$ small so $\left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^{2} \gg \frac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}$ is *not* satisfied тАФ instead, $n \gg \frac{\pi R}{L}$, so circumferential term dominates), the minimum occurs at $n \to \infty$ theoretically, but physical geometry limits $n$. With $m=1$ and minimising over $n$, classical short-cylinder result is: $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3} \quad\blacksquare$$ For $\nu = 0.3$, this gives the numerical factor **$2.197$**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Long Cylinder Formula (Bryan, 1891) ### Step 1 тАФ Geometric Setup Long cylinder, length $L \gg \sqrt{Rt}$, simply supported. Buckled mode has many axial half-waves ($m \gg 1$) and $n$ circumferential lobes. ### Step 2 тАФ Mode Shape Assumption $$w(x,\theta) = A\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{L}\right)\cos(n\theta)$$ ### Step 3 тАФ Total Potential Energy Functional The total potential energy change at buckling is: $$\Pi = \tfrac{1}{2}\int\!\!\int D\left(\nabla^{2}w\right)^{2}dA + \tfrac{1}{2}\int\!\!\int\dfrac{N_{\theta}}{R}\left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial \theta}\right)^{2}dA$$ (Note: $N_{\theta} = -pR$, the destabilising term is the hoop force.) ### Step 4 тАФ Substitute the Mode Shape and Integrate For $w = A\sin(\alpha x)\cos(n\theta)$ with $\alpha = m\pi/L$: $$\nabla^{2}w = -\left(\alpha^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)A\sin(\alpha x)\cos(n\theta)$$ $$\left(\nabla^{2}w\right)^{2} = A^{2}\left(\alpha^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2}\sin^{2}(\alpha x)\cos^{2}(n\theta)$$ Integrating over $\pi R L$ (one axial half-wave, full circumference): $$\Pi = \dfrac{\pi R L}{4}D A^{2}\left(\alpha^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2} - \dfrac{\pi L}{4}\dfrac{n^{2}p}{R}A^{2}$$ ### Step 5 тАФ Minimisation Condition For non-trivial $A$, $\partial\Pi/\partial A = 0$ gives: $$p = \dfrac{D R}{n^{2}}\left(\alpha^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2}$$ ### Step 6 тАФ Minimise Over $\alpha$ and $n$ Let $u = \alpha^{2}R^{2}/n^{2}$. Then: $$p = \dfrac{D}{R^{3}}\,\dfrac{(u+1)^{2}}{u}$$ Minimising over $u$ (treat as continuous): $\dfrac{dp}{du}=0 \Rightarrow u = 1$, so: $$p_{min} = \dfrac{4D}{R^{3}} = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{3(1-\nu^{2})R^{3}}$$ ### Step 7 тАФ But the Number of Lobes $n$ is Also Free! We now minimise over $n$, with $\alpha = m\pi/L$, $m = 1, 2, \ldots$. Substituting $\alpha = n/R$ (from $u=1$) into $m = \alpha L/\pi$: $$m = \dfrac{n L}{\pi R}$$ For $m$ to be an integer $\geq 1$, we need $n \geq \pi R/L$. Substituting back with integer constraint on $n$: $$\boxed{\,p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E}{\left(1-\nu^{2}\right)^{3/4}}\;\dfrac{(t/D)^{5/2}}{(L/D)}\,} \quad\blacksquare$$ The factor **$2.6$** comes from evaluating the minimum at non-integer $n$ and rounding тАФ Timoshenko's classical result. The exponent **$5/2$** on $(t/D)$ reveals the famous high sensitivity to wall thickness. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A long cylindrical shell has $D = 1000$ mm, $L = 4000$ mm, $t = 10$ mm. Find $p_{cr}$, $n$, and compare with the short-cylinder result. Material: carbon steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$). ### Step 1 тАФ Verify "Long" Classification $$L_{c} = 1.73\sqrt{R\,t} = 1.73\sqrt{500 \times 10} = 1.73\sqrt{5000} \approx 122.3 \text{ mm}$$ Since $L = 4000$ mm $\gg 122.3$ mm, the cylinder is **definitely long**. тЬУ ### Step 2 тАФ Apply Bryan-Timoshenko Formula $$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{4000}{1000} = 4$$ $$\dfrac{t}{D} = \dfrac{10}{1000} = 0.01$$ $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 200{,}000}{(4)} \times (0.01)^{2.5} \;\;(\text{MPa})$$ $$(0.01)^{2.5} = 10^{-5} = 0.00001$$ $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{520{,}000}{4} \times 0.00001 = 130{,}000 \times 0.00001 = \boxed{1.30 \text{ MPa}}$$ ### Step 3 тАФ Calculate Number of Lobes $$n = 1.72\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{D}}\left(\dfrac{D}{t}\right)^{1/4} = 1.72\sqrt{4} \times (100)^{0.25} = 1.72 \times 2 \times 3.162 = \boxed{10.88 \approx 11 \text{ lobes}}$$ ### Step 4 тАФ Comparison with Short-Cylinder Formula $$p_{cr,short} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 200{,}000}{0.91}\times (0.01)^{3}$$ $$= \dfrac{400{,}000}{0.91} \times 10^{-6} = 439{,}560 \times 10^{-6} \approx \boxed{0.44 \text{ MPa}}$$ ### Step 5 тАФ Engineering Interpretation The **long-cylinder value (1.30 MPa)** is *higher* than the short-cylinder value (0.44 MPa) for the same shell because the **ends provide stability**. Since this is a long cylinder, the **governing $p_{cr} = 1.30$ MPa**. With a Factor of Safety of 3: $$p_{design} = \dfrac{1.30}{3} \approx 0.433 \text{ MPa}$$ This shell is safe for vacuum service ($p_{ext} = 0.1$ MPa). # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Buckling Analysis Decision Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Cylindrical Shell
Geometry Input
D, t, L, E, nu"] --> B["Compute Thinness Ratio
D divided by t"] B --> C{"Compute L over sqrt R t"} C -->|"L >> 1.73 sqrt R t"| D["LONG CYLINDER CASE"] C -->|"L ~ 1.73 sqrt R t"| E["INTERMEDIATE CASE"] C -->|"L << 1.73 sqrt R t"| F["SHORT CYLINDER CASE"] D --> G["Apply Bryan Formula
p_cr = 2.6 E (t/D)^2.5 / (L/D)"] G --> H["Compute Number of Lobes
n = 1.72 sqrt L/D (D/t)^0.25"] H --> I["Apply FS = 3
p_design = p_cr / 3"] E --> J["Use von Mises Chart
Graphical Interpolation"] J --> I F --> K["Apply Bresse Formula
p_cr = 2 E (t/D)^3 / 1 - nu squared"] K --> I I --> L["Compare with External Pressure
p_ext"] L --> M{"p_ext less than p_design?"} M -->|"Yes"| N["DESIGN SAFE"] M -->|"No"| O["ADD STIFFENERS
or INCREASE t"] ``` ## 4.2 Buckled Mode Shape тАФ Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INTACT["Intact Cylinder - Pre-Buckling State"] A1["Circular Cross Section"] A2["Hoop Stress uniform
sigma_h = pD / 2t"] end subgraph BUCKLED["Buckled State - Post Critical"] B1["n Lobes around Circumference"] B2["m Half Waves along Length"] B3["Cross Section becomes Polygonal"] B4["No Material Yielding
Purely Geometric Collapse"] end subgraph CONSEQUENCES["Engineering Consequences"] C1["Loss of Internal Volume"] C2["Sudden - no warning"] C3["Catastrophic for Vacuum Vessels"] end INTACT -->|"p reaches p_cr"| BUCKLED BUCKLED --> CONSEQUENCES ``` ## 4.3 Long vs Short Cylinder тАФ Block Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph LONG["LONG CYLINDER"] L1["L >> 1.73 sqrt R t"] L2["Bryan Timoshenko Formula"] L3["p_cr scales as t to D power 2.5"] L4["Many Buckling Lobes"] L5["Ends Negligible"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 end subgraph SHORT["SHORT CYLINDER"] S1["L small, ends dominant"] S2["Bresse Formula 1866"] S3["p_cr scales as t to D power 3"] S4["Few Buckling Lobes"] S5["Ends Provide Clamping"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 end ``` ## 4.4 Parameter Influence Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Wall Thickness t"] -->|"Most Sensitive
p_cr scales t cubed to power 2.5"| RESULT["Critical Buckling Pressure"] P2["Diameter D"] -->|"Inverse Cubic Effect"| RESULT P3["Length L"] -->|"Long Cyl only
Inverse Linear"| RESULT P4["Modulus E"] -->|"Direct Linear"| RESULT P5["Ovality Imperfection"] -->|"Knockdown 20 to 40 percent"| RESULT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams**: Note that **wall thickness $t$** has the strongest leverage on buckling resistance тАФ engineers almost always add thickness before adding stiffening rings, because $p_{cr} \propto t^{2.5}$ to $t^{3}$. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK ## PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]** ### Q1. Define *elastic buckling of a cylindrical shell under external pressure*. State the classical Bresse formula for a short cylinder. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Elastic buckling is a **mode of structural instability** in which a thin-walled cylindrical shell, subjected to uniform external pressure, suddenly deforms from its circular cross-section into a multi-lobed (polygonal) shape at a critical pressure $p_{cr}$, **without** the material yielding. The instability is geometric, governed by shell stiffness rather than material strength. The Bresse formula for a *short* cylinder is: $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$ This applies when the cylinder length is small enough that the end constraints effectively clamp the shell against axial wave formation. --- > **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** ### Q2. Distinguish between **long** and **short** cylinder buckling under external pressure. Give the governing formula in each case. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Long Cylinder | Short Cylinder | |---|---|---| | **Criterion** | $L \geq 1.73\sqrt{Rt}$ | $L \leq 1.73\sqrt{Rt}$ | | **Formula** | $p_{cr}=\dfrac{2.6E(t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ (Bryan-Timoshenko) | $p_{cr}=\dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ (Bresse) | | **Sensitivity to $t$** | $p_{cr} \propto t^{2.5}$ | $p_{cr} \propto t^{3}$ | | **Sensitivity to $L$** | $p_{cr} \propto 1/L$ | Independent of $L$ | | **End effects** | Negligible | Dominant | --- ## PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE тАУ Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]** --- ### тнР Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the **classical elastic buckling pressure for a long cylindrical shell** under uniform external pressure, starting from the energy method and using the Bryan-Timoshenko assumption for the buckled mode shape. Clearly state all assumptions and the final closed-form expression. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A thin-walled carbon steel pipe of diameter $D = 800$ mm, wall thickness $t = 6$ mm, and length $L = 6$ m is to be installed at a depth of 2500 m in seawater ($\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m┬│, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓). Material properties: $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate:     (i) the critical elastic buckling pressure using the **long-cylinder formula**,     (ii) the **number of circumferential lobes** in the buckled mode,     (iii) the **design pressure** with a factor of safety of 3,     (iv) whether the pipe is safe against external collapse. **(7 marks)** --- ### тЬЕ Model Answer тАФ Question A #### Part (a) тАФ Derivation (7 marks) **Assumptions:** 1. Shell is **long**, $L \gg \sqrt{Rt}$ тАФ end effects negligible. 2. **Linear elastic** material, $E, \nu$ constant. 3. Buckled mode: $w(x,\theta) = A\sin(\alpha x)\cos(n\theta)$, where $\alpha = m\pi/L$. 4. Pre-buckling membrane state: pure hoop compression $N_\theta = -pR$, $N_x = 0$. **Total potential energy:** $$\Pi = \tfrac{1}{2}\int D\left(\nabla^{2}w\right)^{2}dA + \tfrac{1}{2}\int\dfrac{N_{\theta}}{R}\left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial \theta}\right)^{2}dA$$ Substituting the mode shape and integrating: $$\Pi = \dfrac{\pi R L A^{2}}{4}\left[D\left(\alpha^{2}+\dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2} - \dfrac{n^{2}p}{R}\right]$$ Setting $\partial\Pi/\partial A = 0$: $$p = \dfrac{D\,R}{n^{2}}\left(\alpha^{2}+\dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2}$$ Minimising over continuous $\alpha$ with $n$ fixed: optimum at $\alpha R = n$, giving: $$p_{min}(n) = \dfrac{4D}{R^{3}} = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{3(1-\nu^{2})R^{3}}$$ **However, $n$ must be integer-compatible with the length**: setting $m=1$ axial half-wave (lowest mode) and substituting $\alpha = \pi/L$: $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{D\,R}{n^{2}}\left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]^{2}$$ Minimising over $n$ (treated as continuous for classical result, then rounded): $$\boxed{\,p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4}}\,\dfrac{(t/D)^{5/2}}{(L/D)}\,}$$ For $\nu = 0.3$: coefficient $\approx 2.60$. **End of derivation. [7 marks]** --- #### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 marks) **(i) Critical buckling pressure:** $$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{6000}{800} = 7.5,\quad \dfrac{t}{D} = \dfrac{6}{800} = 0.0075$$ Verify long cylinder: $$L_{c} = 1.73\sqrt{R\,t} = 1.73\sqrt{400 \times 6} = 1.73\sqrt{2400} \approx 84.8 \text{ mm}$$ Since $L = 6000$ mm $\gg 84.8$ mm, **long-cylinder formula applies**. $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 200{,}000 \times (0.0075)^{2.5}}{7.5}$$ $(0.0075)^{2.5} = (7.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2.5}$. Compute: $7.5^{2.5} = 7.5^{2}\sqrt{7.5} = 56.25 \times 2.739 \approx 154.1$. So $(0.0075)^{2.5} = 154.1 \times 10^{-7.5} = 154.1 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-8} \approx 4.872 \times 10^{-6}$. $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{520{,}000}{7.5} \times 4.872 \times 10^{-6} = 69{,}333 \times 4.872 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{p_{cr} \approx 0.3377 \text{ MPa} \approx 0.338 \text{ MPa}}$$ **(ii) Number of lobes:** $$n = 1.72\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{D}}\left(\dfrac{D}{t}\right)^{0.25} = 1.72\sqrt{7.5}\times(133.33)^{0.25}$$ $\sqrt{7.5} = 2.739$, $(133.33)^{0.25} = \sqrt{\sqrt{133.33}} = \sqrt{11.547} \approx 3.398$ $$n = 1.72 \times 2.739 \times 3.398 \approx \boxed{16.0 \approx 16 \text{ lobes}}$$ **(iii) Design pressure:** $$p_{design} = \dfrac{p_{cr}}{FS} = \dfrac{0.338}{3} \approx \boxed{0.1127 \text{ MPa}}$$ **(iv) External pressure at 2500 m depth:** $$p_{ext} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2500 = 25{,}138{,}125 \text{ Pa} \approx 25.14 \text{ MPa}$$ **Comparison:** $p_{ext} = 25.14$ MPa $\gg p_{design} = 0.113$ MPa. $$\boxed{\text{The pipe is NOT SAFE тАФ it will buckle massively at this depth.}}$$ **Engineering recommendation:** Increase wall thickness to $t \approx 25$тАУ30 mm, add **buckling arrestors**, or use an **internal pressurisation** strategy. **[7 marks]** --- ### тнР Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **buckled mode shape** of a long cylindrical shell under external pressure. Explain how the **number of circumferential lobes $n$** is determined, and derive the expression for $n$ in terms of $D$, $t$, $L$, and $\nu$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A short cylindrical pressure vessel of mean diameter $D = 500$ mm, wall thickness $t = 12$ mm, and unsupported length $L = 200$ mm is designed for vacuum service. Using $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$:     (i) Verify whether the vessel qualifies as a short cylinder.     (ii) Calculate the **elastic buckling pressure** using the Bresse formula.     (iii) If the **maximum permissible external pressure** is 0.5 MPa (with FS = 3), check if the design is adequate. Suggest modifications if not. **(7 marks)** --- ### тЬЕ Model Answer тАФ Question B #### Part (a) тАФ Mode Shape & Number of Lobes (7 marks) **Buckled mode shape (textual schematic description):** A long cylinder under external pressure buckles into a **polygonal cross-section with $n$ identical lobes** ("petals") arranged symmetrically around the circumference. The buckled waves run along the axial direction as a series of half-wavelengths $L/m$, with $m$ half-waves fitting along the cylinder length. The deformation pattern resembles a *flower-petal arrangement* when viewed end-on. The radial displacement $w$ varies sinusoidally in both $x$ and $\theta$, alternating inward and outward. **Determination of $n$:** Starting from Bryan's energy minimisation, the optimum (continuous) $n$ is obtained by differentiating $p_{cr}$ with respect to $n$: $$\dfrac{\partial p_{cr}}{\partial n} = 0$$ After algebra, this yields: $$n = \sqrt[4]{12(1-\nu^{2})}\sqrt{\dfrac{L^{2}}{R\,t}}$$ Substituting $\nu = 0.3$, $\sqrt[4]{12(1-\nu^{2})} = \sqrt[4]{12 \times 0.91} = \sqrt[4]{10.92} \approx 1.815$ Re-expressing in terms of $D$, $L$, $t$ (using $R = D/2$): $$n = 1.815 \times \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{R\,t}} = 1.815 \times \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{Dt/2}} = 1.815\sqrt{2}\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{Dt}}$$ $$\boxed{\,n = 1.72\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{D}}\left(\dfrac{D}{t}\right)^{1/4}\,}$$ The integer nearest to this value is the actual lobe count. **Larger $L/t$ тЖТ more lobes.** **[7 marks]** --- #### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 marks) **(i) Short-cylinder verification:** $$L_{c} = 1.73\sqrt{R\,t} = 1.73\sqrt{250 \times 12} = 1.73\sqrt{3000} = 1.73 \times 54.77 \approx 94.75 \text{ mm}$$ Since $L = 200$ mm $> L_c = 94.75$ mm, the cylinder is **NOT in the strict short regime** тАФ it lies in the **intermediate regime**. However, if the question requires short-cylinder application (ends clamped), we proceed. **(ii) Bresse formula calculation:** $$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 200{,}000}{0.91}\left(\dfrac{12}{500}\right)^{3}$$ $$= \dfrac{400{,}000}{0.91}\times(0.024)^{3} = 439{,}560 \times 1.382 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\boxed{p_{cr} \approx 6.075 \text{ MPa}}$$ **(iii) Design check:** $$p_{design} = \dfrac{p_{cr}}{3} = \dfrac{6.075}{3} = 2.025 \text{ MPa}$$ Since $p_{design} = 2.025$ MPa $> p_{max} = 0.5$ MPa, the vessel is **safe for vacuum service**. тЬУ **If FS were marginal or design failed**: Increase $t$ (most effective тАФ cubic effect), add **stiffening rings** at intermediate spacing to reduce effective $L$, or restrict operating vacuum level. **[7 marks]** --- ## тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Buckling Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor** in the Bresse formula тАФ leads to ~9% error, KTU deducts 1 mark. > 2. **Using wrong formula for the cylinder classification** тАФ students often apply the long-cylinder formula to short cylinders, getting $p_{cr}$ too high by an order of magnitude. Always verify $L$ vs $1.73\sqrt{Rt}$ first. > 3. **Unit confusion** тАФ $E$ must be in **MPa** when $p_{cr}$ is required in **MPa**. Mixing GPa and MPa is the #1 silent error. > 4. **Confusing $D$ (mean) vs $D_o$ (outer)** тАФ ASME uses **mean diameter**. Using outer diameter gives ~1тАУ2% error, generally acceptable; using inner diameter is *wrong*. > 5. **Skipping the long/short classification step** тАФ KTU examiners look for this as a deliberate analysis step (2тАУ3 marks). Always classify first. > 6. **Rounding $n$ prematurely** тАФ keep $n$ as a real number until the very end, then round to the nearest integer for the lobe count. --- ## ЁЯУМ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets тАФ Must Memorise** - **Bresse (Short Cylinder):** $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ тАФ independent of $L$, sensitive to $t^{3}$. - **Bryan-Timoshenko (Long Cylinder):** $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E(t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ тАФ sensitive to $L^{-1}$ and $t^{2.5}$. - **Classification boundary:** $L_c = 1.73\sqrt{Rt}$ (or equivalently $L/D \approx 1.73\sqrt{0.5 \cdot t/D}$). - **Number of lobes (long cylinder):** $n = 1.72\sqrt{L/D}(D/t)^{1/4}$. - **Pre-buckling hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = -pD/2t$ (compression). - **Material constants (steel):** $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $1-\nu^2 = 0.91$. - **Engineering takeaway:** Thicker walls beat stiffeners for buckling resistance; reduce unsupported $L$ via ring-stiffeners if $t$ cannot be increased. - **Real-world driver:** External hydrostatic pressure in subsea piping; vacuum in process vessels; condenser shells in steam plants. - **ASME design safety factor:** typically **3** for external pressure. - **Imperfection effect:** Initial ovality of even 1% can reduce $p_{cr}$ by 20тАУ40% тАФ real shells always buckle *below* classical predictions.

topics.title: Design of Stiffeners for Pressure Vessel (Use Relevant ASME Codes and Standard Practices in Pressure Vessel Design for the Entire Third Module)
Pipes - Size and Wall Thickness Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipes - Size and Wall Thickness Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pipe Sizing** is the engineering process of selecting the appropriate nominal diameter (size) of a pipe to safely transport a required flow rate of fluid while satisfying hydraulic, mechanical, economic, and code-based constraints.

**Wall Thickness Specification** is the procedure of determining the minimum required pipe wall thickness to withstand internal pressure, external loads, thermal effects, corrosion, and mechanical allowances, as prescribed by the governing piping code (e.g., ASME B31.1 Power Piping, ASME B31.3 Process Piping, or ASME B31.4 Pipeline Transportation Systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Pipe size refers to its **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** measured in inches, while wall thickness is specified through **Schedule Numbers (Sch)** or by direct millimetre designation. Both size and schedule must satisfy the **pressure design thickness** requirement of the applicable ASME B31 code.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a garden hose. If you want to spray water farther, you either:
1. **Increase the diameter** (wider hose = more water per second but lower velocity), or
2. **Increase the wall thickness** (thicker rubber = the hose can withstand higher water pressure without bursting).

Pipes in a refinery or power plant work exactly the same way, except we use **steel** instead of rubber and **high-pressure process fluids** instead of water. The engineer has two design knobs:
- **Diameter (Size):** Chosen from hydraulics (flow rate, velocity, pressure drop).
- **Thickness (Schedule):** Chosen from mechanics (internal pressure, material strength, safety factor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - Pipe **Size** тЙИ the *width* of the road (controls traffic/flow capacity).
> - Pipe **Thickness** тЙИ the *strength* of the bridge (controls pressure containment).
> - **Schedule Number** тЙИ the *grade rating* of the bridge stamped on its nameplate.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The two universal sizing systems used globally are:

| Parameter | Definition | Standard |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| **NPS (Nominal Pipe Size)** | North American dimensionless identifier for pipe diameter | ASME B36.10M / B36.19M |
| **DN (Diam├иtre Nominal)** | European/ISO equivalent of NPS in millimetres | ISO 6708 |
| **OD (Outside Diameter)** | Fixed value for a given NPS regardless of schedule | ASME B36.10M |
| **Schedule (Sch)** | Number indicating wall thickness for a given NPS | ASME B36.10M |
| **Allowable Stress (S)** | Maximum permissible stress at design temperature | ASME B31.3 Table A-1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Important Rule:** For **NPS 1 to NPS 12**, the **OD is fixed** (e.g., NPS 4 тЖТ OD = 114.3 mm). Wall thickness *increases* as the schedule number increases (Sch 5S, 10S, 10, 20, 30, 40S, 40, 60, 80S, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, XXS).
> For **NPS 14 and above**, OD equals NPS (in inches) ├Ч 25.4 mm, with schedule controlling thickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pipe Sizing тАФ Theoretical Breakdown

Pipe sizing is **not** a single calculation but a **multi-criteria optimisation** between:

1. **Hydraulic Requirement (Flow Criterion)**
   - Continuity equation: $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 \cdot v$
   - Where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m┬│/s), $D_i$ = inside diameter (m), $v$ = fluid velocity (m/s).
   - Recommended velocities (KTU standard values):
     - Liquids: **1.0 тАУ 3.0 m/s**
     - Gases/Steam: **15 тАУ 40 m/s**
     - High-pressure steam: **30 тАУ 60 m/s**

2. **Economic Sizing (Optimum Velocity)**
   - Larger pipe тЖТ higher capital cost, lower pumping cost.
   - Smaller pipe тЖТ lower capital cost, higher pumping cost.
   - Optimum chosen at the velocity where the **total annual cost is minimum**.

3. **Code Constraints**
   - Minimum velocity to avoid solids settling in slurry lines.
   - Maximum velocity to avoid erosion, water hammer, noise (API 14E: $v_{max} \le \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{\rho}}$ for two-phase flow).

### 2.2 Wall Thickness Specification тАФ Theoretical Logic

The minimum required wall thickness for a straight pipe under **internal pressure** is derived from **hoop stress equilibrium**. For a thin cylindrical shell:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}$$

Setting hoop stress equal to allowable stress $S$ (times weld joint reduction factor $E$ and optional design factor $F$):

$$t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F + P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Variant тАФ The "F" Factor:**
> - **ASME B31.1 (Power Piping):** Includes factor **F** = 1.0 for ferritic steels, F = 0.85 for austenitic stainless steels at elevated temperature, F = 0.80 for cast iron.
> - **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping):** Does **NOT** include F (F = 1.0 implicitly). Uses different allowable stress tables.
> - **ASME B31.4 (Liquid Pipelines):** Includes **F** = 0.72 (location class dependent).

### 2.3 Allowance Additions

The **pressure design thickness** ($t_m$) is the bare minimum to resist internal pressure. The **total required thickness** ($t_{nom}$) includes additional allowances:

$$t_{nom} = t_m + c$$

Where the **corrosion + mechanical allowance** $c$ is typically:

$$c = c_c + c_m + c_{mill}$$

- $c_c$ = corrosion allowance (typically **1.5 mm to 3.0 mm** for carbon steel in hydrocarbon service).
- $c_m$ = mechanical allowance (for threading, groove wear, etc. тАФ typically **0 mm** for plain-end pipe).
- $c_{mill}$ = mill tolerance (typically **12.5 % negative** of nominal тАФ taken as additional thickness buffer).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table for KTU Board Exam тАФ Pipe Wall Thickness**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Code | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------|----------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Pressure Design Thickness (B31.1) | $t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F + P}$ | B31.1 | $t_m$ = mm, $P$ = MPa, $D_o$ = mm, $S$ = MPa |
| 2 | Pressure Design Thickness (B31.3) | $t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E + P}$ | B31.3 | F = 1 implicitly |
| 3 | Pressure Design Thickness (B31.3 alt.) | $t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot (S \cdot E + P \cdot Y)}$ | B31.3 ferritic | $Y$ = 0.4 coefficient (for ferritic at T тЙе 510 ┬░C) |
| 4 | Total Required Thickness | $t_{nom} = t_m + c$ | All | $c$ = corrosion + mechanical allowance |
| 5 | Design Pressure | $P_{design} = P_{operating} + P_{margin}$ | All | Usually $P_{margin}$ = 10 % or 0.345 MPa, whichever is greater |
| 6 | Allowable Stress | From ASME II-D or B31.3 Table A-1 | All | Material & temperature dependent |
| 7 | Velocity Check | $v = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D_i^2}$ | Hydraulic | $Q$ = m┬│/s, $D_i$ = m |
| 8 | Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 t}$ | Mechanics | $D_i$ = inside diameter |
| 9 | Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{4 t}$ | Mechanics | For closed-end cylinder |
| 10 | Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i \sqrt{2}}{4 t}$ | Von Mises | Combines hoop + longitudinal |
| 11 | Mill Tolerance | $t_{min,available} = t_{nom} (1 - 0.125)$ | ASME B36.10M | 12.5 % negative tolerance |
| 12 | Effective Stress Check | $S_E = \min(S_{hot}, S_{cold})$ | B31.3 | Lowest of hot/cold allowable |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Pipe sizing ensures flow continuity; thickness specification prevents rupture in hydrocracker units operating at **15тАУ20 MPa** and **400 ┬░C+**.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler tubes and steam lines use B31.1; thickness governs creep life at **540 ┬░C+**.
- **Offshore Platforms:** B31.4 / B31.8 govern subsea pipelines where wall thickness dictates buckling resistance under external hydrostatic pressure.
- **Cryogenic Plants:** Thickness selection balances internal pressure with brittleness at **тАУ196 ┬░C** (LNG service).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Pressure Design Thickness (ASME B31.1)

**Starting Point тАФ Thin Cylindrical Shell Mechanics:**

Consider a straight pipe section of inside diameter $D_i$, wall thickness $t$, containing internal gauge pressure $P$. Cut the cylinder longitudinally along its axis. Force equilibrium on one half requires the internal pressure acting on the projected area to be balanced by the hoop stress acting on the two longitudinal cross-sections:

$$P \cdot D_i \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

Simplifying by cancelling length $L$:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 t}$$

> **Step 1 тАФ Stress to be Limited by Allowable:**
> For safe operation, the hoop stress must not exceed the allowable stress $S$ reduced by weld joint efficiency $E$ (accounts for seam weld quality: $E = 1.0$ for seamless, $E = 0.85$ or $0.95$ for ERW based on NDE) and design factor $F$:

$$\sigma_{allow} = S \cdot E \cdot F$$

> **Step 2 тАФ Solve for Thickness:**
> Setting $\sigma_h = S \cdot E \cdot F$:

$$\dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 t} = S \cdot E \cdot F$$

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F}$$

> **Step 3 тАФ Convert Inside Diameter $D_i$ to Outside Diameter $D_o$:**
> Inside diameter $D_i = D_o - 2 t$. Substituting:

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot (D_o - 2t)}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F}$$

$$2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F \cdot t = P \cdot D_o - 2 P \cdot t$$

$$2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F \cdot t + 2 P \cdot t = P \cdot D_o$$

$$t \cdot (2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F + P) = P \cdot D_o$$

$$\boxed{\, t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F + P} \,}$$

This is the **Barlow-modified thin-wall formula** as codified in ASME B31.1 Equation (3a).

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example тАФ KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**

A pipeline carries oil at design pressure **4.0 MPa** and design temperature **200 ┬░C**. The pipe is specified as **NPS 6, Schedule 40S, ASTM A106 Grade B**, seamless ($E = 1.0$). Outside diameter for NPS 6 is **168.28 mm**. Nominal wall thickness for Sch 40S = **7.11 mm**. Allowable stress for A106-B at 200 ┬░C = **96 MPa**. The applicable code is **ASME B31.3** ($F = 1$, $Y = 0$). Corrosion allowance $c = 1.5$ mm. Mill tolerance = 12.5 %. Determine if the specified pipe is safe.

**Step 1 тАФ Identify Code & Inputs:**

Code: ASME B31.3, so the formula is:

$$t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E + P}$$

Given values:

$$P = 4.0 \text{ MPa},\quad D_o = 168.28 \text{ mm},\quad S = 96 \text{ MPa},\quad E = 1.0$$

> **Step 2 тАФ Substitute:**

$$t_m = \dfrac{4.0 \times 168.28}{2 \times 96 \times 1.0 + 4.0}$$

$$t_m = \dfrac{673.12}{192.0 + 4.0}$$

$$t_m = \dfrac{673.12}{196.0}$$

$$t_m = 3.434 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 3 тАФ Add Corrosion Allowance:**

$$t_{req} = t_m + c = 3.434 + 1.5 = 4.934 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 4 тАФ Account for Mill Tolerance:**
> Minimum delivered thickness (worst case):

$$t_{min,available} = t_{nom} \times (1 - 0.125) = 7.11 \times 0.875 = 6.221 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 5 тАФ Compare:**

$$t_{min,available} = 6.221 \text{ mm} \;>\; t_{req} = 4.934 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 6 тАФ Conclusion:**
> тЬЕ **The pipe NPS 6, Sch 40S is SAFE.** The available thickness (6.221 mm) exceeds the required thickness (4.934 mm) by a margin of 1.287 mm.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always show **all six steps** above in KTU board exams. Marks are awarded for identifying the correct code equation, substituting values with units, and concluding with a clear YES/NO judgment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Decision Flowchart Logic

| Step | Question Asked | Action / Formula | Decision |
|------|----------------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | What is the design pressure? | $P_d = P_{op} + \text{margin}$ | Fixed value |
| 2 | What is the design temperature? | Look up $S$ from code tables | $S$ chosen |
| 3 | Which code governs? | B31.1 / B31.3 / B31.4 | Pick formula |
| 4 | Is pipe seamless or welded? | Pick $E$ from code tables | $E$ chosen |
| 5 | Calculate $t_m$ | Use pressure formula | $t_m$ computed |
| 6 | Add corrosion allowance | $t_{req} = t_m + c$ | $t_{req}$ computed |
| 7 | Compare with available | $t_{nom}$ vs. $t_{req}$ | SAFE / UNSAFE |
| 8 | Check mill tolerance | $t_{min} = 0.875 \cdot t_{nom}$ | Re-verify |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block тАФ Pipe Sizing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Process Requirements] --> A1[Fluid Type and Phase]
    A1 --> A2[Liquid Line]
    A1 --> A3[Gas or Steam Line]
    A2 --> A4[Set Velocity 1 to 3 m per s]
    A3 --> A5[Set Velocity 15 to 60 m per s]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Diameter from Q equals A times v]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Round Up to Standard NPS]
    A7 --> A8[Select Candidate Schedule]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Code Pressure Formula]
    A9 --> A10[Add Corrosion Allowance]
    A10 --> A11[Check Mill Tolerance]
    A11 --> A12{Thickness Sufficient?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Final Selection Locked]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Step Up to Higher Schedule]
    A14 --> A8
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block тАФ Wall Thickness Logic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Inputs: P, T, D, Material] --> B1[Lookup Allowable Stress S]
    B1 --> B2[Lookup Weld Efficiency E]
    B2 --> B3[Lookup Design Factor F]
    B3 --> B4[Compute tm by Code Formula]
    B4 --> B5[Add Corrosion c]
    B5 --> B6[Add Mill Tolerance Buffer]
    B6 --> B7[Compare with Available Schedule]
    B7 --> B8{Accept?}
    B8 -- Accept --> B9[Finalize Pipe Spec]
    B8 -- Reject --> B10[Re-select Higher Schedule]
    B10 --> B7
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block тАФ Cross-Section Schematic of Pipe Wall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Pipe Cross Section View] --> C1[Outer Surface Do]
    C0 --> C2[Inner Surface Di]
    C0 --> C3[Wall Thickness t]
    C0 --> C4[Internal Pressure P]
    C4 --> C5[Acts Radially Outward]
    C5 --> C6[Generates Hoop Stress sigma h]
    C5 --> C7[Generates Longitudinal Stress sigma L]
    C6 --> C8[Tangential Direction]
    C7 --> C9[Axial Direction]
    C8 --> C10[Governing Stress for Thickness]
    C9 --> C11[Governing Stress for Branch Connections]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block тАФ Schedule Selection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Choose Schedule Number] --> D1[Sch 5S Thin Wall]
    D0 --> D2[Sch 10S to 40S Standard]
    D0 --> D3[Sch 80 to XXS Extra Strong]
    D1 --> D4[Low Pressure Service]
    D2 --> D5[Medium Pressure Service]
    D3 --> D6[High Pressure Service]
    D4 --> D7[Typical use: Instrument Air]
    D5 --> D8[Typical use: Process Piping]
    D6 --> D9[Typical use: Steam Headers]
```

### 4.5 Physical Diagram Description (For Examiner Notes)

For a textbook diagram of pipe cross-section, the student should visualise:

```
        <----  D_o  ---->
        |===============|       тЖР Outer Surface (Do)
        |               |
        |   P (internal)|       тЖР Pressure acts radially
        |   ---->       |          outward on inner wall
        |               |
        |===============|       тЖР Inner Surface (Di)
        
        Wall thickness t = (Do тИТ Di) / 2
        Hoop stress sigma_h = P ┬╖ Di / (2t)
```

> **Reading Note:** The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin pipe. Hence, code thickness formulas are derived from hoop stress, the governing failure mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Nominal Pipe Size" (NPS). Why is it called "nominal" and not the actual outside diameter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** NPS is a North American dimensionless designator for pipe size, identified by a number approximately equal to the inside diameter in inches (for NPS 1 to 12) or equal to the outside diameter in inches (for NPS 14 and above).
- **Why "nominal" (1 mark):** It is an *identifier*, not a measured dimension. The actual inside diameter changes with the schedule (wall thickness), while the **outside diameter remains fixed** for a given NPS.
- **Example (1 mark):** NPS 4 has OD = 114.3 mm in all schedules (Sch 40, Sch 80, etc.), but the inside diameter reduces as the wall thickness increases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**
*What is the difference between "pressure design thickness" and "required thickness" in ASME B31.3?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Pressure design thickness $t_m$ (1 mark):** The bare minimum thickness required to resist the design internal pressure alone, computed using the B31.3 formula $t_m = P D_o / (2 S E + P)$.
- **Required thickness $t_{req}$ (1 mark):** Pressure design thickness **plus** the corrosion and mechanical allowances: $t_{req} = t_m + c$.
- **Why the difference (1 mark):** Pipes in service suffer material loss due to corrosion, erosion, and threading. The extra allowance ensures the pipe still has $t_m$ of effective steel after years of service.

---

### 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ KTU ESE Model Question**

**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]**

A NPS 8, Schedule 40, ASTM A106 Grade B seamless pipe ($E = 1.0$) is used in a process plant. Design pressure = **5.5 MPa**, design temperature = **150 ┬░C**. The fluid is non-corrosive. Outside diameter for NPS 8 = **219.08 mm**, nominal wall thickness for Sch 40 = **8.18 mm**. Allowable stress for A106-B at 150 ┬░C = **118 MPa**. Mill tolerance = 12.5 %.

Use **ASME B31.3** ($F = 1$, $Y = 0$). Answer the following:

**(a) Calculate the required pipe wall thickness. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**(b) Verify if the selected Schedule 40 pipe is adequate. If not, suggest the next higher standard schedule and re-verify. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**

1. Code: ASME B31.3 (Process Piping).
2. Pipe is seamless тЖТ $E = 1.0$.
3. No corrosion тЖТ $c = 0$.
4. Design factor $F = 1.0$ (B31.3 implicit).
5. Coefficient $Y = 0$ (since temperature < 510 ┬░C, ferritic steel).

**Pressure Design Thickness (3 marks):**

$$t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E + P}$$

Substituting values:

$$t_m = \dfrac{5.5 \times 219.08}{2 \times 118 \times 1.0 + 5.5}$$

$$t_m = \dfrac{1204.94}{236 + 5.5} = \dfrac{1204.94}{241.5}$$

$$t_m = 4.989 \text{ mm}$$

**Required Thickness (2 marks):**

Since $c = 0$ (non-corrosive service):

$$t_{req} = t_m + c = 4.989 + 0 = 4.989 \text{ mm}$$

**Statement of Result (1 mark):**
Required wall thickness = **4.989 mm**.

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (b):**

**Available Thickness After Mill Tolerance (3 marks):**

$$t_{min,available} = t_{nom} \times (1 - 0.125)$$

$$t_{min,available} = 8.18 \times 0.875 = 7.158 \text{ mm}$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

$$t_{min,available} = 7.158 \text{ mm} \;>\; t_{req} = 4.989 \text{ mm}$$

Margin = $7.158 - 4.989 = 2.169$ mm (тЙИ 30 % excess).

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

тЬЕ **NPS 8, Schedule 40 is SAFE and ADEQUATE** for the given service condition. The excess margin is acceptable; no need to upgrade to a higher schedule.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall тАФ Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mill tolerance:** Many students compare $t_{nom}$ directly with $t_{req}$ without applying the 12.5 % negative tolerance. This overestimates available thickness and may lead to incorrect "upgrade" recommendations.
> 2. **Wrong code:** Mixing B31.1 (with $F$) and B31.3 (without $F$) тАФ examiners deduct **2 marks** for using the wrong formula.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Using $P$ in psi and $D$ in mm without conversion тАФ always convert to consistent units (MPa & mm recommended).
> 4. **Ignoring $E$:** Forgetting weld joint reduction factor when pipe is welded (not seamless).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**

A saturated steam line in a boiler plant operates at design pressure = **3.5 MPa** (gauge) and design temperature = **350 ┬░C**. Pipe material is ASTM A335 P11 (seamless), $E = 1.0$. Pipe size is **NPS 10, Schedule 80** with $D_o = 273.05$ mm and nominal thickness = **12.7 mm**. Allowable stress for A335 P11 at 350 ┬░C = **86 MPa**. Corrosion allowance = **1.5 mm**. Mill tolerance = 12.5 %.

Governing code: **ASME B31.1 Power Piping**. For seamless ferritic steel at this temperature, $F = 1.0$, $Y = 0$.

**(a) Determine the pressure design thickness and the total required thickness. (7 marks)**

**(b) Check adequacy of the chosen pipe and compute the safety margin in percentage. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (a):**

**Given Inputs (1 mark):**

$$P = 3.5 \text{ MPa},\quad D_o = 273.05 \text{ mm},\quad S = 86 \text{ MPa},\quad E = 1.0,\quad F = 1.0$$

**Formula (B31.1) (1 mark):**

$$t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F + P}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$t_m = \dfrac{3.5 \times 273.05}{2 \times 86 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 + 3.5}$$

$$t_m = \dfrac{955.675}{172 + 3.5} = \dfrac{955.675}{175.5}$$

$$t_m = 5.446 \text{ mm}$$

**Total Required Thickness (2 marks):**

$$t_{req} = t_m + c = 5.446 + 1.5 = 6.946 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### **Model Solution тАФ Part (b):**

**Minimum Available Thickness (3 marks):**

$$t_{min,available} = 12.7 \times 0.875 = 11.113 \text{ mm}$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

$$t_{min,available} = 11.113 \text{ mm} \;>\; t_{req} = 6.946 \text{ mm}$$

**Safety Margin (2 marks):**

$$\text{Margin \%} = \dfrac{t_{min,available} - t_{req}}{t_{req}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Margin \%} = \dfrac{11.113 - 6.946}{6.946} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.167}{6.946} \times 100 = 60.0 \text{ %}$$

**Conclusion:**
тЬЕ **NPS 10, Schedule 80 is SAFE.** A 60 % margin over the required thickness indicates the pipe is significantly over-designed, possibly justifying a downgrade to Schedule 40 for cost savings тАФ but this is an **economic decision**, not a code violation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- тЬЕ **NPS vs OD:** NPS is a *nominal label*; OD is the *fixed measured diameter* (e.g., NPS 6 тЖТ OD = 168.28 mm always).
- тЬЕ **Schedule Number:** Higher schedule = thicker wall = higher pressure rating. Common schedules: 5S, 10S, 10, 20, 40S, 40, 80S, 80, 120, 160, XXS.
- тЬЕ **Master Formula тАФ B31.1:** $t_m = P D_o / (2 S E F + P)$.
- тЬЕ **Master Formula тАФ B31.3:** $t_m = P D_o / (2 S E + P)$ тАФ **no F factor**.
- тЬЕ **Allowance Chain:** $t_{req} = t_m + c$ where $c$ = corrosion + mechanical.
- тЬЕ **Mill Tolerance:** Always reduce available thickness by **12.5 %** for safety comparison.
- тЬЕ **Weld Efficiency $E$:** Seamless = **1.0**; ERW = **0.85 to 0.95** depending on NDE.
- тЬЕ **Design Factor $F$:** B31.1 ferritic = **1.0**; austenitic SS at high T = **0.85**; cast iron = **0.80**.
- тЬЕ **Y Coefficient:** B31.3 ferritic at $T \ge 510$ ┬░C uses $Y = 0.4$ to account for creep.
- тЬЕ **Design Pressure Rule:** $P_{design} = P_{max\,operating} + \text{margin}$ тАФ never size for normal operating pressure alone.
- тЬЕ **Allowable Stress Rule:** Always pick the *lower* of hot allowable (at design T) and cold allowable (at ambient).
- тЬЕ **Failure Mode:** Thickness is governed by **hoop stress**, which is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin pipe.
- тЬЕ **Units:** Always use **MPa + mm** for SI consistency; convert from psi or inches only after stating the conversion explicitly.
- тЬЕ **Common Error:** Comparing $t_{nom}$ with $t_{req}$ without mill tolerance = **up to 2 marks lost** in valuation.
- тЬЕ **Code Selection Rule:** Boiler/Steam тЖТ **B31.1**; Refinery/Process тЖТ **B31.3**; Cross-country pipeline тЖТ **B31.4**; Gas transmission тЖТ **B31.8**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Trick:**
> *"**P**ressure **D**esign = **P D / (2 S E F + P)** тАФ remember P D over 2-SEF+P."*

---
