<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sizing and Thickness Calculation of Pipes (ASME B31.3 Orientation)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Compliant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Sizing** is the iterative engineering process of selecting a Nominal Pipe Size (NPS) and Schedule that simultaneously satisfies three competing constraints: (1) adequate hydraulic flow capacity (pressure drop тЙд allowable), (2) sufficient structural wall thickness to withstand internal pressure per **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping) Clause 304.1.2**, and (3) economic viability.

The **minimum required thickness** for a straight pipe under internal pressure per ASME B31.3 (Eq. 3a) is governed by the modified Barlow equation, derived from membrane stress equilibrium on a thin-walled cylindrical shell:

$$
t_m \;=\; \frac{P \cdot D}{2\,(S \cdot E \cdot W \;+\; P \cdot Y)}
$$

where every symbol has a code-stipulated meaning that **must** be selected from code tables (not assumed).

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Water Balloon Inside a Steel Tube"

Imagine filling a long steel tube with pressurized water. The water pushes radially outward on the pipe wall with pressure **P**, trying to split the pipe lengthwise (longitudinal stress) and burst it radially (hoop stress). A **thicker** wall is like a thicker rubber balloon тАФ it resists bursting. The pipe must also carry **weight**, **thermal expansion**, and **cyclic pressure surges**, so the *minimum* thickness calculated from pressure alone is then **augmented** with corrosion allowance, mill tolerance, and a safety margin before ordering the pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Code Boundary**: ASME B31.3 governs *process piping* (chemical, refinery, cryogenic, etc.), while **ASME B31.1** governs *power piping* (boiler external, turbine cycle). The thickness equations are *similar in form* but use **different coefficient values** (notably the Y-coefficient and stress values).

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Maximum allowable stress S** = tabulated value from **ASME B31.3 Table A-1** (for ferritic steel at design temperature тЙд 38 ┬░C, S тЙИ 138 MPa / 20,000 psi).
- **Longitudinal joint efficiency E** = **1.00** for seamless pipe; **0.85тАУ1.00** for welded.
- **Weld joint strength reduction factor W** = **1.00** for P тЙд 0.5 ├Ч SMYS (per B31.3 302.4.3).
- **Y-coefficient** (Table 304.1.1) = **0.4** for ferritic steel below 482 ┬░C.
- **Mill tolerance** = **тАУ12.5 %** (negative tolerance on schedule thickness per ANSI B36.10).
- **Corrosion allowance c** = **0тАУ3 mm** typical (process-specific).

---

## 1.2 Allowable and Displacement Stress Range for Expected Cyclic Life

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Allowable Stress Range, S<sub>A</sub>** is the maximum permissible value of the sum of expansion stresses (S<sub>h</sub>) plus a factor times the sustained stress range (S<sub>c</sub>), which a piping system can tolerate for a design number of full thermal cycles without producing fatigue failure at any bolted joint, fillet weld, or pipe bend.

The governing equation per **ASME B31.3 Clause 302.3.5(d)** is:

$$
S_A \;=\; f\,(1.25\,S_c + S_h)
$$

where **f** is the *Stress Range Reduction Factor* selected from **Table 302.3.5** based on the *expected equivalent number of full thermal cycles* over the plant's design life.

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Slinky That Gets Tired"

If you stretch a metal coil (a pipe that gets hot тЖТ expands тЖТ gets cold тЖТ contracts) only **10 times**, it barely fatigues. But if you stretch it **10,000 times**, microscopic cracks initiate at stress-concentration points (elbow intrados, weld toes). The Code quantifies this with a **fatigue endurance curve**: more cycles тЗТ smaller allowable stress range.

> [!NOTE]
> A "**full equivalent cycle**" is one complete hot-to-cold-to-hot transition. Partial cycles are converted to equivalent full cycles using the **Miner's rule of cumulative damage** (linear damage hypothesis).

### Standard Cyclic Life Allowables for Carbon & Low Alloy Steel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 302.3.5 (ASME B31.3) тАФ S<sub>A</sub> vs Number of Cycles**

| Equivalent Full Cycles, N | S<sub>A</sub> (MPa) | S<sub>A</sub> (psi) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 7,000 and less | **124** | **18,000** |
| 7,000 < N тЙд 14,000 | **118** | **17,100** |
| 14,000 < N тЙд 22,000 | **112** | **16,200** |
| 22,000 < N тЙд 45,000 | **105** | **15,200** |
| 45,000 < N тЙд 100,000 | **93** | **13,500** |

---

## 1.3 Stress Intensification Factor (SIF) and Flexibility Factor (k)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stress Intensification Factor, i** (also denoted i<sub>c</sub> in some texts), is a dimensionless multiplier that quantifies the additional *bending stress* generated in a piping component (elbow, tee, bend, reducer) **relative to** the bending stress in a straight pipe of the same nominal dimensions under the same end moments. It captures the geometric "stress concentration" inherent to non-straight geometry.

The **Flexibility Factor, k**, is the *companion* factor that quantifies how much *more* a fitting deflects (rotates) under a moment than a straight pipe of equivalent length:

$$
\text{Actual bending stress in fitting} \;=\; i \times \sigma_{\text{nominal straight pipe}}
$$

$$
\text{Actual rotation of fitting} \;=\; \dfrac{\theta_{\text{fitting}}}{k \cdot \theta_{\text{straight}}}
$$

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Hurdle vs The Straight Track"

Imagine a runner carrying a heavy bucket. On a **straight path**, the runner's arms swing naturally. On a **curved hurdle**, the bucket swings wider and the runner's elbows experience *higher local stress* due to the curvature тАФ but the runner can also **absorb the curve more easily** (more flexibility). That is exactly the **i (stress тЖС) vs k (flexibility тЖС)** dichotomy of pipe elbows.

### Code Values тАФ Selected from ASME B31.3 Table 304.7.1 (Planar)

| Fitting Type | SIF, **i** | Flexibility Factor, **k** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 90┬░ Long-Radius Elbow (R = 1.5 D) | $0.9 / h^{2/3}$ | $1.65 / h$ |
| 90┬░ Short-Radius Elbow (R = 1.0 D) | $0.9 / h^{2/3}$ | $1.65 / h$ |
| 90┬░ Miter Bend (single, ╬╕ тЙд 22.5┬░) | $0.9 / h^{2/3}$ | $1.65 / h$ |
| 90┬░ Butt-Welded Elbow | $0.9 / h^{2/3}$ | $1.65 / h$ |
| Equal Tee (per ASME B31.3) | $0.9 / h^{2/3}$ | $1.0$ |
| Concentric Reducer | $0.5 + 0.4 \sqrt{r/x}$ | $1.0$ |
| Reinforced Fabricated Tee | per Table | per Table |

where the **flexibility characteristic h** is defined as:

$$
h \;=\; \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2}
$$

with **t** = nominal wall thickness, **R** = bend radius, **r** = mean pipe radius.

---

## 1.4 Flexibility Analysis (Clause 119.7.1 / 319.4.1)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexibility Analysis** is the structural evaluation of a piping system subjected to *displacement loads* (predominantly thermal expansion, plus anchor movements, support settlement, etc.) to verify that the resulting stresses and reactions do not exceed code-allowable limits and that the system's *thermal movements* are safely absorbed without exceeding equipment nozzle loads.

Two distinct code formulations are required reading for KTU:

**ASME B31.1, Clause 119.7.1 тАФ Eq. (11) "Sp = SL + Sb" Check:**

$$
S_P \;=\; S_L + S_b \;\leq\; S_{L,\text{allow}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here S<sub>L</sub> = sustained longitudinal stress from weight + pressure, and S<sub>b</sub> = displacement (thermal) stress range *after* multiplying by SIF and dividing by k in the flexibility analysis. The sum must not exceed the code allowable.

**ASME B31.3, Clause 319.4.1 тАФ Eq. (30) Displacement Stress Range:**

$$
S_E \;=\; \sqrt{S_b^2 + 4 S_t^2 + 4 S_b S_t} \;\leq\; S_A
$$

where S<sub>b</sub> = resultant bending stress (displacement, including SIF), S<sub>t</sub> = torsional stress (displacement), and S<sub>A</sub> = allowable stress range from ┬з1.2 above.

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Snake in a Box"

A piping system constrained between two fixed points (like a snake in a closed box) wants to expand when heated. If the geometry is too *rigid*, large reaction forces and bending stresses develop at elbows and at equipment nozzles тАФ potentially *rupturing* the pipe or *overloading* the connected pump/tank. Flexibility analysis proves the system is *flexible enough* (i.e., has enough "give" via elbows and loops) to absorb thermal growth without damage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Flow of Pipe Sizing (ASME B31.3)

The Code-mandated sequence for sizing a process pipe:

1. **Establish design inputs**: internal design pressure P, design temperature T, fluid service (MOC), corrosion allowance c, cyclic life N.
2. **Select candidate NPS** from hydraulic / process flow requirement (line sizing chart).
3. **Compute minimum required thickness t<sub>m</sub>** from pressure using Eq. (3a).
4. **Apply mill tolerance (тАУ12.5 %)** to obtain *minimum* ordered thickness.
5. **Add corrosion allowance c** to obtain *design thickness t*.
6. **Select next-higher schedule** from ASME B36.10 / B36.19 tables.
7. **Verify** the selected NPS-Schedule against **vacuum**, **external pressure**, and **cyclic** requirements.
8. **Re-iterate** if any check fails.

## 2.2 Rationale Behind Each Coefficient (The "Why")

| Symbol | Why it exists | Engineering meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **P** | Internal pressure that physically loads the wall. | Design pressure (тЙе MAWP). |
| **D** | Larger diameter тЗТ larger hoop stress for same P. | Outside diameter (OD) per ASME B36.10. |
| **S** | Material weakens as T increases (creep, yield drop). | Code-tabulated allowable. |
| **E** | Welded pipe has weaker seam than parent metal. | Joint efficiency (weld inspection). |
| **W** | Weld zones may have HAZ softening in certain alloys. | Strength reduction factor (B31.3 302.4.3). |
| **Y** | Two-axis stress state (hoop + axial) allows wall thinning. | Temperature coefficient (Table 304.1.1). |

## 2.3 The Flexibility Analysis "Why"

The analysis **exists** because:

- **(a)** Piping systems are *statically indeterminate* (multiple unknowns > equilibrium equations). Temperature change produces axial force *if* constrained.
- **(b)** Thermal expansion forces can be **enormous** тАФ a fully restrained carbon-steel pipe at ╬ФT = 200 K develops ~250 MPa axial stress (тЙИ 100,000 ├Ч ╬╡_thermal ├Ч E).
- **(c)** Equipment nozzles (pump casings, turbine casings, vessel shells) have **strict reaction-force limits** published by vendors (e.g., API 610 for pumps).
- **(d)** Without geometric "give" (elbows, loops, expansion joints), the pipe will either **rupture** or **rip off its flange**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE тАФ All Formulas Required for Module 4**

| # | Formula | ASME Reference | Purpose / When to Use |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $t_m = \dfrac{P D}{2(S E W + P Y)}$ | B31.3 Eq. (3a) | Pressure design thickness |
| 2 | $t_m = \dfrac{P D}{2 S E + 1.2 P}$ | B31.1 Eq. (3) | Power piping thickness |
| 3 | $t_{\text{nom}} \geq \dfrac{t_m + c}{1 - 0.125}$ | B31.3 304.1.2 | With corrosion + mill tolerance |
| 4 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | Membrane theory | Hoop (circumferential) stress |
| 5 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | Membrane theory | Longitudinal stress (closed ends) |
| 6 | $S_A = f(1.25 S_c + S_h)$ | B31.3 Eq. (1a) | Allowable stress range |
| 7 | $i = \dfrac{0.9}{h^{2/3}}$ (elbows) | B31.3 Table 304.7.1 | SIF тАФ long-radius elbow |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{1.65}{h}$ (elbows) | B31.3 Table 304.7.1 | Flexibility factor тАФ elbow |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{t R}{r^2}$ | B31.3 304.7.2 | Flexibility characteristic |
| 10 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{i \cdot M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{i \cdot M}{Z}$ | B31.3 319.2.4 | Bending stress in fitting |
| 11 | $S_P = S_L + S_b \leq S_L$ (B31.1 Eq. 11) | B31.1 119.7.1 | Sustained + displacement sum |
| 12 | $S_E = \sqrt{S_b^2 + 4 S_t^2 + 4 S_b S_t} \leq S_A$ | B31.3 319.4.1 | Resultant displacement range |
| 13 | $\Delta_x = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | Free thermal growth |
| 14 | $N_{\text{eq}} = \sum N_i \left(\dfrac{S_i}{S_{\max}}\right)^m$ | Miner's rule | Equivalent cycle conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - If **Y = 0.4** тЖТ ferritic steel, T < 482 ┬░C.
> - If **P > SMYS ├Ч 0.4 ├Ч E** тЖТ use Eq. (3b) (thick-wall correction, but B31.3 typically limits via Eq. (3a) plus code caps).
> - If **╬ФT тЙд 0** (cold condition) тЖТ flexibility analysis may be waived if system is naturally flexible (B31.3 322.2.1 Simplified Analysis).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Application |
|:---|:---|
| **Refineries & Petrochemicals** | Process interconnects between reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers. |
| **Power Plants (B31.1)** | Steam lines between boiler and turbine HP/LP cylinders; hot reheat lines. |
| **LNG / Cryogenic** | Cold piping where contraction is the dominant displacement. |
| **Nuclear** | ASME B31.1 + ASME Section III (Class 1, 2, 3) тАФ strictest SIF / fatigue limits. |
| **Onshore / Offshore Pipelines** | ASME B31.4 / B31.8 govern (not in Module 4 scope). |

---

## 2.6 Displacement Stress Calculation тАФ Full Derivation of S<sub>E</sub>

The resultant *displacement* stress at any point in a fitting is the *vector sum* of bending and torsional contributions:

**Bending stress component:**
$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{total}} \cdot c}{I} \;=\; \frac{M_b}{Z}
$$

where M<sub>b</sub> is the resultant bending moment *including SIF*: M<sub>b</sub> = i ├Ч M<sub>applied</sub>.

**Torsional stress component:**
$$
\sigma_t \;=\; \frac{T \cdot r}{J} \;=\; \frac{T}{2 Z}
$$

**Vector combination** (B31.3 ┬з319.4.1 forms S<sub>E</sub> from these orthogonal stress states; the factor 4 on S<sub>t</sub><sup>2</sup> comes from the Tresca / shear-energy distinction):

$$
S_E \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} + 4\,\sigma_t^{\,2} + 4\,\sigma_b\,\sigma_t}
$$

This must satisfy S<sub>E</sub> тЙд S<sub>A</sub> (the cyclic-allowable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention**: ╧Г<sub>b</sub> and ╧Г<sub>t</sub> are entered as *magnitudes* into S<sub>E</sub>. The cross-term +4 ╧Г<sub>b</sub> ╧Г<sub>t</sub> is **always positive** in B31.3 because the Code conservatively assumes worst-case phase alignment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pressure Design Thickness (B31.3 Eq. 3a)

### Step 0 тАФ Physical Setup

Consider a thin-walled cylindrical pipe of **outside diameter D** and **wall thickness t**. It is subjected to internal pressure **P**. The Code treats the pipe as a membrane (no bending across the wall).

### Step 1 тАФ Free-Body Cut Along a Longitudinal Plane

Cut the pipe along a longitudinal plane (length L). The internal pressure acts on the projected area = D ├Ч L, producing a bursting force F:

$$
F \;=\; P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Resisting Force from Two Wall Strips

The two cut edges each have cross-sectional area = t ├Ч L. The *hoop (circumferential) stress* ╧Г<sub>h</sub> resists the burst:

$$
2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \;=\; P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Solve for Hoop Stress

$$
\sigma_h \;=\; \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}
$$

This is the **Barlow equation** (1866). It is the *primary membrane stress*.

### Step 4 тАФ Introduce Allowable Stress with Code Factors

The Code reduces the allowable by *joint efficiency E*, *weld strength factor W*, and a *two-axis plastic-collapse factor* (1 + P┬╖Y/S) on the denominator:

$$
\sigma_h \;\leq\; \frac{S \cdot E \cdot W}{1 + \dfrac{P \cdot Y}{S \cdot E \cdot W}} \;\cdot\; 2 \quad (\text{per Code limit})
$$

Equivalently, solving for **t**:

$$
t \;\geq\; \frac{P \cdot D}{2\,(S \cdot E \cdot W + P \cdot Y)} \;\equiv\; t_m
$$

This is **ASME B31.3 Eq. (3a)**.

### Step 5 тАФ Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example тАФ Pressure Design Thickness Calculation**
>
> **Given**: NPS 8 (D = 219.1 mm), P = 4.0 MPa (gauge), T = 150 ┬░C, ASTM A106 Gr. B seamless pipe, corrosion allowance c = 1.5 mm, expected cycles N = 5,000 (use f = 1.0).
>
> **Material/Code Properties**:
> - S = 138 MPa (Table A-1, A106 Gr.B at 150 ┬░C)
> - E = 1.0 (seamless)
> - W = 1.0 (P < 0.5 ├Ч SMYS, since SMYS = 240 MPa тЗТ 0.5 ├Ч 240 = 120 > 4 MPa тЬУ)
> - Y = 0.4 (Table 304.1.1)

**Step 5a тАФ Compute t<sub>m</sub>**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2\,(S \cdot E \cdot W + P \cdot Y)} \\
&= \frac{(4.0)(219.1)}{2\bigl[(138)(1.0)(1.0) + (4.0)(0.4)\bigr]} \\
&= \frac{876.4}{2 \cdot [138 + 1.6]} \\
&= \frac{876.4}{2 \cdot 139.6} \\
&= \frac{876.4}{279.2} \\
&= 3.139 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5b тАФ Include corrosion allowance and mill tolerance**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{design}} &= \frac{t_m + c}{1 - 0.125} \\
&= \frac{3.139 + 1.5}{0.875} \\
&= \frac{4.639}{0.875} \\
&= 5.302 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5c тАФ Select schedule from ASME B36.10**:

For NPS 8, Schedule 40 gives **t = 8.18 mm**, Schedule 20 gives 6.35 mm, Schedule 10 gives 4.78 mm. Schedule 20 (6.35 mm) is the *lightest commercially available* schedule exceeding 5.302 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> The selected thickness t = 6.35 mm > 5.302 mm, so the design is acceptable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Stress Intensification Factor (i) for a Long-Radius Elbow

### Step 1 тАФ Geometric Definition

A long-radius elbow has bend radius R = 1.5 D<sub>nominal</sub>. Define the **flexibility characteristic**:

$$
h \;=\; \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2}
$$

where r = (D тИТ t)/2 is the *mean radius*.

### Step 2 тАФ Empirical Origin (B31.3 / B31.1)

The Code does not derive i *analytically* тАФ it tabulates values fitted from **Markl's experiments (1934тАУ1957)** on pressurized pipe loops at elevated temperature. The closed-form approximation for elbows is:

$$
i \;=\; \frac{0.9}{h^{2/3}}
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example тАФ Compute i and k for NPS 8 SCH 40 Long-Radius Elbow**
>
> **Given**: D = 219.1 mm, t = 8.18 mm, R = 1.5 ├Ч 219.1 = 328.65 mm.

**Step 3a тАФ Compute r**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{D - t}{2} \\
&= \frac{219.1 - 8.18}{2} \\
&= \frac{210.92}{2} \\
&= 105.46 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b тАФ Compute h**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2} \\
&= \frac{(8.18)(328.65)}{(105.46)^2} \\
&= \frac{2688.36}{11121.81} \\
&= 0.2417
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3c тАФ Compute i**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \frac{0.9}{h^{2/3}} \\
&= \frac{0.9}{(0.2417)^{2/3}} \\
&= \frac{0.9}{0.3877} \\
&\approx 2.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3d тАФ Compute k**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{1.65}{h} \\
&= \frac{1.65}{0.2417} \\
&\approx 6.83
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: This elbow has *2.32├Ч* higher bending stress and *6.83├Ч* higher rotation than an equivalent length of straight pipe under the same end moment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Allowable Stress Range S<sub>A</sub>

### Step 1 тАФ Origin of the 1.25 Factor

The Code conservatively multiplies the sustained stress S<sub>c</sub> by **1.25** because:

- Sustained (weight + pressure) stress is *not fully reversed* during thermal cycles (offset). The Code combines an *offset mean stress* with a *fully reversed cyclic component* using a simplified **Goodman-type** correction:

$$
\sigma_{\text{eq, range}} = S_{\text{mean}} \cdot \eta + S_{\text{alt}}
$$

where the empirical factor 1.25 corresponds to ╬╖ тЙИ 0.25 for typical carbon-steel pipelines.

### Step 2 тАФ The Stress Range Factor f

For N тЙд 7,000 cycles: **f = 1.0** (use full S<sub>A</sub>).
For 7,000 < N тЙд 100,000: **f < 1.0** (decreases with cycles).
For N > 100,000: detailed fatigue analysis using **ASME Section VIII Div 2** or **Section III** curves is required.

### Step 3 тАФ Worked Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example тАФ Allowable Stress Range Determination**
>
> **Given**: 12,000 expected equivalent full thermal cycles in 30 years of operation, ASTM A106 Gr. B pipe.

**Step 3a тАФ Read S<sub>A</sub> from Table 302.3.5**:

For 7,000 < N тЙд 14,000:

$$
S_A = 118 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{or } 17{,}100 \text{ psi})
$$

**Step 3b тАФ If f is required explicitly**:

$$
S_A \;=\; f\,(1.25\,S_c + S_h)
$$

where **f = 1.0** (since the Code has already de-rated the absolute value in the table).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of B31.1 Clause 119.7.1 тАФ Equation (11) Check

### Step 1 тАФ Total Stress Decomposition

The total longitudinal stress at a section = sustained + displacement:

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{sustained}} + \sigma_{\text{displacement}}
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Convert to Code Variables

$$
S_P \;=\; S_L + S_b
$$

where:
- S<sub>L</sub> = sustained longitudinal stress from weight, pressure, and other sustained loads (computed without SIF, per B31.1 119.6).
- S<sub>b</sub> = *displacement* stress range from thermal expansion (computed *with* SIF and divided by k when iterating).

### Step 3 тАФ Code Limit (B31.1 Eq. 11)

$$
S_P \;\leq\; k \cdot S_L \quad \text{where } k = 1.0 \text{ (typical)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error**: Students confuse B31.1 (which *adds* stresses and checks against allowable) with B31.3 (which computes an *equivalent stress range* and checks against S<sub>A</sub>). They are *different formulations* and must not be mixed.

---

## 3.5 Complete Numerical Worked Example тАФ Flexibility Check (B31.3 319.4.1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Problem тАФ Verify Flexibility of a Steam Line Elbow**
>
> **Given**:
> - NPS 6 SCH 40 pipe (D = 168.3 mm, t = 7.11 mm, r = 80.6 mm)
> - Operating T = 300 ┬░C, install T = 25 ┬░C тЗТ ╬ФT = 275 K
> - ╬▒ = 1.2 ├Ч 10<sup>тИТ5</sup> /K (carbon steel)
> - Long-radius elbow (R = 1.5 D = 252.45 mm)
> - S = 110 MPa at 300 ┬░C
> - Pressure P = 2.5 MPa
> - Expected cycles: 8,000 тЗТ f = 1.0 (Table 302.3.5 тЗТ 7,000 < N тЙд 14,000 тЗТ S<sub>A</sub> = 118 MPa)

### Step 1 тАФ Compute h, i, k

$$
h = \frac{(7.11)(252.45)}{(80.6)^2} = \frac{1794.92}{6496.36} = 0.2763
$$

$$
i = \frac{0.9}{(0.2763)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.9}{0.4228} = 2.13
$$

$$
k = \frac{1.65}{0.2763} = 5.97
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Section Modulus Z

$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - (D-2t)^4}{D} = \frac{\pi}{32}\cdot\frac{(168.3)^4 - (154.08)^4}{168.3}
$$

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^4 &= (168.3)^4 = 8.0106 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 \\
(D-2t)^4 &= (154.08)^4 = 5.6337 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 \\
\Delta &= 2.3769 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 \\
Z &= \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{2.3769 \times 10^8}{168.3} \\
&= 0.09817 \cdot 1.4123 \times 10^6 \\
&= 1.3864 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 = 138.64 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Free Thermal Growth of Adjacent Straight Run (L = 10 m)

$$
\Delta x = \alpha L \Delta T = (1.2 \times 10^{-5})(10{,}000)(275) = 33.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 тАФ Bending Moment at the Elbow (Simplified Cantilever Model)

Assuming a single elbow at the pipe end absorbs the growth:

$$
M \;=\; \frac{6 E I \cdot \Delta x}{L^2}
$$

For carbon steel E = 2.0 ├Ч 10<sup>5</sup> MPa, I = Z ├Ч (D/2) = (1.3864 ├Ч 10<sup>5</sup> ├Ч 84.15) = 1.167 ├Ч 10<sup>7</sup> mm<sup>4</sup>.

$$
M = \frac{6 \cdot (2.0 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.167 \times 10^7) \cdot 33.0}{(10{,}000)^2} = \frac{4.620 \times 10^{14}}{1.0 \times 10^8} = 4.62 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬╖mm} = 4.62 \text{ kN┬╖m}
$$

### Step 5 тАФ Bending Stress (with SIF)

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{i \cdot M}{Z} = \frac{2.13 \cdot 4.62 \times 10^6}{1.3864 \times 10^5} = 71.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 6 тАФ Sustained Stress (Weight + Pressure, neglected torsion, S<sub>t</sub> = 0)

$$
\sigma_L \approx \frac{P D}{4 t} + \sigma_{\text{weight}} \approx \frac{(2.5)(168.3)}{4 \cdot 7.11} \approx 14.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

(Weight stress for a steam line is typically 2тАУ5 MPa тАФ ignore for this illustrative check.)

### Step 7 тАФ Compute S<sub>E</sub> per B31.3 319.4.1

$$
S_E = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4 \sigma_t^2 + 4 \sigma_b \sigma_t} = \sigma_b = 71.0 \text{ MPa (since } \sigma_t = 0)
$$

### Step 8 тАФ Check Against Allowable

$$
S_E = 71.0 \text{ MPa} \;\leq\; S_A = 118 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The elbow passes with significant margin (~40 %). If we had forgotten the SIF (i.e., used ╧Г<sub>b</sub> = 33.3 MPa instead of 71.0 MPa), the design would have appeared *artificially safer*. This is the **most common KTU exam pitfall** тАФ omitting i.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Conversion Table тАФ Miner's Rule

For mixed-cycle loading, the **equivalent full cycle count** at a reference stress S<sub>max</sub> is:

$$
N_{\text{eq}} \;=\; \sum_{i} N_i \cdot \left(\frac{S_i}{S_{\max}}\right)^m
$$

where **m тЙИ 3** for ferritic steels (Coffin-Manson exponent).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Example**: A pipe sees 2,000 cycles at 90 MPa and 1,000 cycles at 120 MPa. Reference at S<sub>max</sub> = 120 MPa:
>
> $$
> N_{\text{eq}} = 2000 \cdot \left(\frac{90}{120}\right)^3 + 1000 \cdot \left(\frac{120}{120}\right)^3 = 2000 \cdot 0.4219 + 1000 = 1844 \text{ cycles}
> $$
>
> So a total of 3,000 actual cycles is equivalent to 1,844 full-range cycles (lower fatigue damage).

# SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**Q:** State and explain the **Barlow equation** used for pipe wall thickness calculation in ASME B31.3. Define each term clearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
>
> The **minimum required thickness** for a straight pipe under internal pressure per **ASME B31.3 Eq. (3a)** is:
>
> $$
> t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2\,(S \cdot E \cdot W + P \cdot Y)}
> $$
>
> Where:
> - **t<sub>m</sub>** = minimum required thickness (mm or in)
> - **P** = internal design pressure (MPa or psi)
> - **D** = outside diameter of pipe (mm or in)
> - **S** = allowable stress at design temperature (from ASME B31.3 Table A-1)
> - **E** = longitudinal joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless; 0.85тАУ1.00 for welded)
> - **W** = weld joint strength reduction factor
> - **Y** = coefficient from Table 304.1.1 (0.4 for ferritic steel < 482 ┬░C)
>
> *(1 mark equation, 2 marks term definitions)*

---

### Question A2
**Q:** Define **Stress Intensification Factor (i)** and **Flexibility Factor (k)**. Why are they *different* for the same fitting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
>
> - **Stress Intensification Factor, i**: A dimensionless multiplier that accounts for the *additional bending stress* generated at a non-straight fitting (elbow, tee, miter) compared to a straight pipe of the same nominal dimensions under the same end moment.
> - **Flexibility Factor, k**: A dimensionless multiplier that quantifies how much *more easily* a fitting *deflects or rotates* under a moment compared to a straight pipe of equivalent length.
> - They are *different* because a curved/branched geometry simultaneously **concentrates stress** (high i) *and* **softens the structure** (high k). The two are independent elastic properties measured in Markl-type tests.
>
> *(1 mark each definition + 1 mark distinction)*

---

## Part B Question (14 Marks) тАФ Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Standard**: The question paper offers **two full-mark alternative choices**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B completely.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A process plant has an NPS 10 (D = 273.05 mm) ASTM A106 Gr. B pipe carrying steam at **P = 5.0 MPa, T = 250 ┬░C**. The system will experience approximately **12,000 equivalent full thermal cycles** over its design life. The pipe is **seamless**, with corrosion allowance **c = 2.0 mm**. Determine:
   1. Minimum required thickness t<sub>m</sub> per ASME B31.3.
   2. Design thickness with corrosion + mill tolerance.
   3. The allowable stress range S<sub>A</sub>.
   4. The recommended pipe schedule.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long-radius 90┬░ elbow in NPS 6 SCH 40 (D = 168.3 mm, t = 7.11 mm, R = 252.45 mm) operates at **T = 350 ┬░C** (install at 30 ┬░C) and carries fluid at **P = 3.5 MPa**. S = 95 MPa at 350 ┬░C. Compute:
   1. Flexibility characteristic h, SIF i, and Flexibility Factor k.
   2. Resultant bending stress ╧Г<sub>b</sub> due to a thermal end-moment M = 5.0 kN┬╖m applied to the elbow.
   3. State (without full solution) whether B31.3 319.4.1 check is satisfied if S<sub>A</sub> = 118 MPa and torsion is negligible.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Solution**:

**Step 1 тАФ Material/Code Data**:

- S = 110 MPa (A106 Gr. B at 250 ┬░C, Table A-1)
- E = 1.0 (seamless)
- W = 1.0 (P < 0.5 ├Ч SMYS = 120 MPa, тЬУ)
- Y = 0.4 (ferritic, T < 482 ┬░C)

**Step 2 тАФ Compute t<sub>m</sub>**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m &= \frac{P D}{2(S E W + P Y)} \\
&= \frac{(5.0)(273.05)}{2\bigl[(110)(1.0)(1.0) + (5.0)(0.4)\bigr]} \\
&= \frac{1365.25}{2 \cdot [110 + 2.0]} \\
&= \frac{1365.25}{224.0} \\
&= 6.095 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Add corrosion allowance and mill tolerance**:

$$
t_{\text{design}} = \frac{t_m + c}{1 - 0.125} = \frac{6.095 + 2.0}{0.875} = \frac{8.095}{0.875} = 9.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Allowable Stress Range**:

For 7,000 < N = 12,000 тЙд 14,000:

$$
S_A = 118 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Schedule Selection (ASME B36.10 for NPS 10)**:

| Schedule | Wall Thickness (mm) | Pass/Fail |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| SCH 20 | 6.35 | тЬЧ (less than 9.25) |
| SCH 30 | 7.80 | тЬЧ |
| **SCH 40** | **9.27** | **тЬУ** |
| SCH 60 | 12.70 | тЬУ (over-designed) |

**Selected: NPS 10 SCH 40, t = 9.27 mm**.

---

**(b) Solution**:

**Step 1 тАФ Geometric Data**:

- D = 168.3 mm, t = 7.11 mm, R = 252.45 mm
- Mean radius r = (D тИТ t)/2 = (168.3 тИТ 7.11)/2 = 80.60 mm

**Step 2 тАФ Flexibility characteristic h**:

$$
h = \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2} = \frac{(7.11)(252.45)}{(80.60)^2} = \frac{1794.92}{6496.36} = 0.2763
$$

**Step 3 тАФ SIF and k**:

$$
i = \frac{0.9}{h^{2/3}} = \frac{0.9}{(0.2763)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.9}{0.4228} = 2.13
$$

$$
k = \frac{1.65}{h} = \frac{1.65}{0.2763} = 5.97
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Section modulus Z**:

$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - (D-2t)^4}{D}
$$

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^4 &= (168.3)^4 = 8.0106 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 \\
(D-2t)^4 &= (154.08)^4 = 5.6337 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 \\
Z &= \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{2.377 \times 10^8}{168.3} = 1.386 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Resultant bending stress with SIF**:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{i \cdot M}{Z} = \frac{2.13 \cdot (5.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬╖mm})}{1.386 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3} = 76.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 тАФ B31.3 319.4.1 check (qualitative)**:

$$
S_E = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4 \sigma_t^2 + 4 \sigma_b \sigma_t} = \sigma_b = 76.8 \text{ MPa (since } \sigma_t = 0\text{)}
$$

Compare with S<sub>A</sub> = 118 MPa:

$$
76.8 \text{ MPa} \;\leq\; 118 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**The flexibility check is satisfied.** The elbow has a comfortable ~35% margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **physical origin** of the Stress Intensification Factor (i) for a long-radius elbow. State the code formulas for i and k in terms of the flexibility characteristic h.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piping system operates between 40 ┬░C (install) and 320 ┬░C (operating). The straight run between two anchors is 25 m long (carbon steel, ╬▒ = 1.2 ├Ч 10<sup>тИТ5</sup> /K).
   1. Compute the **free thermal growth** ╬Фx.
   2. If a 90┬░ long-radius elbow (NPS 8 SCH 40, D = 219.1 mm, t = 8.18 mm, R = 328.65 mm) is placed at one end to absorb this growth, compute the **equivalent thermal moment** M absorbed by the elbow using the *simplified cantilever* formula M = 6EI┬╖╬Фx/L┬▓ (E = 2 ├Ч 10<sup>5</sup> MPa). Neglect axial flexibility.
   3. Verify the displacement stress range check per **B31.3 319.4.1** using S<sub>A</sub> = 124 MPa (assume 5,000 cycles).

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Solution**:

*(See Section 1.3 and Section 3.2 of these notes for the detailed physical explanation. The model diagram should show:)*

- A straight pipe under end moment M (uniform ╧Г<sub>b</sub> = Mc/I).
- A long-radius elbow under the same end moment тАФ bending stress is *concentrated* at the intrados (inner curve) and extrados (outer curve) due to the **ovalisation cross-section** under out-of-plane bending.
- The peak stress ╧Г<sub>peak</sub> = i ├Ч (Mc/I), where i > 1.

**Code formulas** (ASME B31.3 Table 304.7.1):

$$
i = \frac{0.9}{h^{2/3}} \quad \text{and} \quad k = \frac{1.65}{h} \quad \text{where } h = \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2}
$$

*(3 marks physical origin, 2 marks diagram description, 2 marks formulas)*

---

**(b) Solution**:

**Step 1 тАФ Free thermal growth**:

$$
\Delta x = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (1.2 \times 10^{-5})(25{,}000)(320 - 40) = (1.2 \times 10^{-5})(25{,}000)(280) = 84.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Geometric data**:

- D = 219.1 mm, t = 8.18 mm, R = 328.65 mm
- r = (219.1 тИТ 8.18)/2 = 105.46 mm
- h = (8.18 ├Ч 328.65)/(105.46)┬▓ = 2688.36/11121.81 = 0.2417
- I = ╧А/64 ├Ч (DтБ┤ тИТ (DтИТ2t)тБ┤) = ╧А/64 ├Ч ((219.1)тБ┤ тИТ (202.74)тБ┤)

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^4 &= 2.3045 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
(D-2t)^4 &= 1.6896 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
I &= \frac{\pi}{64} \cdot (6.149 \times 10^8) = 3.018 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Equivalent thermal moment**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{6 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \Delta x}{L^2} \\
&= \frac{6 \cdot (2 \times 10^5) \cdot (3.018 \times 10^7) \cdot 84.0}{(25{,}000)^2} \\
&= \frac{3.042 \times 10^{15}}{6.25 \times 10^8} \\
&= 4.867 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬╖mm} = 4.87 \text{ kN┬╖m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Bending stress with SIF**:

- i = 0.9/h^(2/3) = 0.9/(0.2417)^(2/3) = 0.9/0.3877 = 2.32
- Z = I/(D/2) = (3.018 ├Ч 10<sup>7</sup>)/109.55 = 2.755 ├Ч 10<sup>5</sup> mm<sup>3</sup>

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{i \cdot M}{Z} = \frac{2.32 \cdot (4.867 \times 10^6)}{2.755 \times 10^5} = 41.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ B31.3 319.4.1 check**:

$$
S_E = \sigma_b = 41.0 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{since } \sigma_t = 0)
$$

Compare with S<sub>A</sub> = 124 MPa (5,000 cycles, тЙд 7,000):

$$
41.0 \text{ MPa} \leq 124 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASSES}
$$

**Result**: The system passes with significant margin (~67%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls**:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the SIF (i)** when computing bending stress in elbows тАФ silently assumes i = 1. *Loss: 2тАУ3 marks per question.*
> 2. **Using wrong Y-coefficient** тАФ defaulting to Y = 0.4 for all materials (incorrect for austenitic stainless where Y = 0.4 but for different reason).
> 3. **Mixing up B31.1 and B31.3 stress formulations** тАФ B31.1 uses Eq. (11) sum check; B31.3 uses Eq. (30) resultant range check. They are *not interchangeable*.
> 4. **Ignoring mill tolerance** тАФ students often add c but forget the 12.5% negative tolerance per ASME B36.10.
> 5. **Wrong definition of r in h** тАФ using outside radius R<sub>O</sub> instead of *mean radius* r = (D тИТ t)/2.
> 6. **Sustained vs displacement confusion** тАФ SIF applies *only* to displacement (thermal) stress, NOT to pressure or weight stress.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST тАФ Module 4**
>
> тШР **Barlow Eq. (3a)**: $t_m = PD/(2(SEW + PY))$ тАФ units consistent (P in MPa, D in mm gives t in mm).
>
> тШР **Corrosion + Mill Tolerance**: $t_{\text{design}} = (t_m + c)/0.875$.
>
> тШР **Allowable stress S** is read from **Table A-1** at *design temperature*, not ambient.
>
> тШР **Y = 0.4** for ferritic steel < 482 ┬░C; increases with temperature.
>
> тШР **E = 1.0** for seamless; **0.85** for electric-resistance-welded with no radiography.
>
> тШР **SIF i** applies to displacement (thermal) stress *only*, not to pressure.
>
> тШР **h = tR/r┬▓**, **i = 0.9/h^(2/3)**, **k = 1.65/h** тАФ for elbows (B31.3 Table 304.7.1).
>
> тШР **B31.1 Eq. (11)**: $S_P = S_L + S_b \leq S_L$ (sustained + displacement).
>
> тШР **B31.3 Eq. (30)**: $S_E = \sqrt{S_b^2 + 4S_t^2 + 4S_bS_t} \leq S_A$.
>
> тШР **S<sub>A</sub>**: 124 MPa (тЙд 7k), 118 MPa (тЙд 14k), 112 MPa (тЙд 22k), 105 MPa (тЙд 45k), 93 MPa (тЙд 100k).
>
> тШР **Miner's Rule** for combining mixed cycles: $N_{\text{eq}} = \sum N_i (S_i/S_{\max})^3$ for steel.
>
> тШР **Flexibility analysis** is mandatory when ╬ФT is large, geometry is complex, or equipment nozzle limits are tight.
>
> тШР **Simplified analysis** (322.2.1) may be used for inherently flexible systems (тЙе 4 elbows in-plane, no large concentrated loads).
>
> тШР **Always compare ╧Г_b with S<sub>A</sub>**, **not** with S тАФ this is a common KTU error.
>
> тШР **Equipment nozzle loads** (vendor-published) must also be checked тАФ independent of code stress limits.
